### Post by Rubi1200 on 2010-12-06
[CENTER][COLOR=Black][SIZE=6]WUBI MEGATHREAD[/SIZE][/COLOR]
[SIZE=2][COLOR=Black](or, how to fix those Wubi installation breakages without going crazy)[/COLOR][/SIZE][COLOR=Black]

[/COLOR][LEFT][COLOR=Black][B]January 14, 2011
[/B]Are you experiencing black screen issues or other graphics related problems?
See posts # 1 and 8 here for Wubi and non-Wubi installs:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1613132](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1613132)
[B]
January 10, 2012[/B]
Another recurring problem, missing the root.disk on a Wubi install, is dealt with here by forum member bcbc:
[http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html](http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html)

**May 3, 2011**
[/COLOR][COLOR=Black]EDIT: [COLOR=Red]Please do NOT post in this thread if you are not using Wubi.[/COLOR] 
If you have Ubuntu installed **inside** Windows you are using Wubi. Solutions mentioned here are Wubi specific. Fixes for a normal install could potentially break Wubi and we do not wish to confuse users, especially those new to the forums. If you are unsure whether you are using Wubi or not, open a new thread in the Installation & Upgrades forum. 
We would also like to request that you start your own thread if you are using Wubi but have an issue not related directly to the fixes posted here (for example, networking problems etc.). We will see it and try and help as best we can.

[/COLOR][COLOR=Black]**May 3, 2011**
[/COLOR][COLOR=Black]_New information_: With the release of 11.04, it appears that the major issues affecting Wubi installs are over. However, please refer to this thread if you run into problems and post with relevant information such as the boot info script (mentioned below). 
[/COLOR][/LEFT]
 [/CENTER]

I would like to preface this thread with the following comments:

First and foremost, my utmost thanks and gratitude to forum moderator drs305 and long-standing member bcbc for their support, suggestions, feedback, and tireless efforts on behalf of Wubi, as well as regular Ubuntu, users on these forums. 

This guide is based entirely on their troubleshooting and problem solving endeavors.

I am by no means a Wubi expert. Everything I know comes from watching drs305 and bcbc hard at work.

Second, please read ALL the information before posting. If you have tried one of the solutions and it did not work or you are unable to proceed because you did not understand something, feel free to post. 

In the interest of keeping this thread "clean," I ask that you follow this simple procedure before cluttering up the thread. It will also make life easier for those responding who want to help solve problems.

Third, kudos to the developers of Wubi.

Finally, I ask forum members who are not Wubi users to please read this information carefully before offering advice in a post. 

Unfortunately, there have been instances where even experienced members have advised a thread starter to reinstall Wubi, GRUB, or suggested numerous other "fixes" which are either unnecessary or even counter-productive.

[SIZE=3]**Introduction:**[/SIZE]

Wubi, which stands for Windows-based Ubuntu Installer, is a convenient and easy way to try another operating system without the hassle of partitioning or other issues that sometimes occur when attempting to run more than one operating system.
[Wikipedia]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wubi_%28Ubuntu_installer%29%20wikipedia")

Wubi uses a virtual disk mounted within the host system (Windows), allowing users to access the full functionality of Ubuntu after the computer starts and the operating system has been chosen from the available menu.
[Wubi Guide]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide")

However, Wubi was never intended as anything other than a means to try a Linux operating system and, as such, it is usually recommended to install Ubuntu to a dedicated partition on the hard-drive.
[Agostino Russo (Wubi developer)]("http://howsoftwareisbuilt.com/2009/03/12/interview-with-agostino-russo-wubi-ubuntu/")

[SIZE=3]**Things You May Need for Troubleshooting:**[/SIZE]

1. an Ubuntu LiveCD or LiveUSB 
(the download link and instructions on how to create a CD or USB can be found [here]("http://www.ubuntu.com/desktop/get-ubuntu/download"))

2. a Windows installation/recovery disk

3. results of the [boot info script]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/")

[SIZE=3]**A Word of Caution:**[/SIZE]

Do _NOT_ try and reinstall GRUB or use boot managers such as BURG. Wubi installs cannot be booted directly from the MBR of the drive.

[SIZE=3]**The Core Issue:**[/SIZE]

There have been updates for the GRUB bootloader which have been causing problems with Wubi installs. These updates have been known to cause problems on Ubuntu versions from 9.10 onwards including upgrades to the latest 10.10 version.

I am not going to go into extended detail about the specifics of the problem since I assume most users just want the fix.

However, for those who wish to know more, the following bug reports make for interesting reading:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898)
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/613288](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/613288)
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/653134](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/653134)

(I do not know if this also affects older Wubi installs, but I am assuming that most users will have one of the more recent versions)

The issues can be broken down into two main areas, each with its own fix or solutions.

[SIZE=3]**Problem #1:**[/SIZE]

You have Wubi installed and are unable to boot either Ubuntu or Windows.

[SIZE=3]**Possible Cause:**[/SIZE]

A late July 2010 GRUB2 update is causing a "no such disk" error (in some cases it may have a "no such device" error followed by a long string of numbers) for some users of Wubi, resulting in an unbootable system. 

If the system does not display the original Windows menu, the most likely cause of the failure is that GRUB2 was installed in the MBR and/or on the Windows partition.

[SIZE=3]**Solution #1:**[/SIZE]

Restore the Windows bootloader with the relevant Windows Recovery CD.
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1014708](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1014708)

[SIZE=3]**Solution #2:**[/SIZE]

If you do not have a Windows CD, or are unable to get hold of one, you can use an Ubuntu CD/USB to install a Windows-like bootloader.

From a terminal on the LiveCD/USB (Applications > Accessories > Terminal), run the following commands:

```
sudo apt-get install lilo
sudo lilo -M /dev/sd**[COLOR=Red]a[/COLOR]** mbr
```This assumes that sda is the Windows drive. If not, change this to reflect the actual drive e.g. sdb, sdc, etc. 

Ignore any warnings and continue with the installation to the MBR. The main point is to get lilo installed and allow the user to boot Windows, and hopefully Ubuntu, again.

[SIZE=3]**Problem #2:**[/SIZE]

You have Wubi installed and are able to boot Windows normally. 

However, attempting to boot Ubuntu leads to a number of possible failures including, but not limited to, the following:

computer reboots without user interaction, a black screen with or without any error messages, loadfont errors, file not found errors, and the like.

These issues affect the following versions: 10.04, 10.04.1, and 10.10

The solutions for each version are somewhat different and will be noted next to the relevant solution.

[SIZE=3]**Possible Cause:**[/SIZE]

Recent upgrades to GRUB and the lupin package (used for Wubi) have caused the wubildr file to become out of sync with the latest version of GRUB. This appears to be a problem whether Wubi is installed on the Windows partition or a separate partition.

As a result, wubildr is unable to handle the commands in the grub.cfg menu and the computer reboots.

[SIZE=3]**Solution #1 (10.04):**[/SIZE]

If it is a 10.04 install, the fix is to boot an Ubuntu LiveCD/USB, loop mount the wubi root.disk and manually edit the grub.cfg file.

**IMPORTANT:**

You need to correctly identify your Windows partition. The examples here are meant to be just that, examples.

If you do not know or are unsure which is the correct partition, run the [boot info script]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/") and either post it here or use the information for your own purposes.

As an example, a Windows partition with a Wubi install would look like this in the RESULTS.txt:

```
[COLOR=Red]sda3[/COLOR]: _________________________________________________________________________

    File system:       ntfs
    Boot sector type:  Windows Vista/7
    Boot sector info:  No errors found in the Boot Parameter Block.
    Operating System:  Windows 7
    Boot files/dirs:   /Windows/System32/winload.exe /wubildr.mbr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr.mbr /wubildr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr /ubuntu/disks/root.disk 
                       /ubuntu/disks/swap.disk
```Note that sda3 is the partition which contains your Windows installation as well as the files needed by Wubi.

Boot a LiveCD/USB, identify your Windows partition that contains the root.disk (make sure you mount the correct partition; this example assumes /dev/sda**[COLOR=Red]1[/COLOR]**), and edit grub.cfg as follows:

```
sudo mkdir /media/win 
sudo mount /dev/sda**[COLOR=Red]1[/COLOR]** /media/win
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
sudo cp /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg.copy
sudo chmod +w /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg
gksu gedit /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg
```Remove all lines in the file from the start of the file up to,_ but not including_, the following menuentry ```
 ### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
```Save, reboot.

[SIZE=3]**Solution #2 (10.04 upgraded to 10.10):**[/SIZE]

If it is a 10.04 to 10.10 upgrade, boot into Windows make a backup of the wubildr file and then copy the c:\ubuntu\winboot\wubildr file over the c:\wubildr file.

Alternatively, you can do this from the LiveCD/USB using the instructions given to mount the Windows partition. 

Run the following commands:
```
sudo mkdir /media/win 
sudo mount /dev/sda**[COLOR=Red]1[/COLOR]** /media/win
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
sudo cp -p /media/win/wubildr /media/win/wubildr.save
sudo cp -p /media/win/ubuntu/winboot/wubildr /media/win
```This example again assumes sda1, but change to reflect your actual Windows partition e.g. sda2, sda3 etc.
Reboot.

[SIZE=3]**Solution #3 (10.04.1 upgraded to 10.10):**[/SIZE]

This may leave the user with a GRUB prompt and/or the other symptoms previously mentioned.

There are 2 possible approaches here.

Either boot the LiveCD/USB and edit the grub.cfg file as before (Solution #1).

Alternatively, use the instructions provided by drs305 to boot into Ubuntu from the GRUB prompt:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10264157&postcount=33](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10264157&postcount=33)

The advantage of being able to boot from the GRUB prompt is that the user does not need to use a LiveCD/USB.

If booting from the GRUB prompt works and you are able to boot into Ubuntu, follow the instructions in Solution #1 to edit the grub.cfg file.

In this case, enter the following command in the terminal:
```
gksu gedit /boot/grub/grub.cfg
```Again, as above, remove the entries from the start of the file up to, _but not including_, the following menuentry```
 ### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
```[SIZE=3]**Permanent Fix (as of the time of writing):**[/SIZE]

Thanks to bcbc, there is a more or less permanent fix for these problems.

These steps are applied **after** you have restored the Wubi install and are able to boot normally into both Windows and Ubuntu.

From the terminal:

```
sudo mv /boot/grub /boot/grubold
sudo mkdir /boot/grub
sudo cp /boot/grubold/grubenv /boot/grub
sudo update-grub
```[SIZE=2][B]
FIXED![/B][/SIZE]

Not done yet - prevent GRUB updates from breaking the Wubi install again:

Go to System > Administration > Synaptic Package Manager and select the grub-pc and grub-common packages. Click on Package > Lock Version.

[COLOR=Red]I cannot stress this enough; if you want to prevent this happening either on a freshly installed system or a fixed system, please lock the grub packages.[/COLOR]

This fix has been tested and confirmed on a 10.04.1 install, an install upgraded from 10.04.1 to 10.10, as well as a new 10.10 install (with grub-pc reinstalled to intentionally break it).

[SIZE=3]**Additional Information:**[/SIZE]

If you find that one of the solutions does not work for your version, try another. For example, if copying the wubildr file does not get the job done, use the fix to edit the grub.cfg file.

If all of the above seems overwhelming, you can recover your data from the Wubi install from _within_ Windows. In this case, you can use [ext2read]("http://ext2read.blogspot.com/") to recover any important data from the Ubuntu root.disk. 

If using the Ubuntu LiveCD/USB, you can also access and copy your user  files. The Ubuntu folders are located in /mnt if you mounted Wubi as  instructed earlier in the guide. Your HOME folder contents are found at  /mnt/home/<your_user_name>/

Do not run the sudo update-grub command BEFORE rebooting after following the solutions above.

In most cases, locking the GRUB version in Synaptic and/or not upgrading will help prevent problems arising in the first place.

If you are uncertain as to the designation for your Windows partition, either post the results of the boot info script or use the information yourself.

Here is another example from the RESULTS.txt:

```
sda2: _________________________________________________________________________

     File system:       ntfs
    Boot sector type:  Windows Vista/7
    Boot sector info:  No errors found in the Boot Parameter Block.
    Operating System:  Windows 7
    Boot files/dirs:   /bootmgr /Boot/BCD /Windows/System32/winload.exe 
                       /wubildr.mbr /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr.mbr /wubildr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr /ubuntu/disks/root.disk 
                       /ubuntu/disks/swap.disk
```In this case, you would mount sda2 in the commands given above. 

Please be careful and make sure you mount the correct partition.

If in doubt, ask here first.

Finally, if you would like to migrate your Wubi install to a dedicated partition, bcbc has created a fantastic guide on how to do this:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354)

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2010-12-08
Hi heroface101 and welcome to the forums :)

Are you sure you mounted the correct Windows partition?

The example, which I have now clarified, assumes sda1 is the Windows partition with the Wubi install. If your Windows partition is something else, you need to change it to the correct one e.g. sda2 etc.

From the LiveUSB, go to the terminal and post the output of ```
sudo fdisk -lu
```

```

### Post by drs305 on 2010-12-14
> **domoappo said:**
> I can still run Vista, but is there any way I can get my pictures?

If you can boot to a LiveCD you should be able to mount "root.disk" and thus gain access to all your Wubi files.  Look at the first post and use the commands to make mount points, mount windows and then root,disk.

Once mounted per the instructions, the files will be found under /mnt.

### Post by drs305 on 2010-12-21
Here are the instructions on how to boot Wubi from the **grub** prompt. Note these instructions are not for booting from the *grub rescue* prompt - it may work but the presence of the rescue prompt may indicate more than just a grub.cfg problem. 

If the grub.cfg file is missing or corrupted, the following can be used to attempt to boot into Wubi. If successful, the user will then need to determine the problem and fix it. If the problem is with *grub.cfg*, running "sudo update-grub" should recreate it.

Areas in bold must be changed to your system's Windows partition (**[COLOR="DarkRed"]X,Y[/COLOR]** and **[COLOR="DarkRed"][COLOR="DarkRed"]sda1[/COLOR][/COLOR]**). To boot Wubi from the *grub* prompt:
```
insmod ntfs
set root=(hd**[COLOR="DarkRed"]X,Y[/COLOR]**)  # Your Windows partition. Example: set root=(hd0,1)
loopback (loop0) /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sd**[COLOR="DarkRed"]a1[/COLOR]** loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro
initrd /initrd.img
boot
```

### Post by Hippytaff on 2010-12-23
Have you tried using the windows disc to repair windows. Then if succsesful you can boot a ubuntu livecd and install it next to win

### Post by drs305 on 2010-12-23
> **jennkhutch said:**
> 
I'd like to install Ubuntu side by side (?) with dedicated partition rather than within windows but for some reason, the issue above is keeping that from happening.

Any advice?  I am extremely new to Linux/Ubuntu and need lots of hand holding. Thanks!

Did you remove Ubuntu through the Add/Remove feature (or equivalent) in the Control Panel? 

The menu item (at least in XP) is just a line in C:\boot.ini. You might just make a backup copy and then edit the file and remove:
```
C:\wubildr.mbr="Ubuntu"
```

If you need to clean things up, you can just delete the C:\ubuntu folder and any wubi* files in C:\ if you don't have any data you need to try to save from your Wubi installation. This isn't the optimum way to remove Wubi but it will work.

Just having that option in your Windows menu shouldn't prevent you from installing Ubuntu to a partition however.

---

### Post by theasprint on 2010-12-23
Hi,

I was just wanting to ask about the grub update bug thing.
In fact I have already made a thread but no one seems to be answering yet:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1651474]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1651474")

My question is that for this grub update bug that has been affecting Wubi Ubuntu installations, will it affect those who have **Windows 7 and Ubuntu installed side-by-side**, but Ubuntu is installed **through the disc** and the default boot-loader is** Grub2**?

To simplify my question, do those that have Windows 7 and Ubuntu and yet Ubuntu is not installed Wubi-style (means **original disc style**) face this problem?

I'd like to know that if Grub2 is the default boot-loader, then this problem would not affect you?

I understand that this is a Wubi thread, but I would like to know if you've seen any problem like this? You all surely have more experience than me.

*In fact, 55 people looked at that thread and still no answer.

Thanks in advance.

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2010-12-23
Hi theasprint,
forum member Hippytaff answered in the thread you linked to.

I would ask you to please not clog this thread with these types of questions.

To quickly answer:
It is not a bug. 

The GRUB updates that break Wubi installs have nothing to do with a regular Ubuntu install.

If you have a regular Ubuntu install and you see updates for GRUB you can happily apply them. This is not to imply there will never be an issue, but not the same type for sure.

The updates only affect Wubi and in the first post of this thread you will find launchpad bug reports that offer a more technical view of the issue.

Thanks for understanding.

---

### Post by dom96 on 2010-12-27
hello, someone from #ubuntu on freenode told me to ask for help in this thread

I have recently installed a new hard drive, and now I can't boot ubuntu.
It doesn't seem to be able to find the root.disk. I'm getting this error "ALERT! /host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk does not exist. Dropping to a shell"

[Here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=10284490") is a thread that i've started.

Here is the output of [Boot Info Script]("http://pastebin.com/LfachmnT")

---

### Post by bcbc on 2010-12-27
> **dom96 said:**
> hello, someone from #ubuntu on freenode told me to ask for help in this thread

I have recently installed a new hard drive, and now I can't boot ubuntu.
It doesn't seem to be able to find the root.disk. I'm getting this error "ALERT! /host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk does not exist. Dropping to a shell"

[Here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=10284490") is a thread that i've started.

Here is the output of [Boot Info Script]("http://pastebin.com/LfachmnT")
Your wubi install is now on /dev/sdc5 (before it was on /dev/sda5).
So when you see the grub menu, hit 'e' on the first entry. Then change (hd0,5) to (hd2,5) and /dev/sda5 to /dev/sdc5, then hit CTRL+X to boot.
Once booted, go to a terminal (CTRL+ALT+t) and run "sudo update-grub" to update your grub menu.

---

### Post by dom96 on 2010-12-27
Thank you! That worked perfectly!
You are awesome.

Will I have to do that every time, or did the "sudo update-grub" save the changes?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2010-12-27
> **dom96 said:**
> Thank you! That worked perfectly!
You are awesome.

Will I have to do that every time, or did the "sudo update-grub" save the changes?

No, you don't have to do it again. "sudo update-grub" regenerates grub.cfg. Not unless you reorder or change your drives again.

### Post by humanx on 2010-12-31
New user here.  I'm not good with typing all that stuff (which I have no idea what it does) in a command terminal in order to solve the problem I didn't cause.  What I did was use my host (vista) and replaced (using explorer) that big root.disk file with a copy of the last one I backed up. That did the trick and then I turned off updates to grub.  For me that keeps ubuntu simple like it's supposed to be.  

Now I'll duck.

### Post by drs305 on 2010-12-31
> **humanx said:**
> New user here.  I'm not good with typing all that stuff (which I have no idea what it does) in a command terminal in order to solve the problem I didn't cause.  What I did was use my host (vista) and replaced (using explorer) that big root.disk file with a copy of the last one I backed up. That did the trick and then I turned off updates to grub.  For me that keeps ubuntu simple like it's supposed to be.  

Now I'll duck.


humanx,

Welcome to the Ubuntu forums. 

No reason to duck. You will find this to be one of the most accepting computer forums in the world (well, if you don't include the Community Cafe ;-) ). 

There are lots of ways to do things in Linux and there is seldom one 'correct' way. 

The method you propose is simple and reliable, *as long as you remember to make backups*. It's a good tip for all Wubi users to keep in mind. I've sometimes wondered whether you have to back up the other files in the Ubuntu folder for changing conditions, but I suppose as long as you change nothing else on your system only the root.disk file has to be backed up.

### Post by humanx on 2011-01-01
The Binnie article also mentions using windows to do the backup/restore.  That is what I did.  Moreover, there are only a few files to back up in a Wubi install, the largest being the root disk itself.  I back the Wubi install with every backup of the laptop.

### Post by Nuker90 on 2011-01-04
Hello guys,

I have a problem with Wubi and I hope this is the right place to post it.

I installed Ubuntu via Wubi, and I made a few updates/changes to the sound drivers... resulting in making my Ubuntu freeze after 5 minutes. Therefore, I want to try a clean reinstall of it. But I don't know how to uninstall it (it doesn't appear at ADD/REMOVE programs, as shown on the main website of Ubuntu). Also, I have Ubuntu 11.04. 

So, can you please help me uninstall Ubuntu in a safe way, and then reinstall it again using Wubi.exe?

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-04
> **Nuker90 said:**
> Hello guys,

I have a problem with Wubi and I hope this is the right place to post it.

I installed Ubuntu via Wubi, and I made a few updates/changes to the sound drivers... resulting in making my Ubuntu freeze after 5 minutes. Therefore, I want to try a clean reinstall of it. But I don't know how to uninstall it (it doesn't appear at ADD/REMOVE programs, as shown on the main website of Ubuntu). Also, I have Ubuntu 11.04. 

So, can you please help me uninstall Ubuntu in a safe way, and then reinstall it again using Wubi.exe?

Thank you,
Nuker90.
You can't install wubi 11.04 without some major tweaking. There is a bug that shows 11.04 when it fact it is 10.10.
To uninstall either double click "Ubuntu" in Add/remove programs, or run c:\ubuntu\uninstall-wubi.exe. Or follow the manual instructions in the [WubiGuide]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How do I manually uninstall Wubi?")

#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by /usr/sbin/grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ ${prev_saved_entry} ]; then
  set saved_entry=${prev_saved_entry}
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z ${boot_once} ]; then
    saved_entry=${chosen}
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n ${have_grubenv} ]; then if [ -z ${boot_once} ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}
insmod ntfs
set root='(hd2,3)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set c00c37ce0c37bdea
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=640x480
  insmod gfxterm
  insmod vbe
  if terminal_output gfxterm ; then true ; else
    # For backward compatibility with versions of terminal.mod that don't
    # understand terminal_output
    terminal gfxterm
  fi
fi
insmod ntfs
set root='(hd2,3)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set c00c37ce0c37bdea
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
set lang=en
insmod gettext
if [ ${recordfail} = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.32-27-generic" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd2,3)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set c00c37ce0c37bdea
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-27-generic root=/dev/mapper/isw_dafgbbfahh_dell3 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash
    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-27-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.32-27-generic (recovery mode)" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd2,3)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set c00c37ce0c37bdea
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-27-generic root=/dev/mapper/isw_dafgbbfahh_dell3 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro single 
    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-27-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.32-26-generic" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd2,3)'

    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)

    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-26-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.32-26-generic (recovery mode)" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd2,3)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set c00c37ce0c37bdea
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-26-generic root=/dev/mapper/isw_dafgbbfahh_dell3 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro single

}

    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-24-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.32-24-generic (recovery mode)" {

### END /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Windows 7 (loader) (on /dev/mapper/isw_dafgbbfahh_dell2)" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd2,2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 3ab03409b033ca61
    chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###

===================== isw_dafgbbfahh_dell3/Wubi/etc/fstab: =====================

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
# Use 'blkid -o value -s UUID' to print the universally unique identifier
# for a device; this may be used with UUID= as a more robust way to name
# devices that works even if disks are added and removed. See fstab(5).
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>
proc            /proc           proc    nodev,noexec,nosuid 0       0
/host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /               ext4    loop,errors=remount-ro 0       1
/host/ubuntu/disks/swap.disk none            swap    loop,sw         0       0

========= isw_dafgbbfahh_dell3/Wubi: Location of files loaded by Grub: =========


  15.5GB: boot/grub/grub.cfg
    .1GB: boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-24-generic
   1.3GB: boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-26-generic
   3.5GB: boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-27-generic
  15.3GB: boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-24-generic
  15.5GB: boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-26-generic
  15.5GB: boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-27-generic
   3.5GB: initrd.img
   1.3GB: initrd.img.old
  15.5GB: vmlinuz
  15.5GB: vmlinuz.old
=========================== Unknown MBRs/Boot Sectors/etc =======================

Unknown BootLoader  on sda1


Unknown BootLoader  on sda2


Unknown BootLoader  on sda3



=============================== StdErr Messages: ===============================

hexdump: /dev/sda1: No such file or directory
hexdump: /dev/sda1: No such file or directory
hexdump: /dev/sda2: No such file or directory
hexdump: /dev/sda2: No such file or directory
hexdump: /dev/sda3: No such file or directory
hexdump: /dev/sda3: No such file or directory
umount: /media/OS: device is busy.
        (In some cases useful info about processes that use
         the device is found by lsof(8) or fuser(1))
```The commands to mount the partition are usually something like sda1, sda2 etc.

Here, however, there is no such partition (in the same sense).

The solution, as we discovered, is to substitute by using the /dev/mapper path.

Thus, instead of something like this:
```
 sudo mount /dev/sda1 /media/win
```We would use this:
```
 sudo mount /dev/mapper/isw_dafgbbfahh_dell3  /media/win
```Now all the commands will work, the root.disk can be mounted and edited to restore settings etc.

Caveat: this is just one situation and it has not been tested on other RAID0 setups or other types of RAID.

Nonetheless, an interesting experience and thanks for sharing with us WubiKeener.

I would also definitely recommend Wubi users with this type of setup to post the results of the boot info script if at all unsure about what to do in this situation.

---

### Post by dom96 on 2011-01-07
Hello, I decided to move my ubuntu folder to a different Hard Drive, because I believe that the one it's currently on is failing.

I have tried to change the entry in grub to point to the other drive(Using 'e', then CTRL + X and running `sudo update-grub`), but it seems to reset each boot, anyone know how I could move my Ubuntu and do it permanently?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-07
> **dom96 said:**
> Hello, I decided to move my ubuntu folder to a different Hard Drive, because I believe that the one it's currently on is failing.

I have tried to change the entry in grub to point to the other drive(Using 'e', then CTRL + X and running `sudo update-grub`), but it seems to reset each boot, anyone know how I could move my Ubuntu and do it permanently?

If you have a separate hard drive why don't you just create a dedicated partition and migrate the wubi install to it? That seems to me like an easier and more robust solution.

If you want to move a wubi install, it's better to backup the virtual disks outside of the ubuntu folder, uninstall, reinstall where you want it, copy over the virtual disks, then do the 'e' CTRL+X update-grub trick.

If you want help with your current issue you should post the results of the bootinfoscript so we can see what you've done.

---

### Post by dom96 on 2011-01-07
I'm not really sure how I could migrate my partition, mainly i'm scared of losing my ubuntu installation; I want to keep everything the same if that's possible.
Windows is installed on the failing hard drive as well, so I'm not sure whether I should reinstall everything.

Anyway, here is my bootinfo [http://pastebin.com/RkDwRJxk]("http://pastebin.com/RkDwRJxk")

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-07
> **dom96 said:**
> I'm not really sure how I could migrate my partition, mainly i'm scared of losing my ubuntu installation; I want to keep everything the same if that's possible.
Windows is installed on the failing hard drive as well, so I'm not sure whether I should reinstall everything.

Anyway, here is my bootinfo [http://pastebin.com/RkDwRJxk]("http://pastebin.com/RkDwRJxk")
The grub menu is loading from the old Wubi install, you override it to point to the copied one and boot it. Then you update the copied one's grub menu. The next time you boot it loads old one's grub menu again and you repeat the process. I think you'd have to go in and modify the bcd entry to point it at the correct partition (but it's just easier to use the method I mentioned previously).

If you think your drive is failing why don't you clone it?

To migrate use this: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354) (it doesn't change the original wubi install, so you can try it without risk; also it will only allow you to install the grub bootloader to the target drive - so you could boot that drive independently from Windows on your failing drive by modifying the boot order).

---

### Post by dom96 on 2011-01-08
That migration sounds like a good idea, just to be clear it won't overwrite Windows' boot loader on the drive that it is on(as long as I migrate to a different hard drive)? I guess that's what you meant when you said that I could then just change the hard drive boot order.

Also this won't be a Wubi install anymore when I migrate if I understand correctly?

### Post by mörgæs on 2011-01-08

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-01-08
Thank you mörgæs; much appreciated :)

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-08
> **dom96 said:**
> That migration sounds like a good idea, just to be clear it won't overwrite Windows' boot loader on the drive that it is on(as long as I migrate to a different hard drive)? I guess that's what you meant when you said that I could then just change the hard drive boot order.

Also this won't be a Wubi install anymore when I migrate if I understand correctly?
yes - it will only install the grub bootloader on the target drive (and you can skip this if you choose).
yes - modify the boot order to boot from the target drive
no - it won't be a wubi install.

To migrate you need to prepare the partitions before hand - an empty, target partition - and a swap partition if you want to be able to hibernate.

### Post by cosmolee on 2011-01-13
Ubuntu 10.04 WUBI install attempt.

I have a problem similar to #2, but the solution doesn't work for me because I'm never able to boot from a new install of Ubuntu under Windows.  So there isn't a usable Ubuntu image that I can mount.  The root.disk is uninitialized at this point.

I also have Windows installed on /dev/sda2 and Linux on /dev/sda1.  I decided to test out a WUBI installation in my Windows partition.  

The installation goes fine, no errors reported.  I reboot, get the grub menu, I choose Windows XP, then I get the Windows boot loader.  I choose Ubuntu, then I get the error "Try (hd0,0): Ext2:" and the system just hangs.  If choose Windows XP from the Windows boot loader instead, it boots fine.

This is on a virgin installation.  I've tried installing WUBI from CD media, and also the WUBI installer from within Windows, downloading the files from the Net.  I even tried Linux Mint 10 & 9 to see if there might be a different result.  Unfortunately, the same problem occurs.

One thing occurs to me, shouldn't the message say "Try (hd0, 1)"?  Wouldn't that correspond to the /dev/sda2 partition?  Is the WUBI loader perhaps getting confused by the Linux installation on /dev/sda1?  ](*,)

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-14
Anyone subscribed to this thread - who has been affected by Grub problems, please go to the relevant bug report and click on: [COLOR="DarkGreen"]This bug affects # people. Does this bug affect you?[/COLOR]. This will help to get the problem taken seriously by the developers. You will need to register at launchpad.net and sign in to do this.

Problem #1: Install on different partition than windows, grub update/upgrade leaves Computer unbootable: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898)

Problem #2: 
10.04 grub update leaves Ubuntu unbootable: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/682337](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/682337)

Upgrade to 10.10: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/653134](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/653134)

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-01-14

Hopefully, the more people who report this, the more likely the problem will be addressed and resolved.

### Post by BigMoose on 2011-01-14
I can't boot as other people and there is no drive with the root.disk so the correction steps are not working. Attached are my results.txt file.

Running Windows XP on a seperate harddrive from UBUNTU.

Thanks in advance.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-14
> **BigMoose said:**
> I can't boot as other people and there is no drive with the root.disk so the correction steps are not working. Attached are my results.txt file.

Running Windows XP on a seperate harddrive from UBUNTU.

Thanks in advance.
You have wubildr and wubildr.mbr on /dev/sdc1, your windows drive, and an Ubuntu entry in boot.ini. So it looks like you installed Wubi at some point, but the root.disk (the whole \ubuntu directory) is missing.

You also have a normal Ubuntu 10.10 install on /dev/sda. If you want to boot the existing Ubuntu install, just set your bios to boot from /dev/sda (the 40GB drive).

If you don't want to boot the existing install, but would prefer to recover your Wubi install please explain a bit more about what has happened, where and when you installed it, as this is not a typical wubi setup.

=========================== sda1/boot/grub/grub.cfg: ===========================

#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  set have_grubenv=true
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ "${prev_saved_entry}" ]; then
  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then
    saved_entry="${chosen}"
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n "${have_grubenv}" ]; then if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}

function load_video {
  insmod vbe
  insmod vga
}

insmod part_msdos
insmod ext2
set root='(hd0,msdos1)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set dd595d5f-aabd-4e87-b7ce-c635077712d9
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=640x480
  load_video
  insmod gfxterm
fi
terminal_output gfxterm
insmod part_msdos
insmod ext2
set root='(hd0,msdos1)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set dd595d5f-aabd-4e87-b7ce-c635077712d9
set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
set lang=en
insmod gettext
if [ "${recordfail}" = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.35-24-generic-pae' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
	recordfail
	insmod part_msdos
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,msdos1)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set dd595d5f-aabd-4e87-b7ce-c635077712d9
	linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.35-24-generic-pae root=UUID=dd595d5f-aabd-4e87-b7ce-c635077712d9 ro   quiet splash
	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.35-24-generic-pae
}
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.35-24-generic-pae (recovery mode)' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
	recordfail
	insmod part_msdos
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,msdos1)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set dd595d5f-aabd-4e87-b7ce-c635077712d9
	echo	'Loading Linux 2.6.35-24-generic-pae ...'
	linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.35-24-generic-pae root=UUID=dd595d5f-aabd-4e87-b7ce-c635077712d9 ro single 
	echo	'Loading initial ramdisk ...'
	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.35-24-generic-pae
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+)" {
	insmod part_msdos
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,msdos1)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set dd595d5f-aabd-4e87-b7ce-c635077712d9
	linux16	/boot/memtest86+.bin
}
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+, serial console 115200)" {
	insmod part_msdos
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,msdos1)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set dd595d5f-aabd-4e87-b7ce-c635077712d9
	linux16	/boot/memtest86+.bin console=ttyS0,115200n8
}
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Microsoft Windows XP Professional (on /dev/sdc1)" {
	insmod part_msdos
	insmod ntfs
	set root='(hd2,msdos1)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set b29c32fa9c32b8a5
	drivemap -s (hd0) ${root}
	chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###
if [ -f  $prefix/custom.cfg ]; then
  source $prefix/custom.cfg;
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###

# devices that works even if disks are added and removed. See fstab(5).

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-16
@jstaff,
Did you do a hard shutdown? What release are you using? What was the size of your install?

Unfortunately I've seen a few threads where the root.disk has been removed by chkdsk. Not really sure what causes this - it should move it to the hidden folder if it's not clean. I wonder if it could have something to do with the size of the disk and the available free space. 

At any rate - you will need to find this file somehow or your Ubuntu installation is gone. You could try booting a live CD and seeing if [testdisk can find it]("http://www.cgsecurity.org/wiki/TestDisk:_undelete_file_for_NTFS"). If it is deleted and you keep using your drive it will almost certainly overwrite portions of it.

### Post by drs305 on 2011-01-17
> **austin123457 said:**
> My problem is, when you say sda do you mean the name of the drive? or what?

sda would be the first drive as identified by your OS. You can run the following command to see how your devices are listed.
```
sudo fdisk -l  # Lowercase L
```
Your Linux drive will have partitions identified as "Linux". You will see "NTFS" on some of your Windows partitions. Use this information to see which drive is sda and which is sdb.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-18
@Zimatar,
sometimes when you install grub2 and you designate your windows partition as the target, it will place a /boot/grub directory with the core.img file. This can break Windows as often there is a /Boot directory as well - and Windows isn't case-sensitive. 
It's also possible to install Grub to the windows bootsector but this seems to happen less these days.

So, boot an Ubuntu live CD and make sure you don't have two /boot directories. You can also run the bootinfoscript and it should make it clear.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-18
Lately I've noticed that people affected by Problem #1 go on to be affected by Problem #2. It's therefore a good idea to apply the Permanent Fix after resolving Problem #1 as well.

Another point - I've heard cases where Wubi installs with Problem #2 spontaneously repair themselves. I'm trying to find out what the 'fix' might be - as it's obviously easier than the current Solution #1.

Right now I'd recommend running check disk (My computer, right click on Windows drive, Tools, Check disk for errors, automatically fix... reboot into Windows to complete chkdsk). Then try booting Ubuntu. I've heard that booting Windows, not logging in, and then restarting also fixes it - but I haven't had a chance to test this yet.

Ultimately we need to find the root cause of the problem - as yet unknown - so that we can assist the developers to prevent it in the first place - but in the meantime, an easy fix would be a good second best.

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-01-20
I'd like to add something to this too.

We (myself, bcbc, and drs305) have tried to ensure that the Wubi Megathread is as clear as possible for users.

The majority of users who have asked for help have been satisfied with the fixes we provided (I have received numerous PM messages offering thanks for the Megathread).

In some cases, users have even dropped Wubi and decided to go for a normal install (something bcbc and I have also helped with).

The thread you linked to is the exception to the rule not the norm in my opinion.

Moreover, if you look at the number of views this thread has received, it is obvious that we are making a difference.

But, and here is where it gets awkward, we are in a rather tenuous position. 

On the one hand, we all want users to try Ubuntu (Wubi too) and yet it is becoming increasingly difficult to justify using something which either breaks with the first update or even wipes out your whole installation.

On the other hand, we are faced with developers (and Canonical itself by extension) who seem either oblivious that there is a problem or have their minds set on a larger goal.

As they say, in war there are casualties.

It is unfortunate, but we may have to accept that this is something over which we have little or no say.

I have also been working behind the scenes trying to figure out how to deal with the regular 10.10 installer and its behavior.

Initial testing proved what you already confirmed regarding the use of the "install alongside" option. Namely, that is wipes out other installations.

However, and this is something I need to try and confirm, there does not appear to be a pattern as to when, how, or why the installer sometimes uses the option as we would have expected and when it destroys other operating systems.

I have seen plenty of threads where the user reported using that option with no unusual consequences.

Is there a pattern? If so, can we find a way to work around it?

As usual, any thoughts on this are appreciated.

---

 [ATTACH]181564[/ATTACH]

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-01-20
Hi and welcome to the forums :)

You are right that sda1 is the Windows/Wubi partition.

 However, the reason that you ran into the problem is that the root.disk file is missing.

If you look at the main post in this thread there are a couple of examples of how the files look and you can see what I mean.

I suspect that chkdsk removed the root.disk (we have seen this happen before).

What you need to do is enable hidden files and folders in Windows and then look at the root of the C drive for a folder named C:\FOUND.0000 or something similar. If you do find it just move the root.disk back to the Ubuntu folder on C.

If not, then I am afraid you have probably lost the Wubi install and will have to start again.

If that is the case, to prevent this happening again lock the grub-pc and grub-common packages in Ubuntu after a fresh install (see main post just after the Permanent Fix on how to do this).

Good luck!

---

### Post by drs305 on 2011-01-20
bouncy93,

Welcome to the Ubuntu forums.

In the RESULTS.txt you posted, this section should contain an entry for > /ubuntu/disks/root.disk

Yours does not:
> 
sda1: _________________________________________________________________________

    File system:       ntfs
    Boot sector type:  Windows XP
    Boot sector info:  No errors found in the Boot Parameter Block.
    Operating System:  Windows XP
    Boot files/dirs:   /boot.ini /ntldr /NTDETECT.COM /wubildr.mbr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr.mbr /wubildr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr


You should try to find the /ubuntu/disks folder and the root.disk file, which is your Ubuntu file. If you find it, move the /disks folder to /ubuntu/ . Until you can find the root.disks file you aren't going to be able to use your Wubi OS.

---

### Post by drs305 on 2011-01-20
> **bouncy93 said:**
> So there is no way to recover any data from Wubi?

Except for boot information, all your Wubi data files would be stored within the root.disk file. 

You could use any data recovery tools to try to find the lost/misplaced/deleted file. I'd concentrate on Windows file recovery apps since the Wubi file is just another Windows file as far as the OS is concerned.

---

### Post by kansasnoob on 2011-01-20
I hope I did good, this is what I sent:

Title:

> Killing ourselves softly with our own song?

Too rough?

Text:

> I'd like to begin with a quote from Ivanka Majic here:

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/628087/comments/31](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/628087/comments/31)

"The installation experience should be attractive and effortless to reassure new users that Ubuntu is the right choice. The process should feel safe and should only highlight risk when necessary (e.g. when data will be destroyed)."

Unfortunately the 10.10 installer has resulted in the loss of existing operating systems and data:

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/655950](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/655950)

Please check the duplicates!

Thankfully that's finally garnered some attention recently but how many potential Ubuntu converts did we lose because the installer hosed their existing OS and/or data?

Equally as bad, or possibly worse, are the ongoing problems with Wubi installs:

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/653134](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/653134)

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/682337](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/682337)

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898)

While I personally don't use Wubi this is a major problem. Quite often people perform a Wubi install with no external media available (neither a Windows recovery disc or an Ubuntu Live CD) and after their first update in Ubuntu they find they can't boot.

What impression do either of these situations leave on potential Ubuntu converts?

I think we need to increase our focus on bugs that destroy data or the ability to boot an existing OS.

These bugs are harming our reputation.

I'm sorry if any of those involved in creating this guide thought I was criticizing them. That was not the case.

I'm not one to just "give up" on something I believe in, and I believe that Ubuntu is still great, but they seem to be ignoring some very important bugs.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-21
@Javier_SI,

Run chkdsk - boot into Windows, go to My Computer, right click C: drive, Properties, Tools, Check disk for errors, automatically fix. Reboot to complete check. I also recommend defragmenting your C: drive if necessary.

If this doesn't fix it, try reinstalling as you have only a 'half-installed' Wubi. So you can't mount the root.disk: there is nothing on it yet.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-21
@bcow, what version of Ubuntu are you using as a live CD. You'll need something from 9.10 onwards so that it can mount the root.disk as it uses the ext4 file system. I've seen this problem before when someone tried to use an 8.04 CD to repair a current Wubi install.

PS try booting windows and running CHKDSK. I've been hearing reports that this can sometimes fix the problem.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-21
> **bcow said:**
> I've gotten Wubi to boot. I've only edited the grub.cfg file. I still get a "no wubildr" message when I choose Ubuntu but then it boots just fine. 
I had previously copied c:\ubuntu\winboot\wubildr over c:\wubildr because I had seen it in a different forum as a possible fix. 
I'm also getting bombarded with update requests in Ubuntu -- should I do the permanent fix before running any of these updates?

Thanks so much for your help!

Yes do the permanent fix. If you run the updates beforehand it will likely automatically refresh the grub.cfg file and cause the problem again.

That "no wubildr" message is actually normal. Wubi uses grub4dos to chain to Grub2 and this goes through each partition (in the order that the BIOS sees it) looking for the wubildr file (in the root of the partition). If it doesn't find it, it says "Try (hdX,Y): *FAT16* : NO WUBILDR" for each partition - obviously the partition type will be different depending on your setup. The only problem with this is if it encounters an ext4 partition before finding the wubildr - the version of grub4dos that wubi uses is old and can't read ext4 partitions - so it hangs.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-21
> **Ubuntered said:**
> Thanks for the quick reply. I've actually already tried that fix and it didn't work, it will probably work if I can figure out the correct dir. path, but I'm gonna need help for that. Keep in mind I'm new to linux. Here's the results I get. Thanks for the help

ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mkdir /media/win
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mount /dev/sda1 /media/win
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
Killed
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo cp /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg.copy
cp: cannot stat `/mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg': No such file or directory

Here's the Boot Info Summary

First backup the root.disk if possible. 

Then try:
```
sudo mkdir /media/win
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /media/win
sudo fsck /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk
```
Then (if that doesn't give you errors),
```
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
```
If that works - then continue with the rest of the instructions.

Actually I might run chkdsk on your Windows C: drive as well before running fsck.

Thanks for your help, you guys are awesome!Copying the root.disk back over will work. That's a convenient way to backup your Wubi install. 
But backing it up after you are having problems won't help if the problem is on the root.disk itself. It's still a good idea to take a backup before attempting repairs e.g. running fsck

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-25
> **Jakkers said:**
> Its a stock Lenovo Ideapad S10-3 Netbook. 

Video is Intel Graphics Media Accelerator 3150, Audio is Realtek HD, with an Intel Atom processor @ 1.66GHz, and a gig of RAM. 

I'd give the netbook remix a shot, but I'm not sure it would work any better, and I'd prefer a full desktop experience instead of the netbook-oriented interface it sports.

[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/MaverickMeerkat/ReleaseNotes](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/MaverickMeerkat/ReleaseNotes)
> Lenovo S10-3 systems don't boot. Temporary workaround: add "intel_idle.max_cstate=0" as a kernel paremeter at boot ([634702]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/634702")). A fix already exists that will be available only at release time ([647071]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/647071")).

To set kernel boot options on a wubi install see here (replace nomodeset with the above): [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10089820&postcount=8](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10089820&postcount=8)

You need to repeat this (after installing) on the first boot: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10069997&postcount=1](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10069997&postcount=1) (ignore the fact that is says "(not wubi)" - it is the same)

After booting, apply all updates through the update manager and it should fix the problem.

Note netbook-edition is the same as 32-bit ubuntu desktop, except it includes unity (the netbook display manager) - you can select either the standard Gnome Desktop or Unity at login time.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-26
> **rezaulkabir said:**
> Hi, By your suggestion for a permanent fix, I did so in my 10.04 LTS and got rid of the famous rescue prompt which harrassed me for more than a week.
But to my surprise, after update through update manager, I found the network connection is not working. However, booting in failsafex mode gives the connection. 
I want to know, if this happened due to the "permanent fix" I have done.
No. Nothing in the permanent fix modifies anything that could possibly impact your networking. Check your logs and try posting in the [Networking and wireless subforum]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=336") if you need help troubleshooting.

question 1: can i re-install wubi and recover the files i have in ubuntu?  almost all of my data is on externals, but i built up some files that i hadn't managed to transfer yet.

question 3: so, if 2 & 3 aren't an option.... how do i get that boot cd working if that's my only option for getting my files back?

i'll assume that there's a forum topic out there that covers how to go from wubi to setting up partitions for the permanent dual-boot option.  i really like ubuntu, but nights like this make me pretty grumpy.

i appreciate any help on this.  please keep in mind i'm not particularly familiar with all this linux gobbledygook.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-01-28
@kona4breakfast

1. If you reinstall Wubi it deletes your current install. If you copy the root.disk somewhere outside of the c:\ubuntu directory then you can reinstall, and then loop mount your copied root.disk to get data.
2. Yes. In post #1 there is a link to a product called ext2read.
3. burn the cd at the slowest possible speed. Also you can run an [md5sum check]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/HowToMD5SUM#MD5SUM on Windows") on the .iso you downloaded before burning.

---

Uninstalling Wubi will remove Ubuntu from the Windows boot - and regarding installing - it depends on which release, as Ubiquity (the installer) changed a bit for 10.10. I suggest a new thread with the output of "sudo fdisk -l" (once you get the Ubuntu live CD boot, or a picture from Windows disk manager) and then you'll get lots of help with that.

Hey guys, I'm stuck on Problem #1 Solution#2.

I've typed both of the commands listed on this thread and I'm unsure what to do next.
According to the Terminal. I assume I have to type the following command in order to install the mdr.

> 
lilo.real -m /dev/XXX [ mdr | ext ]               install master boot recordHow do I enter the command above? I tried copying & pasting the command above and replaced the "XXX" with "sdc." ("c" being my main drive.) But, no luck...

> No command 'ext' found, did you mean:I apologize if this is a silly question. I'm very new to lunix. :wink:

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-01-31
Hi and welcome to the forums :-)

If lilo is installed on the LiveCD/USB, check with this from the terminal:

```
locate lilo
```To install to the MBR, run this:

```
sudo lilo -M /dev/sdc mbr
```Just copy/paste into the terminal and ignore any warnings; let it finish and then reboot without the CD and you should be good to go.

WAIT: what do you mean by c is the main drive? Do you have more than one hard-drive?

The command needs to be this if you have one drive:
> sudo lilo -M /dev/sda mbr

Before you do anything else, post the output of this command:

> sudo fdisk -lu

Any help is appreciated.Use Problem#2's solution #1 and then, after booting, do the Permanent Fix. The symptoms can vary - as you've found. Just because it's booting one day doesn't mean it will after the next kernel update.

PS you can't hibernate using Wubi - it requires a swap partition (wubi uses a swap file)

Wubi is not problematic per se. The issue at the moment is the fact the updates for the GRUB program are creating havoc with Wubi.

However, once you have got things working with the fix, you can lock the grub packages as described in the thread.

You can also do this on a fresh install to avoid the issue in the first place.

If you need more help, just ask.

### Post by ponksical on 2011-02-03
Much praise and thanks to drs305, bcbc and Rubi1200 for the solutions and this guide which was such a relief to stumble upon after a lot of searching and not really knowing what the problem was.

Being new to Ubuntu, I just registered to the forums yesterday and today, all of a sudden, the computer rebooted instead of booting ubuntu. Panic-filled hours of searching ensued. Anyways, here I am, glad now to be back in ubuntu that starts up. Considering that this seems to be a common problem, this solution was surprisingly hard to find.

I'll mention a couple things I encountered as I did the steps of the solution (soln 1 to prob 2) that made me feel that the solution might not work for me. Neither of them proved to be a problem as I just continued to follow the instructions and things worked out. 

Firstly that the first "menu entry" for me was different than in the guide. I had instead:
```
### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
```

And secondly, in RESULTS.txt before the fix it showed 
```
sda2: _________________________________________________________________________

    File system:       ntfs
    Boot sector type:  Windows Vista/7
    Boot sector info:  No errors found in the Boot Parameter Block.
    Operating System:  Windows Vista
    Boot files/dirs:   /bootmgr /Boot/BCD /Windows/System32/winload.exe 
                       /wubildr.mbr /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr.mbr /wubildr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr /ubuntu/disks/root.disk 
                       /ubuntu/disks/swap.disk

sda2/Wubi: _________________________________________________________________________

    File system:       ext4
    Boot sector type:  -
    Boot sector info:  
    Mounting failed:
mount: wrong fs type, bad option, bad superblock on /dev/loop1,
       missing codepage or helper program, or other error
       In some cases useful info is found in syslog - try
       dmesg | tail  or so
```

where I just noticed all the horrible sounding things at the end. These messages all disappeared when I ran boot_info_script again afterwards.

Sorry if the post is too long or somehow poorly formatted. It is my first :). Thanks again.

Formatting is great; that is exactly how we need to see it.

Post is not too long either.

So, let me address some of the points you made.

First, I/we are really pleased you found the Megathread and that it helped you get things working again.

Second, the menuentry you mention should be the first entry. I see your point about the clarity of the main post and I will update it to reflect this fact.

Lastly, the error you saw in the script about bad superblocks etc. is usually not a good sign as it indicates file-system damage etc. However, you seem to have sorted this out; excellent.

As long as you also apply the Permanent Fix, including locking the grub packages, this should hopefully not occur again.

We are trying to find ways to get the developers to recognize the problem and provide a real solution. Until then, we will do what we can to help you and other Wubi users.

> hi all,

I had the problem where windows would load but not Ubuntu so in the first post of this thread i used the problem #2's solution 1, where i boot usning a USB into ubuntu , mount my wubi installed ubuntu and edit the grub.cfg

It worked as soon as i restarted it was fine, then i locked the version of grub-pc and grup-common , but i have to agree i didnt follow the instructions to copy the grubenv or move the old grub . i just locked the grub-pc and grub-common in packet manager.

After working some time my ubuntu hung i had to power off manually and boot again , it worked that time, but after shutting down for the night booting in morning, i was not able to go in and i get the same erro i got in first place 
root.disk not found
and gives some error about grub.cfg (which i cant fully read as it flashes) and then falls into grub prompt.

so when i tried to use the solution again boot in using USB ->mount-> edit grub.cfg 
the grub is the one i edited as per instuctions i.e the file deleted till the menuentry

 ### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###now i am stuck dont know wat do, can any one help me in this situation
thank you

P.S 
I have win 7 my Hard disk is partioned as 2 (C: and D: ) and i have installed wubi in D:  and C: has the windows
any more details i will be happy to provide....thanx

You should avoid hard shutdowns if possible... hold Alt+SysRq and press R-E-I-S-U-B. Because Wubi is running from a virtual disk that is a loop-mounted file there is a danger that you could corrupt the file under ntfs (in addition to the loop mounted ext4 file system). 

So you should run chkdsk on windows - My computer, right click on D:, Properties, Tools tab, Check now under "Error Checking", check the "Automatically fix file system errors" box.
You should also fsck the root.disk:
1. boot a live cd
2. mount the windows (d: ) partition on /media/win
3. ```
sudo fsck /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk
```
(make sure you don't mount the root.disk before running fsck).
4. Try booting into Ubuntu again - and apply the permanent fix.
5. Also figure out why your computer is locking up - you may have some other issues involved here.

Let us know how that goes.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-03
> **nareshmeee said:**
> hello bcbc thanx for the tip, though i didnt have chek disk, my ubuntu as booting in after editing the grub.cfg
i have applied the permanent fix as mentioned 
regarding the hanging of my ubuntu, i feel its more due to my internet usage (firefox) when ever i go to media rich sites it hangs
and also some times during low battery it goes into hibernate and does not recover...
I will try using ur tip for Alt+Sys Res and then R-E-I-S-U-B
Go to the power options (System, Preferences, Power management) and turn off the hibernate option on low battery - it doesn't work on Wubi. Set it to suspend instead (or shutdown if you have the option).
If you don't have a lot of RAM you could try [increasing]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How do I increase my swap space?") the size of the swap file. Maybe this will help the hanging.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-03
> **nareshmeee said:**
> thanx though the power option was in suspend , my wubi , ubuntu wont come out with key press or mouse or the power button... now i have set it to just shut down... 

i have 2 GB RAM , with 7 GB file system (root.disk) swap disk size is 256MB and i had recently used the wubi guide wiki to increase the root.disk size , but now /home is a seperate disk and has a capacity of only 133MB, so is that goin to give me trouble in ubuntu hanging... 

If increasing the swap size helps i will do it

and the hanging problem has been with my previous size of 5GB root.disk and 256mb swap (home was not a separate a drive)
I don't understand. You increased the size of the root.disk (that's one thing) and now you have a separate home.disk (another thing) and in that separate home.disk you only have 133MB space remaining? That can definitely cause problems since /home is where all your data (including browser cache) gets stored.

I'd be looking to either 
1. back up important data and reinstall or
2. create a partition for ubuntu and migrate to it

Of those options, 2. is probably the best if you have a lot of customization you don't want to lose.
But if you don't want to partition and don't care about reinstalling, then 1. is the simplest. 

PS also make sure you didn't install grub (legacy). When you use lvpm it sometimes removes grub-pc and replaces it with grub, as it's listed as a dependency. If you have installed grub legacy then the command "grub-install --version" will say 0.97

I am asking - help

What I did:

New computer, single 1TB disk made into 3 partitions.
Installed XP Turbo on one
Installed ubuntu (10.10 I think, what was available last week).
Third partition was intended for Win 7 but I clicked the wrong format button and it became XP Turbo as well. 
Updated ubuntu and killed the computer.

Today I:
Reinstalled XP Turbo over itself from disk (I have an install disk, not boot). This allowed the computer to boot up into XP.

Made a ubuntu cd that does work and will boot the computer.

Tried to run the Boot Info Script, could not find report and I think it failed.

Since the computer now boots into Win XP or from the ubuntu disk I want to install from the disk. I plan to put ubuntu onto the same partition I originally used.

1 - Is there anything I need to do BEFORE I reinstall?
2 - Anything I need to do after IE: "updates" that may be offered?

(About me: I have used computers since the old Atari 400 days, still have my first mac SE - and it works. I teach classes on Apple OS and Windows from time to time. I don't mind changing out boards, ram, power supplies and have done some soldering (surface mount makes this almost impossible now). I know the Apple OS quite well, Windows fairly well (and don't like Windows). Less than 3 hours with working unbuntu.

Hi Elaphae,
first things first, we really need to see either the output of the boot info script and/or the following command:
```
sudo fdisk -lu
```

Here are detailed instructions on how to run the boot script:

Boot the Ubuntu Live CD/USB. Choose the option "Try Ubuntu without any changes." Once the desktop loads come back here and do the following:

1. Download the boot info script. There is a link in my signature.
2. Once downloaded, move the boot info script to the desktop.
3. Open a terminal and run the command

```
sudo bash ~/Desktop/boot_info_script*.sh
```

This will create a RESULTS.txt file on the desktop. Paste the _entire_ contents of that file back here in a new post. Once pasted highlight all text and click the # sign on the toolbar to place code tags around the text. 

Once we have this information it will be easier to advise you on the next step.

Thanks.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-04
> **texas.chef94 said:**
> I have read every word of this thread and while it does not relate to my problem I will state mine and not intending to hijack thread.
1. My effort on this new PC at partitioning on a win 7 device met with total failure. I only know gparted to partition and using it was a cause for recovery. Attachment is partition out of the box.
2. So wubi seems to be my most trouble free way of enjoying Ubuntu BUT need a way around pass word recognition
3.On recovery called it l675allen and gave it PW (initial first question)
4. went to wubi installer user name came up l675allen so I could reasonably assume the PW I had just entered for that user name would work. Not a chance.
Finally is there anyway to start out fresh with wubi and be assured a install
Thank you and await advice

I'm not quite clear on what your problem is... however I'll explain how Wubi works...

When you install it prompts you for your user name (defaults to the Windows user name but you can change this). Then you enter your Ubuntu password twice. Then it sets up your Ubuntu account as follows:
User name: ubuntu user name you chose
User Description: Windows account you installed under
Password: the one you entered twice (not the Windows one)

When you boot your Ubuntu it prompts you with your User Description which is your Windows account user name (not your Ubuntu user name), and then the password you enter must be the one you entered on the WUBI screen, not the Windows one.

e.g. my Windows account I install Wubi under is "Admin" (as my normal account is a limited user). I enter my user name as "bcbc" and my password: xxxx.
When I boot Ubuntu it prompts me with the user Description "Admin" (not the user name "bcbc") and I enter the xxxx password for bcbc (not the Windows one for Admin).
wrong password. 

That's a bit longwinded - but hope it solves your problem.

---

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ ${prev_saved_entry} ]; then
  set saved_entry=${prev_saved_entry}
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z ${boot_once} ]; then
    saved_entry=${chosen}
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n ${have_grubenv} ]; then if [ -z ${boot_once} ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}
insmod ntfs
set root='(hd0,1)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 6c648dac648d7a1a
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then

  insmod gfxterm
  insmod vbe
  if terminal_output gfxterm ; then true ; else
    # For backward compatibility with versions of terminal.mod that don't

fi
insmod ntfs

loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk

set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
set lang=en
insmod gettext
if [ ${recordfail} = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else

fi

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Microsoft Windows XP Home Edition (on /dev/sda1)" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 6c648dac648d7a1a
    drivemap -s (hd0) ${root}
    chainloader +1
}
menuentry "Windows NT/2000/XP (on /dev/sda2)" {
    insmod fat
    set root='(hd0,2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set cced-990e
    drivemap -s (hd0) ${root}
    chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

Which drive did you install Wubi to?

Do me a favor please and check on the C drive it you have the following files:
/wubildr and /wubildr.mbr

If not, copy them over from the drive where Wubi is installed and try again.

They need to be at the root of the C:\ drive.

If this does not work, please post the results of the boot info script mentioned in the first post.

And thanks for the kind words about the thread.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-05
Kemkev,
2 things cause this - the failure to place an entry for Ubuntu in the windows boot manager, or the failure to modify the timeout value (how long windows waits before booting the default).

If you go to the Startup & Recovery settings, it will show you the boot entries as well as the timeout. It's easy to adjust the timeout (e.g. to 15 or 30) if it's set to zero.

If the Ubuntu entry is not there, then you can either edit the boot.ini (XP only) or - in your case - you'd have to add an entry using Windows' command line tool 'bcdedit.exe' or download and run easybcd.

I am not sure what the cause of these problems are - I suspect there are a few different things at play.

PS while I am on the subject, I should add a warning - never set Ubuntu as the default OS and the timeout to zero. Some people do this to skip one of the two boot menus shown (they see a Windows entry in Grub and figure they can use it to boot windows). What happens in this case is that Grub boots windows, windows waits for 0 seconds before transferring control back to Grub. The result is no Windows. It's fairly easy to fix on XP, but not on Vista/7 - requires rebuilding the BCD completely.

---

### Post by KemKev on 2011-02-05
**UPDATE**

**bcdc:** [COLOR="Blue"]**I got the problem resolved**[/COLOR].  As mentioned before, I used EBCD to add the Ubuntu to Windows Boot Manager (using the Linux/BSD "Webi" option).  This put the default Ubuntu bootloader path as C:\NST\AutoNeoGrub0.mbr however, when Ubuntu was selected it displayed only a grub prompt.  The challenge was to point the Ubuntu bootloader path to C:\wubildr.mbr as EBCD does not allow for that kind of editing.

Like you mentioned, the option was to do this using bcdedit.exe - definitely not a job for the faint of heart!  However, after quite a bit of reading up on the command and the various parameters, I was able to do the following:

Run "bcdedit" at the command prompt (set prompt to run as Administrator) and then the following command:

bcdedit /set {identifier} path \wubildr.mbr

where "identifier" = the alphanumeric value shown for Ubuntu when "bcdedit" was run in the first step above.
 
Windows Boot Manager now shows Windows 7 and Ubuntu and when the latter is selected, it launches as it should.

The only hitch is that Ubuntu totally ignores my wireless connection so I am updating this thread using a wired connection.  I'll hop over to the network forum to see if this is a known issue with 10.10, but in the interim, thanks much for the help in pointing me in the right direction.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-07
@myles444
Probably the best way to get help quickly is to provide all the requested information. Also Rubi1200 put a lot of effort into writing up post #1 and it explains in detail...
but based on your own comment that your root.disk is on sda2, then you would have to make sure you substitute sda1 with sda2 in those instructions.
i.e. the line "sudo mount /dev/sda2 /media/win"

Also if any of those lines give you an exception message, you need to stop and figure out the problem. 

If you're still having problems after this then you should post the output of the bootinfoscript, as well as the exception messages you get.

Hope that helps.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-08
@Steve Moss,
sometimes this happens if your computer has leftover software raid metadata. I can't think of anything else right now that might cause this. If you are running a raid, or your computer gave you the option to setup raid when you first got it, this might be the issue.

This is what I suggest - go to [http://www.ubuntu.com/desktop/get-ubuntu/download](http://www.ubuntu.com/desktop/get-ubuntu/download) , download the latest release (or the long term support 10.04) and burn it to CD. Then boot your computer from it, and try it without installing. This will give you an idea if there are any issues with your hardware, and also you can run that bootinfoscript and paste it back here. Plus, you can install from that CD later (either Wubi or normal) so it's not a wasted exercise.

Hope that helps.

---
