### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-09
@dieter-erich
Likely you made a typo when you edited the grub.cfg. Hint, if you could boot Wubi you didn't have to do Solution #1, you could have just proceeded to the **Permanent Fix**.

But it's too late for that I guess. So... now you'll have to do Solution #1 again, this time by booting a live CD. Then make sure you didn't corrupt the grub.cfg.
All you need in grub.cfg to boot is the following:
```
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.32-28-generic" {
	insmod ntfs
	set root='(hd0,1)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set d498435098432ff2
	loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
	set root=(loop0)
	linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-28-generic root=/dev/sda1 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash
	initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-28-generic
}

``` 

BUT if by 'making it worse' you mean that you now get a grub prompt, that's actually not so bad.
Just enter the following from the grub prompt:
```
linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda1 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro
initrd /initrd.img
boot
```

(note - this only works from a grub prompt, not a grub rescue prompt)

Then once booted, go and do the permanent fix and you'll be good.

This is a really good post that explains how to do this with pictures. [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1613132](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1613132)
If you temporarily need to override the options on an installed system - see the part entitled "**How to temporarily set kernel boot options on an installed OS (not wubi)**" (ignore the 'not wubi' bit - it's the same. The next heading shows how to make the options permanent - again works for Wubi.

If you need to override the boot options on the **initial Wubi install** (where it says *Press ESC for more options*) see [post #8 in that thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10089820&postcount=8").

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-10
> **dieter-erich said:**
> Thanks a lot for helping. The outputs are attached!

I can't see anything wrong...

This is what I suggest. If you are running Ubuntu now, run "sudo update-grub" to regenerate your grub.cfg.

Then reboot into Windows and run chkdsk to make sure it's clean: Go to my computer, right click on C:, Properties, Tools tab, click 'Check now' under Error-checking, then check Automatically fix errors. You will have to reboot to complete the check and make sure you see it running to completion.

Then I'd rename your backup of ubuntu on D: ? to e.g. d:\ubuntubackup. I doubt this is an issue, but I want to rule it out - because grub has a search function looking for /ubuntu/disks/root.disk (on any partition) and while I assume it finds the first one (lowest partition), I don't know for sure.
You might want to run chkdsk on the D: drive as well.

Then boot back into Ubuntu again and see if that resolves things.

Note: since I can't see anything wrong, this is more 'general' Wubi advice and I can't guarantee that it will make a difference. Please take this into account - particularly if it could get you back into that 'boots 49/50 times situation'. For example, if you can't afford to have that downtime right now, you might want to just keep Ubuntu running until a more convenient time.

Also, you might want to consider whether migrating to a partition install is the way to go, particularly if you find you are using Ubuntu more than Windows.

---

### Post by dieter-erich on 2011-02-11

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-11
@dieter-erich,
Great, that's good news!

To get rid of kernels, just go into synaptic and uninstall them. Enter the one you want to remove in the search box e.g. 2.6.32-21 (there are 3 items to remove for each) - click on the green box and purge. 

I advise you to keep 2 kernels - so you have a backup if something goes wrong with the current one.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-12
@Steve Moss, it looks like bootinfoscript is unable to mount your ntfs partition under the nvidia raid. So that's like why the install is failing. I'm not sure what you can do about this. 
> Mounting failed:
mount: unknown filesystem type ''

Have a read of this [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/FakeRaidHowto](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/FakeRaidHowto) and see if there's anything in there that makes sense.
But it may simply be that your raid setup is not supported.

It would help us if you could please do the following:

Boot the Ubuntu Live CD/USB. Choose the option "Try Ubuntu without any changes." Once the desktop loads come back here and do the following:

1. Download the boot info script. There is a link in my signature.
2. Once downloaded, move the boot info script to the desktop.
3. Open a terminal and run the command

```
sudo bash ~/Desktop/boot_info_script*.sh
```

This will create a RESULTS.txt file on the desktop. Paste the _entire_ contents of that file back here in a new post. Once pasted highlight all text and click the # sign on the toolbar to place code tags around the text.

### Post by rka599 on 2011-02-13
Hello!  Thank you for your excellent thread.

Your fix worked on my netbook running 10.04.  

Unfortunately, it froze while I was in the middle of entering in the permanent fix.

I had typed in

sudo mkdir /boot/grub

And then the computer froze and when I rebooted, I now go into GNU GRUB version 1.98-1ubuntu9, where it says "Minimal BASH-like Line editing is supported...."

I do not know how to proceed past this point or fix the effects of having only entered in the 1st 2 commands of the permanent fix before the computer freezing.

Any advice would be much appreciated!

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-13
> **rka599 said:**
> Hello!  Thank you for your excellent thread.

Your fix worked on my netbook running 10.04.  

Unfortunately, it froze while I was in the middle of entering in the permanent fix.

I had typed in 

sudo mv /boot/grub /boot/grubold
sudo mkdir /boot/grub

And then the computer froze and when I rebooted, I now go into GNU GRUB version 1.98-1ubuntu9, where it says "Minimal BASH-like Line editing is supported...."

I do not know how to proceed past this point or fix the effects of having only entered in the 1st 2 commands of the permanent fix before the computer freezing.

Any advice would be much appreciated!

Thanks!
Enter the following at the grub prompt
```
configfile /boot/grubold/grub.cfg
```

The when you get booted, go to the terminal again and continue where you left off. You might want to figure out why the computer is freezing too. What brand/model do you have?

---

### Post by rka599 on 2011-02-13
> **bcbc said:**
> Enter the following at the grub prompt
```
configfile /boot/grubold/grub.cfg
```The when you get booted, go to the terminal again and continue where you left off. You might want to figure out why the computer is freezing too. What brand/model do you have?

Thanks so much!  That worked perfectly.  

I have an ASUS EEE PC 1000HE.  It usually freezes when I am in firefox (though I am always in firefox).  The cursor becomes progressively less responsive until I cannot move it any longer and I have to reboot.  Usually after 1 reboot it does not happen again during that session.  But it happens from time to time and is pretty annoying!

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-13
> **rka599 said:**
> Thanks so much!  That worked perfectly.  

I have an ASUS EEE PC 1000HE.  It usually freezes when I am in firefox (though I am always in firefox).  The cursor becomes progressively less responsive until I cannot move it any longer and I have to reboot.  Usually after 1 reboot it does not happen again during that session.  But it happens from time to time and is pretty annoying!

I searched around and you are not alone... (e.g. [this thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1478787")) but there is no clear answer - and nothing specific to Asus 1000HE that I could find. 
All I would suggest is - since Wubi is sensitive to hard resets - you should try [Alt+SysRq R-E-I-S-U-B]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magic_SysRq_key#.22Raising_Elephants.22_mnemonic_device") before resorting to a hard poweroff.

### Post by drs305 on 2011-02-16
newubies,

Welcome to the Ubuntu forums.

It appears you are booting from sdb and using lilo, and that Ubuntu incorrectly thinks your system is on sdf1 rather than sdb1.

If the process you use is to select Windows, then Ubuntu, see if you can press 'e' on the Ubuntu menu (when you see the Ubuntu kernel and recovery mode options) to enter the editing mode. This should allow you to edit the "linux" line. 

Use the cursor buttons to move to the part of the "linux" line which shows "root=sdf1" and change it to "root=sdb1", then press CTRL-x to boot.


> linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.35-22-generic root=/dev/sd**[COLOR="Red"]b1[/COLOR]** loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro 
If it boots, then run the following to update the menu:
```
sudo update-grub
```

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-02-19
Hi and thanks for posting the results.

This seems to be part of the problem with your Wubi install:
> mount: wrong fs type, bad option, bad superblock on /dev/loop1,
       missing codepage or helper program, or other error

Here is what you need to try and do from the LiveCD or LiveUSB:
```
sudo mkdir /media/win
sudo mount /dev/sda2 /media/win
sudo fsck /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk
```

Try mounting the Wubi install after running the previous last command:

```
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
```

If this works and you don't get any error messages, then continue from the main post of this thread with problem #2, solution #1

---

### Post by Artemis Fowl on 2011-02-19
> **Rubi1200 said:**
> Hi and thanks for posting the results.

This seems to be part of the problem with your Wubi install:


Here is what you need to try and do from the LiveCD or LiveUSB:
```
sudo mkdir /media/win
sudo mount /dev/sda2 /media/win
sudo fsck /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk
```Try mounting the Wubi install after running the previous last command:

```
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
```If this works and you don't get any error messages, then continue from the main post of this thread with problem #2, solution #1

Thank you. I am extremely grateful. One question: Will this happen again?

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-02-19
No problem, you are more than welcome :-)

> Will this happen again?
If you apply the Permanent Fix from the main post once booted back in Ubuntu, including locking the grub packages, then hopefully not.

However, since Wubi uses a virtual disk within the host system (Windows), there is always the possibility that some type of file corruption can occur.

In general, it also advisable to make a backup copy of the root.disk in case this kind of problem occurs again. Best to keep it on a USB stick or some other external medium.

---

### Post by Artemis Fowl on 2011-02-19
> **Rubi1200 said:**
> No problem, you are more than welcome :-)


If you apply the Permanent Fix from the main post once booted back in Ubuntu, including locking the grub packages, then hopefully not.

However, since Wubi uses a virtual disk within the host system (Windows), there is always the possibility that some type of file corruption can occur.

In general, it also advisable to make a backup copy of the root.disk in case this kind of problem occurs again. Best to keep it on a USB stick or some other external medium.

Again, thank you, I assumed when I successfully booted into Ubuntu I was done. Applied the permanent fix.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-20
> **gouthamsidarth said:**
> When ever the system restarts there is no option or menu to go to ubuntu. its directly loading to windows 7.

Follow this link to check whether an entry for Ubuntu has been added - make sure the timeout is not zero (set it to 15 or 30 if it is). [http://windows7-tutorial.com/?p=132](http://windows7-tutorial.com/?p=132)

If there is no Ubuntu entry it's possible to add one manually, but I'd recommend reinstalling instead.

Reinstalling is unlikely to fix an issue such as your wireless card. Next time go to the [wireless & networking subforum]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=336") and follow the stickies there for instructions.

---

  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=

    save_env saved_entry

}

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-25
> **ajay1313 said:**
> Hi there,

I'm a newcomer to Ubuntu and have tried installing the netbook edition of 10.10. I am able to boot into Ubuntu with the liveUSB but when attempting a boot from the HD I get the error:

ALERT! /dev/sdb5 does not exist

I have tried solution #1 to problem #2 as i thought this was the most probable cause, without any luck however. It would not let me make a new directory as it was already in use. An alternative problem may be that I have an Acer Aspire One which came with pre-installed dual-OS of Windows 7 and Android (pretty useless tbh). Anyway, I have been mightily impressed with the advice being dished out in this forum to individual cases and hope you can help me too.

I have attached the results.txt from boot_info_script.

Many thanks in advance!
It looks like you can boot Wubi - you were booted in it when you ran the bootinfoscript. You can confirm this by running "sudo losetup /dev/loop0" and it should say "/host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk"
> ============================ "mount | grep ^/dev  output: ===========================

Device           Mount_Point              Type       Options

/dev/loop0       /                        ext4       (rw,errors=remount-ro,commit=600)
/dev/sdb5        /host                    fuseblk    (rw,nosuid,nodev,relatime,user_id=0,group_id=0,allow_other,blksize=4096)


When you have the usb stick plugged in your computer sees it as /dev/sda and your internal drive as /dev/sdb. When you unplug it, it probably sees your internal as /dev/sda. So, next time you boot without the usb, hit 'e' on the first entry, change the line starting with 'linux /boot/vmlinuz...'  modifying "root=/dev/sdb5" to "root=/dev/sda5" and CTRL+x to boot.
Then "sudo update-grub" as soon as you've booted.

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-02-26
Glad you got things sorted out.

Make sure you lock the grub packages to prevent problems with updates for GRUB.

See the main post just after the Permanent Fix section.

### Post by dieter-erich on 2011-02-26
Hi,
  thanks to bcbc I got my netbook to work again with wubi. 

I now tried the same with an old Acer laptop that failed to boot into wubi upon having installed it as usual. I started from a life CD with ubuntu 10.10, mounted the partition, which ubuntu was on without problems. However, when entering "sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt" I always get the message (translated from German) "you must indicate the type of file system" It seems that the option -o does not exist any more in ubuntu 10.10. When trying with -t loop I get "unknown file system type "loop". Whats wrong?
Thanks for hints!

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-26
> **dieter-erich said:**
> Hi,
  thanks to bcbc I got my netbook to work again with wubi. 

I now tried the same with an old Acer laptop that failed to boot into wubi upon having installed it as usual. I started from a life CD with ubuntu 10.10, mounted the partition, which ubuntu was on without problems. However, when entering "sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt" I always get the message (translated from German) "you must indicate the type of file system" It seems that the option -o does not exist any more in ubuntu 10.10. When trying with -t loop I get "unknown file system type "loop". Whats wrong?
Thanks for hints!
The mount command automatically detects the file system type - usually. If there is some issue detecting it then it complains. This could indicate some sort of corruption but it can also occur if the root.disk hasn't yet been formatted.

If you had Wubi running on that machine (had booted into Ubuntu, or completed the installation - seen the slideshow in other words) then likely the file is corrupt. In that case, run fsck on it. If you had problems on the first reboot, then probably there is nothing to mount.

---

### Post by dieter-erich on 2011-02-27
Definitely, the wubi setup had not run to completion: On reboot after the install it already did not work (message such as "no wubildr" etc.). I had installed on D: (not on C:). So I copied wubildr and wubildr.mbr into D: **It then was able to reboot and complete the installation.** I suppose that the root.disk had not yet been prepared correctly! Although, there is still a message saying "no wubildr" or something alike it now boots!

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-27
> **dieter-erich said:**
> Definitely, the wubi setup had not run to completion: On reboot after the install it already did not work (message such as "no wubildr" etc.). I had installed on D: (not on C: ). So I copied wubildr and wubildr.mbr into D: **It then was able to reboot and complete the installation.** I suppose that the root.disk had not yet been prepared correctly! Although, there is still a message saying "no wubildr" or something alike it now boots!
wubildr.mbr is a grub4dos loader and it looks on the root of every partition until it finds the wubildr file. It outputs a message "Try (hdX,Y): <file system type>" for each partition it checks. That's normal. If it doesn't find it it appends, ": no wubildr" and then looks on the next partition.

It's odd that the install failed to copy these into the root of at least one partition (C:\ for sure, but I get them on my hidden OEM tools partition as well) - and if it did place these in C: it should have found wubildr. I think there's an issue on some BIOSes detecting files > 137GB from the start of the partition so if C: is severely defragmented it could fail to find it.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-02-27
> **helosk said:**
> I keep getting the error msg "there is no disk in the drive. Please insert a disk into drive /device/harddisk5/dr5."

Wubi does various drive checks and whenever it encounters a drive that is empty it spits that exception out (or python's runtime does). Either way if you remove the offending drives (usually a multimedia card reader) then it won't do that anymore.

---

### Post by ericbroder on 2011-02-28
> **Rubi1200 said:**
> 
[SIZE=3]**Problem #2:**[/SIZE]

You have Wubi installed and are able to boot Windows normally. 

However, attempting to boot Ubuntu leads to a number of possible failures including, but not limited to, the following:

computer reboots without user interaction, a black screen with or without any error messages, loadfont errors, file not found errors, and the like.

These issues affect the following versions: 10.04, 10.04.1, and 10.10

The solutions for each version are somewhat different and will be noted next to the relevant solution.

[SIZE=3]**Possible Cause:**[/SIZE]

Recent upgrades to GRUB and the lupin package (used for Wubi) have caused the wubildr file to become out of sync with the latest version of GRUB. This appears to be a problem whether Wubi is installed on the Windows partition or a separate partition.

As a result, wubildr is unable to handle the commands in the grub.cfg menu and the computer reboots.

[SIZE=3]**Solution #1 (10.04):**[/SIZE]

If it is a 10.04 install, the fix is to boot an Ubuntu LiveCD/USB, loop mount the wubi root.disk and manually edit the grub.cfg file.

**IMPORTANT:**

You need to correctly identify your Windows partition. The examples here are meant to be just that, examples.

If you do not know or are unsure which is the correct partition, run the [boot info script]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/") and either post it here or use the information for your own purposes.

As an example, a Windows partition with a Wubi install would look like this in the RESULTS.txt:

```
[COLOR=Red]sda3[/COLOR]: _________________________________________________________________________

    File system:       ntfs
    Boot sector type:  Windows Vista/7
    Boot sector info:  No errors found in the Boot Parameter Block.
    Operating System:  Windows 7
    Boot files/dirs:   /Windows/System32/winload.exe /wubildr.mbr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr.mbr /wubildr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr /ubuntu/disks/root.disk 
                       /ubuntu/disks/swap.disk
```Note that sda3 is the partition which contains your Windows installation as well as the files needed by Wubi.

Boot a LiveCD/USB, identify your Windows partition that contains the root.disk (make sure you mount the correct partition; this example assumes /dev/sda**[COLOR=Red]1[/COLOR]**), and edit grub.cfg as follows:

```
sudo mkdir /media/win 
sudo mount /dev/sda**[COLOR=Red]1[/COLOR]** /media/win
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
sudo cp /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg.copy
sudo chmod +w /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg
gksu gedit /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg
```Remove all lines in the file from the start of the file up to,_ but not including_, the following menuentry ```
 ### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
```Save, reboot.


I had Problem #2, and Solution #1 worked for me, thanks! I'll post some of my error messages here to help with the SEO juice.

> alloc magic is broken at 0xbb747880
Aborted. Press any key to exit.> PXE-E61: Media test failure, check cable
PXE-M0F: Exiting Intel Boot Agent.> No valid operating system

---

### Post by rgstrator on 2011-03-09
Hi everybody, my support and congratulations for this thread- I am very very glad to see it, as I kind of yelled about the possibility of bricking (from the point of an inexperienced user) new netbooks a few months back. For me wubi became a rather permanent solution, and I assume the same goes for others as well- so any wubi-related thread suddenly became very valuable. Again thanks to all of you, including developers, who charged to the rescue in such a short notice. You set an example, and I for one will try to give more of my time to the community.

Now to business; and I quote

> **Rubi1200 said:**
> [SIZE=3]**Permanent Fix (as of the time of writing):**[/SIZE]

Thanks to bcbc, there is a more or less permanent fix for these problems.

These steps are applied **after** you have restored the Wubi install and are able to boot normally into both Windows and Ubuntu.

From the terminal:

```
sudo mv /boot/grub /boot/grubold
sudo mkdir /boot/grub
sudo cp /boot/grubold/grubenv /boot/grub
sudo update-grub
```[SIZE=2][B]
FIXED![/B][/SIZE]

Not done yet - prevent GRUB updates from breaking the Wubi install again:

Go to System > Administration > Synaptic Package Manager and select the grub-pc and grub-common packages. Click on Package > Lock Version.

[COLOR=Red]I cannot stress this enough; if you want to prevent this happening either on a freshly installed system or a fixed system, please lock the grub packages.[/COLOR]

So the question is; what if one has not had any problems yet, because one has not updated ANY grub-related packages so far? Would skipping all the above, and just locking grub version work as a solution? Will I then be able to stay carefree and update ALL other packages, recommended or otherwise, without having to worry that I'll break the system?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-03-09
> **rgstrator said:**
> So the question is; what if one has not had any problems yet, because one has not updated ANY grub-related packages so far? Would skipping all the above, and just locking grub version work as a solution? Will I then be able to stay carefree and update ALL other packages, recommended or otherwise, without having to worry that I'll break the system?

Locking grub will prevent the *known* issue: that grub updates in 10.04 and above are breaking wubi installs. If you haven't updated grub yet (you only have two files in /boot/grub/) then this is a good idea.

Does it mean you won't have a problem with *all* updates now and into the future? No, I don't think anybody would guarantee that. But if a new problem appears, we should pick it up fairly quickly and add appropriate warnings here (so subscribe to this thread).

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-03-10
mrtn474,
Random freezing is likely not related to Wubi. There are a number of threads devoted to this (e.g. [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1478787](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1478787)), and I've experienced this on my own non-wubi 10.10 install. Of course, it's difficult to know what causes this as there are no log entries... hard to say - could be hardware, software, graphics; might be different things for different people too.

I have noticed freezing on shutdown of my wubi installs. I just hit [ALT+SysRQ and REISUB]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magic_SysRq_key#.22Raising_Elephants.22_mnemonic_device") instead of hard shutdowns as it's safer. (This is different to the other freezes where I've found that nothing works to unfreeze). You might consider running chkdsk on windows to make sure the file system is clean if this happens frequently.

Hibernation is not supported in Wubi installs as they use a swap file. In order to hibernate you require a swap partition that is greater that the size of your RAM.

Since I'm using a netbook that does not have the SysRq key but does have a PrtSc key, how do I have it so that it acts as a SysRq key?

> Furthermore, some keyboards may not provide a separate SysRq key. In this case, a separate "Print Screen" key should be present. Under graphical environments (such as Gnome or KDE) 'Alt'+'PrintScrn/SysRq'+key combination generally only leads to a screenshot being dumped. To avoid this Print Screen feature the magic SysRq combination should include the Ctrl, becoming 'Ctrl'+'Alt'+'SysRq'+key. For the same purposes the AltGr key, if present, can be used in place of the Alt key. The magic SysRq can also be accessed from the serial console.

Can I simply use it as a SysRq key by adding Ctrl to the key combination, or do I need to change some settings?

### Post by bcbc on 2011-03-10
> **mrtn474 said:**
> Since I'm using a netbook that does not have the SysRq key but does have a PrtSc key, how do I have it so that it acts as a SysRq key?

Can I simply use it as a SysRq key by adding Ctrl to the key combination, or do I need to change some settings?
Yes, use the PrtSc. Maybe they didn't have enough real estate to put both labels on the key. This is a kernel level interrupt so it's not something you can map yourself as far as I know.

So summary: 

1) It doesn't appear to like the cdrom partition on my flash usb drive. I'll remove that and try again. I can always re-do it later. :) 

2) It successfully shrunk the ntfs partition, but did not successfully create a new partition in the free space. 

3) the installer pretended to continue anyways, even though it wasn't really doing anything. 

4) because it wasn't really doing anything after it shrunk windows, and didn't create or install anything on the windows partition, it also didn't update the boot record or install grub or anything, so windows booting is unaffected. 

Given all that, so far, should I go ahead and try with a usb drive that has no cdfs partition, and see if I can get it to successfully install that way? I'd burn a real cd if I had a working burner. (laptop drives just don't seem to hold up to heavy use with me ) Alas, it will just have to be a bootable usb. 

Is it possible it needs to reboot after shrinking windows, before continuing the install? Dunno. I'm thinking it might go smoother now that it has actual unpartitioned free space to use? Hope so! 

will keep you posted. Please let me know if you spot any errors in my thought process, or have further suggestions. Thanks!

side note: when booting back into windows after resizing the ntfs volume, it will detect and install new hardware "generic volume" and tell you to reboot. I guess it's because the ntfs volume got resized? anyhow, I rebooted windows and all was fine there, so I guess this was normal.

I think I have 2 different issues with kubuntu going on here. 

One where wubi creates its volume and puts the installer in it, within windows, and correctly updates the boot loader, but then crashes when it reboots and tries to install. 

One where a normal install from bootable media is failing to actually create a linux partition or install stuff. 

Both of these are using the latest kubuntu 32bit iso

Going to try manually creating partitions in the newly created free space. I wonder if it's because all that free space is at the end of a 160gb drive? But I didn't think that should be a problem on such a new system. You don't think I'm gonna have to move all my stuff around to make room near the beginning of the drive for a boot partition, do you? ### Post by grayceworks on 2011-03-14
Alright, so here's what I did. This obviously won't help with wubi installs, sorry. :(

I partitioned and formatted the free space as 3gb swap, 10gb root w ext3, and 20gb home w ext3 partitions using partition wizard. 

I then rebooted and loaded the kubuntu installer. It was going to ignore my newly created partitions and try creating its own again, but I told it to do a custom partition table instead. I then set the mount points for the swap, root, and home partitions, and set the boot loader to install to the mbr. 

it continued with a brief warning that I might have existing data in the partitions I selected. I continued, and now, instead of it staying at "detecting filesystem.............100%" forever, even as it went thru the slideshow, it actually did something, like copying files, setting up grub, downloading additional drivers and updates, and lo and behold! finishing the installation. 

I rebooted when requested, and it had my windows partition and recovery partition labels confused, which confused me, when I selected Windows Vista, and got the recovery wizard, and so I rebooted and selected Windows Recovery, and got my windows installation. I'll have to edit that. 

Other than that, so far so good! 

But like I said, it doesn't help wubi at all. Unless it's somehow related, with it not understanding how the wubi volume is formatted? Since it obviously didn't understand another unformatted partition on my drive, maybe when it's trying to do the wubi install, it's not understanding the wubi volume? I have no idea, I'm just tossing out ideas here.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-03-15
> **Ceriyx said:**
> &#65279;Hi
Ceriyx  :KS
Boot from windows rescue disk to rescue prompt and run:
```
bootrec.exe /fixmbr
```

That should get both windows and ubuntu booting. Then look at post #1 and apply the Permanent Fix after booting into Ubuntu.

Next time you should paste the entire results.txt as it gives more info than what you've pasted. But I think that'll do it for you.

### Post by drs305 on 2011-03-16
> **BLTicklemonster said:**
> I've got stuff on this drive I cannot afford to loose. Once I get access, I intend to back them up on the network and remove wubi and do a full install of Unbuntu on a partition. In the meantime, I can't even get my files from a live cd! 

What can I do?

Have you tried running the LiveCD and mounting root.disk to access your Ubuntu files? The following assumes Windows is on sda1. Change 'sda1' to the appropriate partition.

```

sudo mkdir /mnt/win /mnt/wubi
sudo mount /dev/sd[COLOR="Red"]a1[/COLOR] /mnt/win
sudo mount -o loop /mnt/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt/wubi
gksu nautilus /mnt/wubi &

```

#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by /usr/sbin/grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ ${prev_saved_entry} ]; then
  set saved_entry=${prev_saved_entry}
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z ${boot_once} ]; then
    saved_entry=${chosen}
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n ${have_grubenv} ]; then if [ -z ${boot_once} ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}
if [ ${recordfail} = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.32-24-generic" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 1e2c1f312c1f0405
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-24-generic root=/dev/sda2 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash
    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-24-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.32-24-generic (recovery mode)" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 1e2c1f312c1f0405
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-24-generic root=/dev/sda2 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro single 
    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-24-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Windows 7 (loader) (on /dev/sda1)" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 90d6f958d6f93ed2
    chainloader +1
}
menuentry "Windows 7 (loader) (on /dev/sda2)" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 1e2c1f312c1f0405
    chainloader +1
}
menuentry "Windows Vista (loader) (on /dev/sda3)" {
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,3)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 381044ea1044b124
    chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###

============================= sda2/Wubi/etc/fstab: =============================

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
# Use 'blkid -o value -s UUID' to print the universally unique identifier
# for a device; this may be used with UUID= as a more robust way to name
# devices that works even if disks are added and removed. See fstab(5).
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>
proc            /proc           proc    nodev,noexec,nosuid 0       0
/host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /               ext4    loop,errors=remount-ro 0       1
/host/ubuntu/disks/swap.disk none            swap    loop,sw         0       0

================= sda2/Wubi: Location of files loaded by Grub: =================


   9.0GB: boot/grub/grub.cfg
   2.5GB: boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-24-generic
  10.9GB: boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-24-generic
  10.9GB: boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-30-generic
   2.5GB: initrd.img
  10.9GB: vmlinuz


```

### Post by bcbc on 2011-03-23
@StutteringJohnSmith,
seeing "Try (hd0,0): NTFS5: No wubildr" is not an error. It's a 'notice' that there is no wubildr on the first partition /dev/sda1. Wubi uses grub4dos and it checks all partitions until it finds the wubildr in the root of the partition, which in your case is on /dev/sda2.

Since you reinstalled Wubi - that means your old Wubi install has been removed. You now have a new one. So what exactly is the error you are getting? i.e. what happens after you see that Try (hd0,0) message?

---

### Post by StutteringJohnSmith on 2011-03-23
I started to get the "NO WUBILDIR" message yes2today if the system updated.  I never had it before and the system would not boot up anymore..

I did a installed and reinstall (well stilling reinstalling should take me about a month to get everything working again) and now I see the message still but it boots. I am just scared that it is going to stop working.  I did not have the message the first few months..

### Post by bcbc on 2011-03-23
> **StutteringJohnSmith said:**
> I started to get the "NO WUBILDIR" message yes2today if the system updated.  I never had it before and the system would not boot up anymore..

I did a installed and reinstall (well stilling reinstalling should take me about a month to get everything working again) and now I see the message still but it boots. I am just scared that it is going to stop working.  I did not have the message the first few months..

The presence or absence of the message has no impact on whether a Wubi install boots or not. 

Likely you were hit with the same grub bug that is breaking many other 10.04 installs. So you need to lock the packages grub-pc and grub-common as described in post #1.

And avoid reinstalling until you've exhausted attempts to recover. That should always be a last ditch effort - because it deletes everything you had. In most cases you can recover data from the root.disk through other means (see post #1) or fix the problem.

In general a wubi install is more vulnerable due to the virtual disk being a single file (typical install). Although this was not likely a factor in the problems you had, if you are going to the trouble to set everything up again and plan to use Ubuntu long-term it's worth considering whether to do a normal dual-boot. Or consider keeping current backups of the virtual disk(s).

---

### Post by tc101 on 2011-03-25
When do you think this grub problem will be fixed?  I would like to install ubuntu on an XP computer I use for work, but it is important that I don't break the XP install.  I don't want to spend a lot of time figuring out problems.  

Is it realistic to think I can do this quickly and easily, or could it eat up hours or even days of my time?

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-03-25
> **tc101 said:**
> When do you think this grub problem will be fixed?  I would like to install ubuntu on an XP computer I use for work, but it is important that I don't break the XP install.  I don't want to spend a lot of time figuring out problems.  

Is it realistic to think I can do this quickly and easily, or could it eat up hours or even days of my time?
From what I know, not anytime soon.

However, there is a simple and easy fix for the issue here:

After a fresh install of Wubi, lock the grub-* packages. See the section in the main post just after the Permanent Fix (which you don't need to apply on a fresh install).

If you do this, it is highly unlikely that any issues will arise.

The other thing to do to keep things safer, so to speak, is to keep a backup of the root.disk on an external medium such as a USB stick. That way, should Wubi become corrupted or anything else, you have a quick means of restoring the install; simply copy the backup root.disk back to the Ubuntu folder on C (or wherever you installed) and you are back in business.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-03-26
pichi123,
Your root.disk is missing. This is usually due to file corruption - Windows tries to recover it and may or may not be successful. Did you experience any hard shutdowns or anything else that might have caused problems with the root.disk?

What I'd do is 1) look for the file in the C:\ubuntu\disks\root.disk directory and see if it's there - backup if you can. 2) if it's there, run fsck on it. 3) if it's not there, look in C:\FOUND.000 (hidden directory) for it or something of a similar size (could be called chk0000.xxx). If you find it copy it back. 

Unfortunately I have seen a few cases of missing root.disks. If it is gone then your Wubi Ubuntu is gone too.

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-03-26
Okay, I will assume that the Windows/Wubi install is sda1 (based on the results text you posted previously). If it is not, change the commands to reflect the correct partition.

From the LiveCD:

```
sudo mkdir /media/win
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /media/win
sudo fsck /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk
```

When finished, you should be able to mount the root.disk:
```
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
```

### Post by bcbc on 2011-03-26
> **pichi123 said:**
> your like god, thanks for the think. i could acces the root.disk without a problem

That's great that you can recover the data. 

The output from fsck shows:
> fsck.ext2: Input/output error while trying to open /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.diskIt should say fsck**.ext4**, which indicates that the "input/output" error might be preventing it from accessing the root.disk. So I would recommend running chkdsk (boot windows, My computer, right click on C:, Properties, Tools Check disk for errors, fix automatically). You'll have to reboot to let chkdsk run.

Hopefully this will fix that i/o error associated with the file. Note that some people have reported their root.disk files going missing - and the cause is unclear - so make sure you get everything you can off it. And consider trying to back it up under windows before running chkdsk.

PS can you think of anything that might have caused the problem e.g. hanging, hard shutdowns? In general, you should avoid hard shutdowns if possible. Use Alt+SysRq REISUB instead.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-03-26
> **san_198111 said:**
> Thanks bcbc!
Everything looks ok now.
When possible please let me know what could have caused this and anything I can do to avoid it.
I don't know all the causes of file system corruption. I know that wubi installs are more vulnerable to hard shutdowns because the file system is on a loop device (root.disk file). So if you notice that your system is non-responsive, try and use Alt+SysRq REISUB to restart rather than powering off.

Not only do you have a chance of corruption on the loop mounted file system, but there is a chance of corruption on the host file system as well. And the main issue with using Wubi installs is that all files are stored on a single file. So if you use Wubi long term it's good to keep backups of the root.disk and/or backups of important data on the root.disk. (Backups are also important on any type of install)

### Post by drs305 on 2011-03-27
masterdan,

Welcome to the Ubuntu forums.  :-)

Are you just asking how you can rename some of the menu entries? If so, I recommend Grub Customizer. It's a GUI app that make a lot of tweaks to the menu, including name changes and reordering.

You can learn more about it via the "Customizer" link in my signature line.

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-03-30
The best thing to do right now is post the results of the boot info script so we know what we are dealing with:

Boot the Ubuntu Live CD/USB. Choose the option "Try Ubuntu without any changes." Once the desktop loads come back here and do the following:

1. Download the boot info script. There is a link in my signature.
2. Once downloaded, move the boot info script to the desktop.
3. Open a terminal and run the command

```
sudo bash ~/Desktop/boot_info_script*.sh
```

This will create a RESULTS.txt file on the desktop. Paste the entire contents of that file back here in a new post. Once pasted highlight all text and click the # sign on the toolbar to place code tags around the text.

---

### Post by fiklein on 2011-03-30
I tried getting the script to run. I even tried downloading Nautilus Open Terminal, but could not get Open Terminal to run, although the it seemed to install okay using the live CD.
This is what I got from a terminal:
#ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo bash ~/home/ubuntu/Downloads/boot_info_script055.sh
bash: /home/ubuntu/home/ubuntu/Downloads/boot_info_script055.sh: No such file or directory
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ #
Where do I go from here?

---

### Post by drs305 on 2011-03-30
> **fiklein said:**
> 
This is what I got from a terminal:
#ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo bash [COLOR="Red"]~/home/ubuntu[/COLOR]/Downloads/boot_info_script055.sh
bash: /home/ubuntu/home/ubuntu/Downloads/boot_info_script055.sh: No such file or directory
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ #
Where do I go from here?

**~** is the same as **/home/ubuntu** so it repeated itself. Use either one or the other, but not both:
```
sudo bash /home/ubuntu/Downloads/boot_info_script055.sh
or
~/Downloads/boot_info_script055.sh
```

---

### Post by drs305 on 2011-03-30
> **fiklein said:**
> 
This is what I got from a terminal:
#ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ sudo bash [COLOR="Red"]~/home/ubuntu[/COLOR]/Downloads/boot_info_script055.sh
bash: [COLOR="Red"]/home/ubuntu/home/ubuntu[/COLOR]/Downloads/boot_info_script055.sh: No such file or directory
ubuntu@ubuntu:~$ #
Where do I go from here?

**~/** is the same as **/home/ubuntu/** so it repeated itself. Use either one or the other, but not both:
```
sudo bash /home/ubuntu/Downloads/boot_info_script055.sh
or
sudo bash ~/Downloads/boot_info_script055.sh
```

### Post by pichi123 on 2011-04-03

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-04-03
> **pichi123 said:**
> heyyy guys, im back again... this time i istalled wubi on my other computer but i cant conect to the internet b ecause i have a thing on the wireless that says "wireless, device not ready (missing firmware)" wat do?
This is not really related to this thread, but I will try and help anyway.

Go to System > Administration > Additional Drivers and check whether or not something needs to be installed for the wireless card.

Also, this command will tell you what card you have so you can search for it:

```
sudo lshw -C network
```In future, please start a new thread for these types of issues.

Thanks.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-06
Good news for Wubi users. One of Canonical's lead devs (Colin Watson) is currently working on the outstanding Wubi bugs. He has found the real cause of the booting failures - some sort of memory corruption when replacing the loop disk - and this problem will be fixed in Natty and also passed back to Maverick and Lucid. In addition he is coming up with a solution to the MBR overwriting problem when Wubi is not installed to the windows partition.

I wouldn't unlock grub-* packages just yet, but it's good news for Wubi users to finally see these long-standing issues addressed.

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-04-06
Excellent news indeed bcbc and thanks for sharing this information with all of us.

I look forward to hearing more good things in the near future.

---

I wouldn't unlock grub-* packages just yet, but it's good news for Wubi users to finally see these long-standing issues addressed.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-07
> **bcbc said:**
> Good news for Wubi users. One of Canonical's lead devs (Colin Watson) is currently working on the outstanding Wubi bugs. He has found the real cause of the booting failures - some sort of memory corruption when replacing the loop disk - and this problem will be fixed in Natty and also passed back to Maverick and Lucid. In addition he is coming up with a solution to the MBR overwriting problem when Wubi is not installed to the windows partition.

I wouldn't unlock grub-* packages just yet, but it's good news for Wubi users to finally see these long-standing issues addressed.

Just a follow up - I believe there is one other grub update that will be applied to Lucid/Maverick prior to the Wubi fix. I've asked that they be combined to avoid more problems (especially on fresh Maverick installs which have been safe so far), but it's definitely a good idea to keep refusing grub updates unless it specifically mentions the Wubi fix (LP# 610898 ). (And also there's really no need to update grub on a working Wubi, so leaving these packages locked is the safest solution for now).

I used Wubi to install Mythbuntu 10.10 to my second logical partition, which is the fifth partition on the drive, and put wubildr and wubildr.mbr on my third primary partition.

title		Mythbuntu
unhide		(hd0,2)

insmod part_msdos

then it goes to my boot.ini, which has an XP entry and
C:\wubildr.mbr = "Mythbuntu"

If I select the Mythbuntu entry, I then get another Grub v1.98 menu, with an sda1 and an sda3 entry, which reads:
insmod part_msdos
insmod ntfs
set root='(hd0,msdos3)'
search .... (this was too long to copy)
drivemap -s (hd0) ${root}
chainloader +1

and if I select that, I get an error:

and it sends me back to the boot.ini menu.

I'd prefer to boot to Mythbuntu straight from my Grub4dos menu, so is there anyway to get that working? If not, could someone help me with sorting out the boot.ini->Grub v1.98 method please.
You'd probably need to chainload wubildr.mbr, not wubildr.

That boot selection you're showing is to boot windows, not MythBuntu. The grub menu with 1.98 is correct, but it should have an Ubuntu/Mythbuntu entry right at the top. If not... did you perhaps press SKIP during the Ubuntu installation slideshow? That's a known bug.
The entry will look like the above, but instead of 'chainload' it will have "loopback loop0 ...." and then lines to load the linux kernel and initrd.img.

I don't believe grub4dos can loopmount the root.disk although I may be wrong: maybe newer versions of grub4dos can do this.

It seems like lupin-support (the package that Wubi requires to setup the boot entries) isn't being installed. I haven't personally tried Mythbuntu using Wubi... 
You might need to install Ubuntu instead and add the packages required for Mythbuntu afterwards. Or I suppose you can try booting the wubi manually and installing lupin-support. I haven't done this either - seems like a lot of work and no guarantee of success :)

I'll try Mythbuntu sometime and register a bug if I can duplicate this - or if you like you can register the bug yourself on [https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+filebug](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+filebug)

What release are you installing?

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-13
> **doveman said:**
> It's Mythbuntu 10.10.

I'll uninstall and reinstall with the verbose option to generate some logs to attach to the bugreport.
I just tried the Natty Mythbuntu beta2 (I was doing ISO testing on Ubuntu beta 2 anyway) and exactly the same thing happened to me. I booted it manually and confirmed that the package lupin-support is not installed.

I'm going to play with it and see if I can fix it manually. It's kind of cool, except I don't have a tv tuner or any hardware to make it useful so this won't be a long-lived experiment. I'll track down your bug report an confirm it though.

EDIT: OK... so it's pretty easy to patch. You just need to know what partition your root.disk is on. Mine is on /dev/sda2:
1. Manually boot mythbuntu, by hitting 'c' to get to a grub prompt and then entering:
```
linux /vmlinuz root=/dev**/sda2** loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro quiet splash
initrd /initrd.img
boot
```Then once you are in Mythbuntu, make sure you are connected to the internet; then go to a terminal, Accessories, Terminal and install lupin-support:
```
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get  install lupin-support
```Then reboot (you can check grub.cfg first to make sure, confirming the new menuentries are there):
```
less /boot/grub/grub.cfg
sudo reboot
```

EDIT2: I couldn't find your bug report so created my own: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/mythbuntu/+bug/759492](https://bugs.launchpad.net/mythbuntu/+bug/759492)

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-13
> **doveman said:**
> OK, I worked out that /dev/sda6 is the correct partition for me.

However, when I used the commands you gave I got an error about MMIO, then the Ubuntu loading screen came up and then my monitor switched off!
I searched around and the MMIO message is supposed to be harmless. What graphics card do you have? You could try some different kernel boot options... i.e. change the linux line with:
```
linux /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda6 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro *nomodeset* quiet splash
```
Nomodeset seems to be helpful on nvidia cards, and I think I used it last night (I have an ati x1300) when booting mythbuntu - I've had intermittent boot issues since lucid and that seems to help.

### Post by doveman on 2011-04-14
Well things just got a little easier.

Turns out that Grub4DOS can loopmount, so I managed to get Mythbuntu to boot using:

find --set-root /ubuntu/disks/mytag.tag
kernel /ubuntu/disks/boot/vmlinuz root=/dev/sda6 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro quiet splash 
initrd /ubuntu/disks/boot/initrd.img

I had to extract the vmlinuz and initrd.img from a Virtualbox Ubuntu 10.10 on another PC, so I should probably replace them with the Mythbuntu ones now (I've got little idea about Linux, so I don't know if these files are specific to each install). I did see a couple of errors about something in /lib when it was booting though.

Mythbuntu's still not working properly as it can't connect to the backend (related to the problem I had when installing), but there's a Mythbuntu forum at [http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=301](http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=301) so I'll ask there about that.

Thanks for your help. I wouldn't have got this far without it!

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-14
> **doveman said:**
> I had to extract the vmlinuz and initrd.img from a Virtualbox Ubuntu 10.10 on another PC
That's what I meant - it's not grub4dos loopmounting the disk. That's being done after the kernel is loaded (that's how Wubi used to work before Grub2 - it kept it's /boot partition on the Windows host to get around the loop mounting problem), and also I wouldn't use the 10.10 kernel for that. If it works, okay, but it seems like a bad idea.

I'm also a bit puzzled why I was able to manually boot Mythbuntu but the same technique is not working in your case ??? Maybe some other issue...

---

### Post by altheablue on 2011-04-16
Thank you bcbc and Rubi1200 and everyone else for this thread; it has saved me twice now.
 
 I just did something dumb. I ran ```
sudo apt-get upgrade
``` And now I know  that sudo overrides the locks I have on grub-common and grub-pc,  because it updated both of them. (I should have known that.)

So, to preemptively prevent getting the boot issue again, should I edit grub.cfg by doing this: "Remove all lines in the file from the start of the file up to,_ but not including_, the following menuentry ```
### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
```I did that before when I had this problem after upgrading to Maverick, but I wanted to check about this particular situation before I edit the file that says "DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE." ;) 

Thanks again.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-16
Altheablue,

I don't think that's the problem. As far as I know there is no grub update yet on Maverick - and the one that is coming is now being bundled with the Wubi fix that will prevent these boot problems from recurring.

Did you apply the Permanent Fix from post #1? If you didn't, then it's likely that a kernel update resulted in grub.cfg being regenerated.

The Permanent Fix is the only manual workaround that solves this problem until the proper grub/lupin fix is released. Editing grub.cfg by hand isn't a good idea except when it's the only way to boot back into Wubi to apply the permanent fix.

Hope that helps. If you need more help, please post the output of ```
ls /boot/grub
```(that's lower case LS)

---

### Post by drs305 on 2011-04-17
> **altheablue said:**
>  I just did something dumb. I ran ```
sudo apt-get upgrade
``` And now I know  that sudo overrides the locks I have on grub-common and grub-pc,  because it updated both of them. 

Just a bit of clarification on package holds. In my experience, if you lock the version via Synaptic, the package won't update via Synaptic or Update Manager. However, for whatever reason, if you run "sudo apt-get upgrade" it *will* not honor Synaptic 'locks' and the package will be updated.

If the package is 'pinned' via the following command (or other methods in terminal), it will not be updated either by Synaptic, Update Manager or the 'apt-get update' method, even if the latter command is invoked using 'sudo'.

```
echo <packagename> hold | sudo dpkg --set-selections
```
Example: echo grub-pc hold | sudo dpkg --set-selections

---

If the package is 'pinned' via the following command (or other methods in terminal), it will not be updated either by Synaptic, Update Manager or the 'apt-get update' method, even if the latter command is invoked using 'sudo'.

```
echo <packagename> hold | sudo dpkg --set-selections
```Example: echo grub-pc hold | sudo dpkg --set-selections

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-19
We should probably create a bug for this or is there one already? The [community docs]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AptGet/Howto") indicate that the synaptic lock is equivalent:
> echo "<package_name> hold" | dpkg --set-selections
This command places the desired package on hold. [COLOR="Red"]This is the same as Synaptic's Package->Lock Version.[/COLOR]
 This command may have the unintended side effect of preventing upgrades to packages that depend on updated versions of the pinned package. apt-get dist-upgrade will override this, but will warn you first. ...

---

### Post by drs305 on 2011-04-19
> **bcbc said:**
> We should probably create a bug for this or is there one already? The [community docs]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/AptGet/Howto") indicate that the synaptic lock is equivalent:


It's been this way on my machines ever since I started using Ubuntu. I've always thought it strange that it worked that way. You should probably try to confirm that this is the way it works on your system.

I always use 2vcard for my tests, since it's a very small package. You can install the older version, lock via Synaptic or place a hold/pin via CLI, and then try to update by various methods since 5-3 is available in the repositories.

Here is a link to the 0.5-2 version (the .deb link is at the lower left).
[https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/2vcard/0.5-2/+buildjob/509470]("https://launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/2vcard/0.5-2/+buildjob/509470")

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-22
> **JimmyBnz said:**
> 
When I turn on my computer and try to boot to Ubuntu, i get the message:
(hd0,0): NTFS5: No wubildr
(hd0,1): NTFS5: No wubildr
(hd0,2): NTFS5: No wubildr

After which point it reboots.


Are you sure you get the last line (hd0,2): NTSF5: No wubildr? There is in fact a /wubildr there, and also if it doesn't find it there it should keep looking or give an error message.

Whatever you're using to boot your live environment doesn't support all the bootinfoscript commands and it doesn't seem to be mounting the root.disk. So probably you are failing doing it manually as well.

You should post the terminal output showing the commands you use to mount the root.disk so we can see the errors. Also, consider downloading and burning a current Ubuntu CD so we can see the full bootinfoscript.

---

### Post by ultranet on 2011-04-27
From comment 1:
> **Rubi1200 said:**
> 
These steps are applied **after** you have restored the Wubi install and are able to boot normally into both Windows and Ubuntu.

From the terminal:

```
sudo mv /boot/grub /boot/grubold
sudo mkdir /boot/grub
sudo cp /boot/grubold/grubenv /boot/grub
sudo update-grub
```[SIZE=2][B]
FIXED![/B][/SIZE]

I'd appreciate some feedback on whether both of the following statements are true.
A. Running the commands above is only required if a Wubi installation has already been corrupted (and need to be performed after recovery steps).
B. To prevent the bootloader issues on a **brand-new** Wubi installation, **only the *following* package-locking** steps are required.

> **Rubi1200 said:**
> 
prevent GRUB updates from breaking the Wubi install again:

Go to System > Administration > Synaptic Package Manager and select the grub-pc and grub-common packages. Click on Package > Lock Version.


---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-04-27
The answer is yes to both statements.

However, take a look at this post for some clarification on package holds:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10687475&postcount=373](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10687475&postcount=373)

hope this answers your questions. If not, feel free to ask for more clarification.

---

### Post by ultranet on 2011-04-27
> **Rubi1200 said:**
> 
However, take a look at this post for some clarification on package holds:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10687475&postcount=373](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10687475&postcount=373)


Thank you for mentioning this nuance.

Based on this, IMHO, in comment 1 it would be nice to indicate
```

echo grub-common hold | sudo dpkg --set-selections
echo grub-pc hold | sudo dpkg --set-selections

```
as an alternative that is preferred to Synaptic (or perhaps even as the only recommended approach to package version locking).

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-28
> **adamryan said:**
> Hey, can you guys help me out? I've tried and tried, and there just is no success with Wubi.

There is no more netbook-edition of Ubuntu. It's been merged with the standard Ubuntu desktop (as it uses Unity by default now).
So, someone forgot to remove it from Wubi. Just try with the normal Ubuntu option.

---

### Post by Jasonix on 2011-04-30
I am new here, but that's not important. I am installing natty and I was wondering if I need to worry about these issues (installing with wubi). And if I do, does the permanent fix work for natty?
Thank You

Edit:Thank you for the reply, and all I can say that is great!

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-30
> **Jasonix said:**
> I am new here, but that's not important. I am installing natty and I was wondering if I need to worry about these issues (installing with wubi). And if I do, does the permanent fix work for natty?
Thank You

It's not required for Natty. With Natty both problem #1 and #2 have been fixed. You don't have to lock grub-* either.

---

### Post by kansasnoob on 2011-04-30
I just wanted to pop in and thank bcbc for his valiant efforts in correcting the root issue of this problem. I've been following his test results here:

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898)

And also to say thanks again for helping me troubleshoot the Wubi via ubiquity option in Natty. Hopefully our combined efforts in regard to the latter issue will result in a good Natty experience for a lot of potential Ubuntu noobs :)

Without help from bcbc I wouldn't have been able to pull that off, we literally got the fix in about 14 hours before Natty released \\:D/

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-04-30
> **kansasnoob said:**
> I just wanted to pop in and thank bcbc for his valiant efforts in correcting the root issue of this problem. I've been following his test results here:

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898)

And also to say thanks again for helping me troubleshoot the Wubi via ubiquity option in Natty. Hopefully our combined efforts in regard to the latter issue will result in a good Natty experience for a lot of potential Ubuntu noobs :)

Without help from bcbc I wouldn't have been able to pull that off, we literally got the fix in about 14 hours before Natty released \\:D/
Thanks Kansasnoob, I try to make time for testing, when possible... I have to say your bug catch was awesome - especially for someone who never runs wubi. So well done for that. I'm hoping this Natty release will be the most 'boring' for Wubi (in terms of dealing with problems that is) - and your bug find just convinces me to increase testing during development (which is where it really counts)... So bring on Oneiric (!?) - I feel obligated to !? whenever I say that word ;)

---

### Post by miLl3niUm on 2011-05-01
Hello everyone, I got a headache from all this mess trying to dual boot Ubuntu/Windows 7 on my computer so I decided to ask for your help. Here is my problem:

Info:
I used Wubi and ubuntu-11.04-desktop-amd64.iso.
Main OS: Windows 7 Pro 64bit
Videocard: Nvidia GT 240


Problems (underlined) as they occured:

1) Installed Ubuntu within Windows, rebooted, saw Ubuntu loading screen (with dots) and then I get _graphics corruption_ (it's really bad I can't recognize anything in the screen).

Then I booted in recovery/failsafe graphic mode and I let the installation finish, then automatically rebooted.

2) Let it boot without pressing anything and instead of graphic corruption it _boots in terminal_.

Tried startx command but it says something relative to "x not found".
Then I found out I had to install proprietary Nvidia drivers so I:

3) Booted again in failsafe graphic mode (in recovery mode) and tried to install it but... _no internet connection_.

I tried "sudo ifconfig eth0 up" and I got: eth0: ERROR while getting interface flags: No such device

NOTE: If I let it boot itself (terminal) **I HAVE** internet connection (found out with wget and ping commands).

Theoretically I could install the drivers from terminal but I don't know what commands I have to use, any suggestion?

---

error: no such device: /ubuntu/install/boot/grub/grub.cfg.

Hi,
there appears to be damage to your Wubi install. This is what you can try from the LiveCD/USB:

```
sudo mkdir /media/win
sudo mount /dev/sdb1 /media/win
sudo fsck /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk
```

Let the last command complete and then try mounting the Wubi install:
```
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
```If everything is okay, save any important data and reboot taking out the CD.

If there are errors or problems, report them here please.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-01
> **miLl3niUm said:**
> I followed those steps and I managed to boot normally (but in lower-graphics mode) BUT STILL I don't have internet access to download the proprietary drivers. I think the problem is that it won't recognize my Ethernet interface.

I think that nvidia card is an optimus card (on demand) - which isn't supported. You basically have to use the intel gpu. So if you install the nvidia drivers it won't work.

Focus your investigation on why the intel gpu isn't working.

> **miLl3niUm said:**
> Hello everyone, I got a headache from all this mess trying to dual boot Ubuntu/Windows 7 on my computer so I decided to ask for your help. Here is my problem:

Info:
I used Wubi and ubuntu-11.04-desktop-amd64.iso.
Main OS: Windows 7 Pro 64bit
Videocard: Nvidia GT 240


Problems (underlined) as they occured:

1) Installed Ubuntu within Windows, rebooted, saw Ubuntu loading screen (with dots) and then I get _graphics corruption_ (it's really bad I can't recognize anything in the screen).

Then I booted in recovery/failsafe graphic mode and I let the installation finish, then automatically rebooted.

2) Let it boot without pressing anything and instead of graphic corruption it _boots in terminal_.

Tried startx command but it says something relative to "x not found".
Then I found out I had to install proprietary Nvidia drivers so I:

3) Booted again in failsafe graphic mode (in recovery mode) and tried to install it but... _no internet connection_.

I tried "sudo ifconfig eth0 up" and I got: eth0: ERROR while getting interface flags: No such device

NOTE: If I let it boot itself (terminal) **I HAVE** internet connection (found out with wget and ping commands).

Theoretically I could install the drivers from terminal but I don't know what commands I have to use, any suggestion?
You said here that your network is working except when you tried recovery mode. And since there is no graphics card driver you need to install, you should be able to boot normally.

Then check your logs to find out what's up with the graphics. Here is a bug, for example, [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/natty/+source/linux/+bug/600453](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/natty/+source/linux/+bug/600453) that might be relevant. Maybe not.

From a terminal you could process updates to see if there is a fix out as well:
```
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get upgrade

```

PS from the terminal run

```
sudo apt-get update
sudo service gdm start

```
and see what errors you get.

I doubt [2] is causing network problems - the nomodeset shouldn't have anything to with your internet.
> Adding the nomodeset parameter instructs the kernel to not load video drivers and use BIOS modes instead until X is loaded. 

Try: 
```
sudo service gdm restart
```

What's the brand/model of your computer?

### Post by Dobroslav on 2011-05-01
I've installed Ubuntu 11.04 via Wubi and I got this error

```
Try hd(0,0): NTFS5: No wubildr
```

After about 10 - 20s i got

```
Try hd(0,1)
```

and then system starts normally. How to fix this error?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-01
> **miLl3niUm said:**
> I don't know if the command should show up anything, but it didn't, just a short text stating that it has restarted/running and "process <number>" (probably the proc. ID).

Err what do you mean? My computer is a custom build desktop PC, Intel motherboard/cpu, nvidia graphics. Care for full specs?

How about just the motherboard and intel graphics spec.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-01
> **Dobroslav said:**
> I've installed Ubuntu 11.04 via Wubi and I got this error

```
Try hd(0,0): NTFS5: No wubildr
```

After about 10 - 20s i got

```
Try hd(0,1)
```

and then system starts normally. How to fix this error?

For some reason grub4dos (wubildr.mbr) is getting stuck figuring out whether /wubildr is on /dev/sda1 - I assume it's a boot or recovery partition and your C: is on /dev/sda2. You can probably resolve this by copying wubildr to the root of /dev/sda1. But there must be some reason why this is happening...

Wubildr.mbr is pretty dumb - it just looks on all partitions (in BIOS order) until it finds the wubildr file. So if you're looking for clues, and since it's not only used for Wubi, searching on grub4dos will probably give you more hits.

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-05-02
> **Antsquasher said:**
> Still getting problems. I assume after mounting the Wubi install, I should proceed with editing the grub.cfg file.

After rebooting, I got these:
init : ureadahead-other main process (419) terminated with status 4
udevd[443]: can not read '/etc/udev/rules.d/z80_user.rules'

Got a picture with a few more lines here: [http://imgur.com/6b2UA](http://imgur.com/6b2UA)
Does the boot process then continue or it stops completely?

First try rebooting and see if you can get in.

If yes, then do the following:

```
gksudo gedit /etc/udev/user.rules
```Add only the following symbol, #, to the empty file, then save and close.

Let me know if this works.

You should also read > man udevto understand what these things are and what they do.

---

### Post by Antsquasher on 2011-05-02
> **Rubi1200 said:**
> Does the boot process then continue or it stops completely?

First try rebooting and see if you can get in.

```Add only the following symbol, #, to the empty file, then save and close.

Let me know if this works.

You should also read to understand what these things are and what they do.

It just stops there. I can get into a recovery console through the selection menu in GRUB though. Is there anything I can do from there?

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-05-02
Go into Recovery and drop to a root prompt and then try this first:

```
 sudo mv /etc/udev/rules.d/z80_user.rules /etc/udev/rules.d/z80_user.rules.old
```

This makes a backup of the file just in case.

Then do this:

```
 sudo rm /etc/udev/rules.d/z80_user.rules
```

Then reboot and see if it helps.

---

### Post by miLl3niUm on 2011-05-03
> **bcbc said:**
> Yeah sorry... I'm not sure what's going on here :(

Have you tried installing 10.10 or maybe 10.04?

No point to that, as I just wanted to try the new version.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-03
> **miLl3niUm said:**
> No point to that, as I just wanted to try the new version.

OK... this is what I suggest. Your problems aren't related to Wubi - that's just the method you used to install. I'd create a separate thread detailing your hardware and the symptoms. You could also create a bug on the linux package and see if you get some hits.

The fact is that you're limited here on the Wubi megathread to those people who support Wubi - whereas you will get a broader audience outside of this thread. I also recommend you try booting from a live CD - and report on that as well - so you can clarify that the symptoms are to do with the linux kernel.

Good luck.

---

### Post by blurge on 2011-05-03
Wubi under Windows 7. One partition.

11.04 installed fine (had to use ACPI workaround), then automatically rebooted my machine. Upon this second boot, the only statement that prints: mem timing table length unknown: 14

I have reset all the settings in my BIOS to factory default, what the ehf is going on?

---

### Post by blurge on 2011-05-03
> **blurge said:**
> Wubi under Windows 7. One partition.

11.04 installed fine (had to use ACPI workaround), then automatically rebooted my machine. Upon this second boot, the only statement that prints: mem timing table length unknown: 14

I have reset all the settings in my BIOS to factory default, what the ehf is going on?

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=832375]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=832375")

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-03
> **blurge said:**
> Wubi under Windows 7. One partition.

11.04 installed fine (had to use ACPI workaround), then automatically rebooted my machine. Upon this second boot, the only statement that prints: mem timing table length unknown: 14

I have reset all the settings in my BIOS to factory default, what the ehf is going on?

Boot workarounds aren't persisted - so after installing you then also have override each subsequent boot until you can save the workaround or find a solution. [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1613132](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1613132)

PS those acpi workarounds are a bit of a sledgehammer that take out battery management and sometimes fan control so it's important to search on your model and find out how to get things running smoothly without.

---

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.35-28-generic" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(/dev/sdb,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 9280467180465BBB
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.35-28-generic root=UUID=9280467180465BBB loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash
    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.35-28-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.35-28-generic (recovery mode)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(/dev/sdb,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 9280467180465BBB
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)

    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.35-28-generic
}

### END /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Windows 7 (loader) (on /dev/sda1)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(/dev/sda,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root C0B62C4BB62C446E
    chainloader +1
}
menuentry "Microsoft Windows XP Professional (on /dev/sdb1)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(/dev/sdb,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 9280467180465BBB
    drivemap -s (hd0) ${root}
    chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###
if [ -f  $prefix/custom.cfg ]; then
  source $prefix/custom.cfg;
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###

You didn't complete the upgrade. For some reason it must have failed before it was complete - there is no initrd.img for 2.6.38-8 (the natty kernel) and it's not in grub.cfg either.

So... I'll see if I can come up with some suggestions. In the meantime, consider retrieving important data from the root.disk if you haven't already, just in case.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-04
Best I can find is this: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/SynapticHowto](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/SynapticHowto)
> What to do if an installation process fails and you find it is no longer possible to install or remove packages:
Open a Terminal and type the following commands, pressing the Return or Enter key after each (you may have to type in your password):
```
sudo dpkg --configure -a
sudo apt-get install -f
```

In your case you cannot boot any kernel right? And you can't boot in recovery either? If that's the case you probably have to chroot into your install and run the commands from there.

Before you do this - take a backup of the root.disk. Gives you the ability to reset and try an alternative later.

Boot live CD, connect to the internet, and drop to terminal CTRL+ALT+t:
```
sudo mkdir /media/win 
sudo mount /dev/sdb1 /media/win
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
for i in dev proc sys dev/pts; do sudo mount --bind /$i /mnt/$i; done
sudo cp /etc/resolv.conf /mnt/etc/resolv.conf
sudo chroot /mnt
dpkg-divert --local --rename --add /sbin/initctl
ln -s /bin/true /sbin/initctl
cp /boot/grub/grub.cfg /boot/grub/grub.cfg.save
#
# you're now root in your wubi install - attempt to fix
dpkg --configure -a
apt-get install -f
#
#exit chroot after repairs are complete
rm /sbin/initctl
dpkg-divert --local --rename --remove /sbin/initctl
exit
for i in dev/pts dev proc sys; do sudo umount /mnt/$i; done
sudo umount /mnt
sudo umount /dev/sdb1
```
I think you'll find some interesting stuff - maybe it will work, or maybe you'll have another issue to solve. But the basic framework is there to chroot in and you can research and experiment ways to fix the problem. Just avoid running update-grub in the chroot - it won't work (that's why I added the cp step to back it up - just in case). And that means - assuming it repairs itself - you might have to boot the Natty kernel manually the first time.

Anyway... good luck and post back if you have any questions. (Don't forget to backup first).

PS if you get an error on any of the above (excluding the dpkg and apt-get bits) don't continue blindly. Chances are there is a problem or I made a typo (I've checked more than once, but sometimes ... it happens).

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-05-04
Antsquasher,

that error is often seen when some of the following conditions are true:

The most common reasons for the error are improperly mounting the  necessary items (did you run the "for i in ..." command), or trying to  use 32-bit CD with 64-bit installation (or vice versa).

```
for i in /dev /dev/pts /proc /sys; do sudo mount -B $i /mnt/temp$i;  done
```

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-04
+1. I'd guess you're running a 32-bit Ubuntu CD and your wubi install is 64-bit.

If you mount the root.disk and run:
```
file /bin/bash
file /mnt/bin/bash
```
Then the architecture should match e.g. something like
> /mnt/bin/bash: ELF [COLOR="Red"]64-bit[/COLOR] LSB executable, x86-64, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked (uses shared libs), for GNU/Linux 2.6.15, stripped

or
> /bin/bash: ELF [COLOR="red"]32-bit[/COLOR] LSB executable, Intel 80386, version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked (uses shared libs), for GNU/Linux 2.6.15, stripped

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-04

> I tried to upgrade to natty from within my wubi install, and the process stopped halfway due to low disk space and my fonts in dialog boxes got destroyed (just seeing [][][][][][] type of boxes).
