That's the first time you mentioned running out of space during the upgrade (unless I missed it earlier). Which is important. 

Anyway if you backed up the root.disk before doing the chroot, you can redo the fix if you feel it's worth the effort. (At some point, you might consider a recovery and reinstall makes more sense, but I'll let you decide.) So, before you do that, increase the size of the root.disk. You can do this from a live CD using this technique: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10590851&postcount=2](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10590851&postcount=2)

Then you can try to repair it again.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-05
> **Antsquasher said:**
> Still getting the mostly blank grub :(
guess I should just hose the whole install then

If you have the initrd.img why don't you try booting manually...
Press 'c' to get to a grub command prompt. Then enter the following:
```
linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-8-generic root=/dev/sdb1 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro
initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.38-8-generic
boot

```

---

### Post by Antsquasher on 2011-05-05
> **bcbc said:**
> If you have the initrd.img why don't you try booting manually...
Press 'c' to get to a grub command prompt. Then enter the following:
```
linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-8-generic root=/dev/sdb1 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro
initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.38-8-generic
boot

```

IT WORKED! :D
So should I follow the rest of the instructions? namely:

> [SIZE=3]**Solution #3 (10.04.1 upgraded to 10.10):**[/SIZE]

This may leave the user with a GRUB prompt and/or the other symptoms previously mentioned.

There are 2 possible approaches here.

Either boot the LiveCD/USB and edit the grub.cfg file as before (Solution #1).

Alternatively, use the instructions provided by drs305 to boot into Ubuntu from the GRUB prompt:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php...7&postcount=33]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10264157&postcount=33")

The advantage of being able to boot from the GRUB prompt is that the user does not need to use a LiveCD/USB.

If booting from the GRUB prompt works and you are able to boot into  Ubuntu, follow the instructions in Solution #1 to edit the grub.cfg  file.

In this case, enter the following command in the terminal:
 	Code:
 	gksu gedit /boot/grub/grub.cfg 
Again, as above, remove the entries from the start of the file up to, _but not including_, the following menuentry 	Code:
 	 ### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ### 


along with this? 
 	Code:
 	sudo mv /boot/grub /boot/grubold sudo mkdir /boot/grub sudo cp /boot/grubold/grubenv /boot/grub sudo update-grub

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-05
> **Antsquasher said:**
> IT WORKED! :D
So should I follow the rest of the instructions? namely:


Wow! Awesome

So no, don't follow those instructions. They don't apply to 11.04 as the grub problems are fixed. You need to update grub to get your new menu. And you should probably update the wubildr as well... let's see:

WARNING (for others) - THESE INSTRUCTIONS ARE FOR THE VERSION OF GRUB SHOWN (11.04 only)
First check the version of grub.
```
sudo grub-install -v
```
It must say 1.99~rc1-13ubuntu3

Then update /wubildr (it will find it on /dev/sda1 but we have to specify /dev/loop0)
```
sudo grub-install /dev/loop0

```

Then update the grub.cfg (grub menu):
```
sudo update-grub
```

If it looks like it found your 2.6.38-8 kernel, then you should be ok to reboot. Maybe check and run updates beforehand as well.

Then let's see if it holds up...

Actually before you reboot, just make sure wubildr was updated or you might have a problem:
```
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt
ls -l /mnt/wubildr
sudo umount /mnt
```
Make sure the date and time show that wubildr was updated. or don't reboot yet and report back.

---

### Post by drs305 on 2011-05-08
> 
How can I make it install it when it has already downloaded the package. It appears with a error message that it can't install it.

What should I do? Please help me.


If you have the .deb file and know where it is, change to that folder and run this command:
```

sudo dpkg -i <packagename>
```

Example: sudo dpkg -i 2vcard.deb

If it doesn't install, you should get an error message in the terminal. Provide us with the error message and perhaps we can help.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-10
> **rikugo1 said:**
> I did that already, but it still gives me the message: "There is no disk in the drive. Please insert a disk into \Device\Harddisk1\DR1." If I click cancel, try again, or continue, it simply proceeds with DR2, DR3, and DR4. It appears to install correctly for the most part despite the message, but is not added to the boot menu.

There are two separate issues. One is this [https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/365881](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/365881)

The other is that there is either no entry in BCD (less likely) or that the Timeout is set to 0 (more likely).
Right click on Computer, properties, advanced, startup & recovery settings... and check that the timeout is 15. (Don't make it less)
If the Timeout is not zero, check the drop down box and see if Ubuntu is there (don't make it the default).

---

### Post by Rhysy on 2011-05-16
Awesome ! It worked a treat, many thank - especially for the quick response.

The only thing was that when I applied the permanent fix, namely...
> sudo mv /boot/grub /boot/grubold
sudo mkdir /boot/grub
sudo cp /boot/grubold/grubenv /boot/grub
sudo update-grub
... I only got a grub terminal on trying to boot up Ubuntu. So I re-applied the original fix, skipped the above steps and went straight to locking the grub packages, and now all is well.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-16
> **Rhysy said:**
> Awesome ! It worked a treat, many thank - especially for the quick response.

The only thing was that when I applied the permanent fix, namely...

... I only got a grub terminal on trying to boot up Ubuntu. So I re-applied the original fix, skipped the above steps and went straight to locking the grub packages, and now all is well.

You need to apply the permanent fix as well. Otherwise it will break again the next time you apply a kernel update (or any process that updates the grub.cfg file).

Maybe there was a typo? If you apply the permanent fix again and save the output from the terminal. (Copy and paste to a file) If it fails again, please post the output along with the grub.cfg file and I'll take a look.

PS when you're done there should only be 2 files in /boot/grub (grubenv and grub.cfg). If there are more, then there's some error - don't bother rebooting. Just post the terminal output here.

---

### Post by AeoliX on 2011-05-22
I've spent some time trying to figure this out, and feel like I'm oh-so-close, but still need a personal push to send me on my way.

Last year, I used Wubi to install 10.04 onto my Dell laptop.  This is the first time I have ever used Linux, so I'm really muddling my way around.  At any rate, all was going well until one day several months ago, I experienced Problem #2.  Before I found this excellent mega-thread, some other misguided soul told me to do this:

```
sudo -i
mount /dev/sda2 /mnt
grub-install --root_directory =/mnt/ /dev/sda
```This made matters much worse, since it then booted to grub instead of the windows bootloader, and grub was unable to find a bootable partition since my install was wubi. This really freaked me out.  I used the WinXP recovery disk to repair the Windows Bootloader, which it did.  So then I was back to Problem #2 again.

I then was directed to this mega-thread, and attempted Problem #2 Solution #1.  Upon reboot, when I select Ubuntu from the Windows Bootloader, it now goes to the grub menu, and won't boot Ubuntu.  I presume this has something to do with the fact that I used the above code and installed grub unnecessarily?  So now I'm looking for some assistance on how to un-do this whole grub debacle.

Anyway help for a newbie really wanting to be an Ubuntu user but having a hard time of it would be appreciated!

Thanks.

---

### Post by drs305 on 2011-05-22
> **AeoliX said:**
> I've spent some time trying to figure this out, and feel like I'm oh-so-close, but still need a personal push to send me on my way. So then I was back to Problem #2 again.

I then was directed to this mega-thread, and attempted Problem #2 Solution #1.  Upon reboot, when I select Ubuntu from the Windows Bootloader, it now goes to the grub menu, and won't boot Ubuntu.  I presume this has something to do with the fact that I used the above code and installed grub unnecessarily?  So now I'm looking for some assistance on how to un-do this whole grub debacle.

Anyway help for a newbie really wanting to be an Ubuntu user but having a hard time of it would be appreciated!

Thanks.

If you are still using Wubi, used Windows to repair your boot, and are accessing Ubuntu after seeing only the *Windows* bootloader, your problem isn't with the command you cited. 

Something else is going on. Are you still using 10.04 or have you attempted an upgrade.

We need more information, so please click [_this link_]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net"). It will take you to the Boot Info Script web page. Download and run the script.

Run the script as root - instructions are on the Boot Info Script page. There are additional instructions by *louieb* in a link on the left side of that page.

To post the contents of RESULTS.txt for us to review:
[LIST]
[*]Open a new post. 
[*]Click on the **#** icon in the post's menu bar. This will generate **[SIZE="2"][noparse]```
 
```[/noparse][/SIZE]** tags. 
[*]Paste the content between the code tags.
[/LIST]
RESULTS.txt shows us the status of your boot files and other system information which allow us to give you specific recommendations.

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-05-23
Hi AeoliX,
part of the problem is probably the fact that you have a GRUB file on the Wubi install:
> /boot/grub/core.img

Your Wubi install is also failing to mount:
> Mounting failed:   mount: wrong fs type, bad option, bad superblock on /dev/loop1

This is what you can try from the LiveCD to fix this:

```
sudo mkdir /media/win
sudo mount /dev/sda2 /media/win
sudo fsck /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk
```

Let the last command run until completed and then try and mount the root.disk:
```
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt

```

You should now be able to reboot and be back in Ubuntu.

If the fsck doesn't fix the problem, I would try and remove the grub files that I indicated before from the Windows side and then run the fsck from a LiveCD again.

Let us know how it goes please and if you need any other help.

---

### Post by Floatingclouds on 2011-05-23
Hey, directed Wubi to d drive, installed there, and when I reboot into ubuntu to finish install, will it overwrite c drive witch has vista on it?

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-05-23
> **Floatingclouds said:**
> Hey, directed Wubi to d drive, installed there, and when I reboot into ubuntu to finish install, will it overwrite c drive witch has vista on it?

Hi and welcome to the forums :-)

The short answer is no.

The Windows installer (Wubi) creates a file within the host system (Windows) and can be removed at any time as with any Windows program.

For more information see here:
[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide)

---

	linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-26-generic root=/dev/sda2 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash
	initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-26-generic

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-05-23
AeoliX,
the menu entries you see are normal. You can clean them up following this guide (make sure to keep the current kernel and one spare for troubleshooting purposes):
[http://www.howtogeek.com/howto/17787/clean-up-the-new-ubuntu-grub2-boot-menu/](http://www.howtogeek.com/howto/17787/clean-up-the-new-ubuntu-grub2-boot-menu/)

I am still concerned about the core.img file being there on Windows.

I would prefer that bcbc or drs305 comment on whether that file needs to be removed and whether you need to apply the Permanent Fix.

I definitely recommend that you lock the grub-* packages as mentioned in the main post.

### Post by AeoliX on 2011-05-23

Thanks Rubi1200.  I've now cleaned up my menu entries and locked down grub-pc and grub-common to prevent future problems.

Yeah, I was thinking that the core.img file shouldn't be there on Windows.  As I mentioned earlier, before I found this megathread another post convinced me that my problem was that the Windows Bootloader has wrestled control away from grub, and that I needed grub to take control again to be able to boot into Ubuntu.  I now realize that this advice is entirely inappropriate for a Wubi install, so I'm wondering if the initial steps I took erroneously put grub into Windows:
```
sudo -i
mount /dev/sda2 /mnt
grub-install --root_directory =/mnt/ /dev/sda
```
The above syntax may not be totally accurate - I can no longer find the post that lead me to attempt this solution, and I just jotted down notes on what I did after I realized it had broken the machine further.

At any rate, I'll see if bcbc or drs305 can shed more light on the issue.

Thanks to all of you - invaluable help for a newbie trying to embrace Ubuntu!

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-23
> **AeoliX said:**
> Thanks Rubi1200.  I've now cleaned up my menu entries and locked down grub-pc and grub-common to prevent future problems.

...

At any rate, I'll see if bcbc or drs305 can shed more light on the issue.

Thanks to all of you - invaluable help for a newbie trying to embrace Ubuntu!

Yes you will need to apply the Permanent Fix or else you will have problems booting. 

Having that /boot/grub/core.img can interfere with Vista/7 booting sometimes if there is a case conflict (e.g /Boot and /boot directories as Windows isn't case-sensitive), but I don't think it will hurt XP. However, it isn't helping anything so you might as well delete it.

---

### Post by AeoliX on 2011-05-23
> **bcbc said:**
> Yes you will need to apply the Permanent Fix or else you will have problems booting. 

Having that /boot/grub/core.img can interfere with Vista/7 booting sometimes if there is a case conflict (e.g /Boot and /boot directories as Windows isn't case-sensitive), but I don't think it will hurt XP. However, it isn't helping anything so you might as well delete it.

Thanks bcbc.  Before I do the Permanent Fix - I have already locked down grub-pc and grub-common.  Do I need to unlock them before doing this fix, or is it OK that they remain locked for this?
```
sudo mv /boot/grub /boot/grubold
sudo mkdir /boot/grub
sudo cp /boot/grubold/grubenv /boot/grub
sudo update-grub
```


Lastly, in terms of deleting that /boot/grub/core.img file, where exactly is it found?  I don't see it when I go into the /boot/grub directory in the ubuntu file system - is it in the Windows XP file system somehow?  Seemed like it's best to get rid of it - just want to make sure I delete the right thing...

Thanks.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-23
AeoliX, 
you don't need to unlock grub-pc before doing the permanent fix. Locking them just stops Update manager from prompting you when future grub updates are released (and updating grub's packages is what caused the problem in the first place).

If you've booted Ubuntu, then your windows host is mounted under /host so you would find core.img at /host/boot/grub/core.img or you could just boot windows and delete folder C:\boot.

---

### Post by AeoliX on 2011-05-24
> **bcbc said:**
> AeoliX, 
you don't need to unlock grub-pc before doing the permanent fix. Locking them just stops Update manager from prompting you when future grub updates are released (and updating grub's packages is what caused the problem in the first place).

If you've booted Ubuntu, then your windows host is mounted under /host so you would find core.img at /host/boot/grub/core.img or you could just boot windows and delete folder C:\boot.

Thanks bcbc.  Everything is all taken care of now and I'm back to 100%.  You guys rock - thanks so much for the help!

### Post by Scunnered on 2011-05-29
Currently I dual boot Lucid and Windows 7 on my system and everything works well.  Having received a Ubuntu 11.04 DVD from a magazine I had a look at it but felt that I needed to study matters more before I fully adopt it.

I thought that one way of investigating 11.04 would be to boot Windows 7 and to download from Ubuntu.com using the **Run it with Windows** option.  Having done that I then rebooted the system and selected the Windows option. I had expected to be given an option to select 7 or 11.04 but nothing displayed. Windows 7 booted and on checking everywhere the only reference to Ubuntu was in the add/remove section.

Obviously something is wrong.  No doubt my approach to the problem could be the cause.  I don't want at the moment to move away from the LTS version but as I anticipate that the next LTS will have all the new Unity desktop I would like to be familiar with it prior to adopting the new LTS.

Can you offer guidance on how I should things to achieve my aims.

---

### Post by drs305 on 2011-05-29
Sounds like you have a reasonable plan. Either the entire installation has problems or more likely Grub2 didn't get fully installed. 

The best way for us to see what is happening is to run the boot info script will running Ubuntu from a LiveCD (or from Lucid, which would be more convenient). Click on the "BIS" link in my signature line, follow the instructions, and post the contents of RESULTS.txt.

---

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by /usr/sbin/grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ ${prev_saved_entry} ]; then
  set saved_entry=${prev_saved_entry}
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z ${boot_once} ]; then
    saved_entry=${chosen}
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n ${have_grubenv} ]; then if [ -z ${boot_once} ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}
insmod ext2
set root='(hd0,5)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set a39a9c11-2dbc-42c9-927b-19b5c21c261f
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=640x480
  insmod gfxterm
  insmod vbe
  if terminal_output gfxterm ; then true ; else
    # For backward compatibility with versions of terminal.mod that don't
    # understand terminal_output
    terminal gfxterm
  fi
fi
insmod ext2
set root='(hd0,5)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set a39a9c11-2dbc-42c9-927b-19b5c21c261f
set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
set lang=en
insmod gettext
if [ ${recordfail} = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.32-31-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
	recordfail
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,5)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set a39a9c11-2dbc-42c9-927b-19b5c21c261f

}

}

	recordfail
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,5)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set a39a9c11-2dbc-42c9-927b-19b5c21c261f
	echo	'Loading Linux 2.6.32-30-generic ...'
	linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-30-generic root=UUID=a39a9c11-2dbc-42c9-927b-19b5c21c261f ro single 
	echo	'Loading initial ramdisk ...'
	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-30-generic
}
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.32-29-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
	recordfail
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,5)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set a39a9c11-2dbc-42c9-927b-19b5c21c261f
	linux	/boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-29-generic root=UUID=a39a9c11-2dbc-42c9-927b-19b5c21c261f ro   quiet splash
	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-29-generic
}

	recordfail
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,5)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set a39a9c11-2dbc-42c9-927b-19b5c21c261f

	initrd	/boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-29-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+)" {
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,5)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set a39a9c11-2dbc-42c9-927b-19b5c21c261f
	linux16	/boot/memtest86+.bin
}
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+, serial console 115200)" {
	insmod ext2
	set root='(hd0,5)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set a39a9c11-2dbc-42c9-927b-19b5c21c261f
	linux16	/boot/memtest86+.bin console=ttyS0,115200n8
}
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Windows 7 (loader) (on /dev/sda1)" {
	insmod ntfs
	set root='(hd0,1)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 78badbf4badbacbe
	chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### Post by drs305 on 2011-05-29
Scunnered,

What I notice is that there is no mention of the /ubuntu/disks/root.disk in your Windows partition. This file is essentially the Wubi installation. So either the file has gone missing or the installation didn't complete.

The Wubi gurus will have to step in here to help you figure out the next step (if there is something short of a Wubi reinstall). They are a knowledgeable bunch and I expect someone will chime in shortly.

---

### Post by Scunnered on 2011-05-29
**drs305**

Many thanks for your reply.  

I will patiently await further advice from the Wubi gurus who no doubt will lead me to the promised land.

Again my thanks for all your kind assistance

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-29
Scunnered,
I've never seen a case where the disks folder is missing directly after installing. Could be a first...
But I've noticed some strange results with the latest version of bootinfoscript. I'd try version 0.55 instead - just see if it's the same for yourself. Or just go to C:\ubuntu and see if there is a subdirectory "disks" that contains root.disk and swap.disk.

Normally when windows boots directly it's due to an issue getting the boot entry setup in the BCD. You can right click on "Computer", Properties, Advanced system settings, Startup and Recovery settings... and make sure that Ubuntu is listed under the "Default operating system dropdown" (don't set it as the default). Then check the "Time to display list of operating systems" and make sure it is 15 or 30.

Hopefully it's just the timeout setting. 
If you really do have no root.disk you'd have to reinstall. 
If there's no BCD entry for Ubuntu it's possible to add it.

---

>>retval=1
>>stderr=The boot configuration data store could not be opened.
The system cannot find the file specified.

>>stdout=

For more information, please see the log file:
c:\users\ian\appdata\local\temp\wubi-11.04-rev211.log

Had another look in the C drive but was totally unable to follow the file path. Unable to find appdata and after that things go to pot.

I am now at a total loss and hope that you can offer further guidance otherwise I shall resort to drink.

Many thanks for your advice.

I attempted to follow your instruction only to end up a creek without a paddle. No matter what I tried I could not find what you asked.

Gave up on that, removed Ubuntu from Windows7 by using add/remove programmes.

Went back to Ubuntu.com and tried again.

At the end of the sequence the following was displayed :

An error occurred:

Error executing command
>>commend=c:\windows\sysnative\bcdedit.exe/create /d Ubuntu /application bootselector
>>retval=1
>>stderr=The boot configuration data store could not be opened.
The system cannot find the file specified.

>>stdout=

For more information, please see the log file:
c:\users\ian\appdata\local\temp\wubi-11.04-rev211.log

Had another look in the C drive but was totally unable to follow the file path. Unable to find appdata and after that things go to pot.

I am now at a total loss and hope that you can offer further guidance otherwise I shall resort to drink.

Thanking you in anticipation
I'm not sure why wubi would have problems locating or updating the BCD - when you didn't get this error the first time you ran it. That is puzzling.

You can locate the log file in the %temp% directory. If you try to browse for it, you'll get stuck as it's defaulted to be a hidden directory. But it seems pretty clear that it cannot update the BCD for some reason.

Your further assistance will be most appreciated.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-30
Scunnered,

Open up Windows explorer, click on the address bar and enter **%temp%**
That takes you to your temp folder. The log file for 11.04 is called wubi-11.04-rev211.log

You can do this without getting hidden files/folders to show. But if you want to show hidden folders/files on Win7, open Windows explorer, hit ALT+t to get to the Tools menu, select Folder options, select View tab, and check "Show hidden files, folders and drives"

---

I had a look at the wubi file earlier but as it did not show as .log I deleted it and started everything fresh once again only to be confronted with the error message that I posted earlier.

I can't get any further forward than that point so I hope that the details provided can throw some light on the subject and that I can get wubi to work as expected.

I hope this assists

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-30
Scunnered,

I'm not really familiar with the ins and outs of the BCD store. I only recently upgraded my XP pc to Windows 7. However, here is a good article about it: [http://www.multibooters.co.uk/bootmgr.html](http://www.multibooters.co.uk/bootmgr.html)

Basically, I believe the solution would be to use bcdedit to identify the BCD store. Currently it appears that this is not set, and this is causing the bcdedit command to fail.

---

### Post by Scunnered on 2011-05-31
**bcbc**

If my hair was not grey before I had a look at the link provided then it sure is now.  My level of competence is so far below that described there that I could see serious problems it I set foot down that road.

I do not want to cause you any further grief by botching matters and expecting you to come to my rescue.  I will therefore have a look at how I may approach testing of 11.04 from another way.

My sincere thanks to you and everyone else who attempted to assist.

### Post by jaezcurra on 2011-05-31
Hi, I am using Wubi 10.04 on my Dell XPS 630i under Vista SP2 just because I don't know how to dual boot it as I have a NVIDIA RAID controller (see attachment with the result of doing a "sudo lshw") and each time I have tried to use a Live CD it could not even boot.  The only time I succeeded to boot from a CD was the OpenSuse 11.3 DVD (it's not live).
I am happy with Wubi, but I have only 5 Gb free in my 30Gb Wubi Ubuntu disk, as my Ubuntu One account grows and grows ... (if only Wubi could manage more than 30 Gb!)
Any suggestion regarding the chance of getting Ubuntu installed in my computer?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-31
> **Scunnered said:**
> **bcbc**

If my hair was not grey before I had a look at the link provided then it sure is now.  My level of competence is so far below that described there that I could see serious problems it I set foot down that road.

I do not want to cause you any further grief by botching matters and expecting you to come to my rescue.  I will therefore have a look at how I may approach testing of 11.04 from another way.

My sincere thanks to you and everyone else who attempted to assist.

Sorry... I feel it should be something simple. But I too an unfamiliar with this - and since my other computer died recently I don't have a backup and am loathe to experiment too much with my current one (which pays the bills).

One technique I have used is to just install to an external hard drive. I have one with about 6 separate Ubuntu installations. If you're just trying it out, that is a reasonably cheap method provided you take care to install grub2 to the External, and not the internal which is seems to do by default.
Also you can run 11.04 from a live USB - it's much slower than Wubi to boot, but performs reasonably just to have a look - and much faster than a live CD (plus allows some persistence).

If I find some update on how to fix bcdedit I'll let you know.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-31
> **jaezcurra said:**
> Hi, I am using Wubi 10.04 on my Dell XPS 630i under Vista SP2 just because I don't know how to dual boot it as I have a NVIDIA RAID controller (see attachment with the result of doing a "sudo lshw") and each time I have tried to use a Live CD it could not even boot.  The only time I succeeded to boot from a CD was the OpenSuse 11.3 DVD (it's not live).
I am happy with Wubi, but I have only 5 Gb free in my 30Gb Wubi Ubuntu disk, as my Ubuntu One account grows and grows ... (if only Wubi could manage more than 30 Gb!)
Any suggestion regarding the chance of getting Ubuntu installed in my computer?
If you have wubi running, then installing Ubuntu direct should work too. Your problems booting a live CD most likely have to do with the CD - make sure you burn it at the slowest speed and check the md5sum. See step 2 [here]("http://www.ubuntu.com/download/ubuntu/download") for instructions.

---

### Post by jaezcurra on 2011-05-31
> **bcbc said:**
> If you have wubi running, then installing Ubuntu direct should work too. Your problems booting a live CD most likely have to do with the CD - make sure you burn it at the slowest speed and check the md5sum. See step 2 [here]("http://www.ubuntu.com/download/ubuntu/download") for instructions.

Actually, the problem is that Ubuntu live does not see my RAID structure, as I showed in my lshw file.  I need to manage the RAID disks with the Ubuntu installer, and I cannot.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-31
> **jaezcurra said:**
> Actually, the problem is that Ubuntu live does not see my RAID structure, as I showed in my lshw file.  I need to manage the RAID disks with the Ubuntu installer, and I cannot.
Okay, I took you literally when you said you couldn't even boot from the live CD. 

So I'm curious how Wubi manages to run if Ubuntu cannot see your disks? Did you install it to an external drive? Why don't you download and run the [bootinfoscript]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/") - that should be easier to figure out what's going on.

---

Wish me luck.

Again thanks

So I'm curious how Wubi manages to run if Ubuntu cannot see your disks? Did you install it to an external drive? Why don't you download and run the [bootinfoscript]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/") - that should be easier to figure out what's going on.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by /usr/sbin/grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ ${prev_saved_entry} ]; then
  set saved_entry=${prev_saved_entry}
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z ${boot_once} ]; then
    saved_entry=${chosen}
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n ${have_grubenv} ]; then if [ -z ${boot_once} ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}
insmod ntfs
set root='(hd3,3)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set e03e8c863e8c5804
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=640x480
  insmod gfxterm
  insmod vbe
  if terminal_output gfxterm ; then true ; else
    # For backward compatibility with versions of terminal.mod that don't
    # understand terminal_output
    terminal gfxterm
  fi
fi
insmod ntfs
set root='(hd3,3)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set e03e8c863e8c5804
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
set lang=
insmod gettext
if [ ${recordfail} = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.32-32-generic" {
	insmod ntfs
	set root='(hd3,3)'
	search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set e03e8c863e8c5804
	loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
	set root=(loop0)
	linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-32-generic root=/dev/mapper/nvidia_bcidhdja3 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash noapic nolapic
	initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.32-32-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.32-32-generic (recovery mode)" {

### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### Post by bcbc on 2011-05-31
> **jaezcurra said:**
> Well, I made a USBmultiboot with Multisystem app and a cd-boot-liveusb.iso CD and so I could try a bit 11.04 live and check with GParted and the Disk Utility what I told you about Ubuntu not seeing my RAID structure.  But this is the first time I succeed booting from a live device!

Anyway, back to my 10.04 Wubi, here is my Results.txt:
...

As the Wubi installation is within Vista, it is a matter of Windows to manage the RAID disks, I guess.

Actually the same installer is used for both Wubi as well as normal Ubuntu (it's called ubiquity). Wubi just does some additional work to create the virtual disk, setup a custom boot mechanism, and create some 'preseed' instructions so that Ubiquity doesn't prompt you during the install process. 
In other words, if you can install Wubi on a RAID then you should be able to install Ubuntu.

Interestingly you have two /boot directories as well:
> /boot/grub/grub.cfg /Boot/BCD  
This has been reported to cause problems with Windows.
Also your Wubi grub.cfg looks like it could cause the Problem #2 described in the first post of this thread - so you might want to apply the "Permanent fix" there before it stops booting.

---

### Post by jaezcurra on 2011-06-01
> Actually the same installer is used for both Wubi as well as normal Ubuntu (it's called ubiquity). Wubi just does some additional work to create the virtual disk, setup a custom boot mechanism, and create some 'preseed' instructions so that Ubiquity doesn't prompt you during the install process. 
In other words, if you can install Wubi on a RAID then you should be able to install Ubuntu.

So, why, when being with the 11.04 live USB, Ubuntu did not seem to see the RAID?  Is there any instruction from within 11.04 live that could offer a picture of what it actually sees (I mean the RAID)?

> Interestingly you have two /boot directories as well:
/boot/grub/grub.cfg /Boot/BCD
This has been reported to cause problems with Windows.
Also your Wubi grub.cfg looks like it could cause the Problem #2 described in the first post of this thread - so you might want to apply the "Permanent fix" there before it stops booting.

I am in 10.04.2 Wubi, so I guess I should apply first the Solution #1 and then the Permanent Fix; as Solutions #2 and #3 seem to be for upgrades to 10.10.
I suppose it is capital to follow the right procedure to have a 100% clean Ubuntu boot.  In my case, I always succeed in booting Ubuntu (in fact, I nearly never boot Windows), though I must admit that it is not a very clean one.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-06-01
> **jaezcurra said:**
> So, why, when being with the 11.04 live USB, Ubuntu did not seem to see the RAID?  Is there any instruction from within 11.04 live that could offer a picture of what it actually sees (I mean the RAID)?

Well the bootinfoscript seemed to identify the raid correctly. That's running under Ubuntu. Wubi is the same as Ubuntu: it boots natively (the main difference being the virtual disk) - and Wubi could not run if Ubuntu didn't see the root.disk on the raid.

So, I'm no raid expert - but it looks like Ubuntu can see it fine.

> **jaezcurra said:**
> 
I am in 10.04.2 Wubi, so I guess I should apply first the Solution #1 and then the Permanent Fix; as Solutions #2 and #3 seem to be for upgrades to 10.10.
I suppose it is capital to follow the right procedure to have a 100% clean Ubuntu boot.  In my case, I always succeed in booting Ubuntu (in fact, I nearly never boot Windows), though I must admit that it is not a very clean one.
Solution #1 (and 2 and 3) are only when you cannot boot the Wubi install. Since you can boot it, you can proceed to the Permanent Fix.

---

### Post by jaezcurra on 2011-06-01
> Well the bootinfoscript seemed to identify the raid correctly. That's running under Ubuntu. Wubi is the same as Ubuntu: it boots natively (the main difference being the virtual disk) - and Wubi could not run if Ubuntu didn't see the root.disk on the raid.

So, I'm no raid expert - but it looks like Ubuntu can see it fine.

So, I guess that I'll first go and "clean" my Vista disks from within Vista itself, by allocating space for a new partition (the native Linux one(s): swap + boot + home) and then proceed to install Ubuntu from the USB live.

> Solution #1 (and 2 and 3) are only when you cannot boot the Wubi install. Since you can boot it, you can proceed to the Permanent Fix.

OK.  Thanks :)

 Thanks in advance.

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-06-04
ssreddy555,
Wubi uses grub4dos to search for the Wubi partition and boots it when found. Although this is perhaps annoying it is normal. I am not aware of a direct fix for this.

---

### Post by ssreddy555 on 2011-06-04
Thank u. 

I was rearranging partitions to migrate ubuntu to partition from WUBI. In the process, somehow, it got corrected itself. It is now going directly to GRUB, without delay after we choose the OS.

Coming to the issue of migration, I created sda 5 of about 45GB & a small sda 6 of about 1.5 GB for swap as detailed in this thread:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354). This is in addition to sda 1, 2 & 3, where Windows 7 is installed. 

When I tried to execute the command for "migration with swap file" in the Terminal, as detailed in the above thread,It says sda 6 is not a swap partition. What does it mean? All the sda's are NTFS formatted.

> 
error: no such device /ubuntu/disk/root.disk
error: no such device /ubuntu/install/boot/grub/grub.cfg
error: no such device /grub.cfg

(REST OF THE ERROR MESSAGE IS NOT EXACT, AND IS FROM MEMORY)
Cannot boot from Ubuntu disk image.
Please make sure that you have not removed the Ubuntu installation.
This could possibly happening due to a corrupted Windows NTFS filesystem, and can be fixed by running 'chkdsk /r' in Windows.

 
NOTE: GRUB2 quickly displayed the error message and went into command line mode, so I couldn't grasp the message. To reproduce the above, I typed in 'configfile /wubildr.cfg'.
 
Now, I tried running both: 'chkdsk /r' and 'chkdsk G:' but it didn't work. Then I read somewhere that perhaps they copy the files in a hidden folder 'C:\found.000\'. However, this folder didn't exist.
 
Now, I have some very important files in the Ubuntu installation, and I want it back. Please someone help me? :(
 
Regards,
Shikhin

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-06-10
Hi,
Here is what you need to do:

Move the root.disk and swap.disk files back to sda3 (G drive I assume) and place them in their folders with the following structure:

\ubuntu\disks\root.disk
\ubuntu\disks\swap.disk 

Next, make a backup copy of the root.disk to an external medium such as a USB stick in case this happens again.

Finally, reboot and see if everything is normal again.

You may need to apply solution # 1 to problem # 2 from the main post (but first see if Ubuntu boots normally).

If not, run the boot script again and post it here please.

Thanks.

---

### Post by pommu on 2011-06-10
Hi
Just wanted to update the forum. Solution 1 worked for the problem 2 for me.

I have 10.04 of wubi. Is it worth moving to the latest ubuntu (11.xx)
If so how can do it without losing my data in 10.04

thanks
Karthik

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-06-10
> **pommu said:**
> Hi
Just wanted to update the forum. Solution 1 worked for the problem 2 for me.

I have 10.04 of wubi. Is it worth moving to the latest ubuntu (11.xx)
If so how can do it without losing my data in 10.04

thanks
Karthik

You'd have to upgrade twice. First to 10.10. Then upgrade to 11.04. There are potential problems you'll face:
1. Wubi booting problems after upgrading to 10.10 (see post #1)
2. General upgrade errors... some users have problems with upgrades for numerous reasons (hardware, software, custom config)
3. Space issues. Upgrades require a certain amount of space and despite the upgrade process checking beforehand, some users have run out of space (so make sure you have plenty available).

In general, it is advisable to backup your files (especially the \ubuntu\disks\root.disk and C:\wubildr) before upgrading.

You might also consider moving to a regular dual boot. You won't be immune from upgrade issues, but it's generally more stable.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-06-16
> **fantasy99 said:**
> i am having a problem as mentioned in the mega thread 
[B]Problem #1:
You have Wubi installed and are unable to boot either Ubuntu or Windows.[/B]

so what i hv did was:
i installed ubuntu 11.04 using wubi and partitioning my e: giving 17 gb to it.(But i hv my windows xp in my c: )
after installing ubuntu i did my coding and surfed the net for few hours;then i restarted my pc in xp did some work and then again restarted in ubuntu.

**problem:**
but when i shutdown my pc and turned it on a few hours later i saw was a message **"This is not a bootable disk. Please insert a bootable floppy and press any key to try again..."**:(:(
and when i press a key it says **"No bootable device -- insert boot disk and press any key"** and then keep on giving similar messages.
The solution provided is not helping me as i dont have a recovery disk and not able to understand the terminal commands and things as i am not able to open anything but just this screen pops out whenever i turn on my pc.:confused:

so any type of help will be appreciated
ps:i am newbie to linux
This message indicates drive failure - or something has removed the boot flag and the bootloader cannot locate a bootable OS.

Please try booting from an Ubuntu CD/USB in live mode (Try it without installing) and run the [bootinfoscript]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/"). Then we can see if the drive has failed or whether something is preventing the bootloader from finding the OS.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-06-16
> **knight night said:**
> i have a problem when i installed ubuntu
via usb drive and cd .. and i try it in windows xp and windows 7
it's a same problem .. but this is the error message



This is likely caused by a bad CD. To test, boot from the CD (press a key when you see the little keyboard icon, and run the "check cd for errors" option).
Or instead, download the ISO yourself, place in the same folder as wubi.exe and run wubi from there. (Make sure you have the matching releases between wubi and the desktop cd iso as described [here]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How do I install Wubi on a machine with no Internet connection?")).

---

### Post by Atomix26 on 2011-06-16
I tried installing the newest version of Wubi onto a removable hard disk, partitioned with 30 GB(separate from what windows is on), and I am getting an error when trying to start Ubuntu, saying that wubildr.mbr is missing or corrupt. Windows 7 works fine, but I have no idea what to do.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-06-16
> **Atomix26 said:**
> I tried installing the newest version of Wubi onto a removable hard disk, partitioned with 30 GB(separate from what windows is on), and I am getting an error when trying to start Ubuntu, saying that wubildr.mbr is missing or corrupt. Windows 7 works fine, but I have no idea what to do.

Sometimes you can see removable media within an OS - in this case Windows, and so you can install on it - but in order to boot Wubi the media has to be visible to BIOS. It sounds like it isn't - that would explain the error you are getting.

You could just install Wubi to C: instead. There's not much to be gained by installing Wubi on a different drive unless you are short on space. 

If you're sure that your bios can see the removable hard disk (in other words you can go into BIOS and select to boot from it) then there might be another problem.

---

### Post by Dr Noam on 2011-06-17
Hi, I think my troubles might be under this category. My WUBI seems to work fine, but now will not connect to the internet - wired or wireless - whereas booting up with Windows (as I have now) connects with no problem. Any suggestions? If I re-install WUBI, how do I save all my existing files currently under it?

Apologies if this is the wrong thread!!

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-06-17
> **Dr Noam said:**
> Hi, I think my troubles might be under this category. My WUBI seems to work fine, but now will not connect to the internet - wired or wireless - whereas booting up with Windows (as I have now) connects with no problem. Any suggestions? If I re-install WUBI, how do I save all my existing files currently under it?

Apologies if this is the wrong thread!!

Don't reinstall. This won't fix a networking issue - unless it's not working because you broke it (you say: "but **now** will not connect" - does that mean it used to connect but something happened and now it no longer connects? or that it never connected?).

Networking issues are unrelated to Wubi - it's generally best to go to the [Networking & wireless subforum]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=336") and read the stickies to troubleshoot it yourself. Then if you still are having issues, create a new thread there with the information they requested and someone with networking expertise will be able to help you better.

---

### Post by justinmd982 on 2011-06-28
> **Rubi1200 said:**
> [CENTER][COLOR=Black][SIZE=6]WUBI MEGATHREAD[/SIZE][/COLOR]
[SIZE=2][COLOR=Black](or, how to fix those Wubi installation breakages without going crazy)[/COLOR][/SIZE][COLOR=Black]

[/COLOR][LEFT][COLOR=Black]May 3, 2011
[/COLOR][COLOR=Black]EDIT: [COLOR=Red]Please do NOT post in this thread if you are not using Wubi.[/COLOR] 
If you have Ubuntu installed **inside** Windows you are using Wubi. Solutions mentioned here are Wubi specific. Fixes for a normal install could potentially break Wubi and we do not wish to confuse users, especially those new to the forums. If you are unsure whether you are using Wubi or not, open a new thread in the Installation & Upgrades forum. 
We would also like to request that you start your own thread if you are using Wubi but have an issue not related directly to the fixes posted here (for example, networking problems etc.). We will see it and try and help as best we can.

[/COLOR][COLOR=Black]May 3, 2011
[/COLOR][COLOR=Black]_New information_: With the release of 11.04, it appears that the major issues affecting Wubi installs are over. However, please refer to this thread if you run into problems and post with relevant information such as the boot info script (mentioned below). 
[/COLOR][/LEFT]
 [/CENTER]

I would like to preface this thread with the following comments:

First and foremost, my utmost thanks and gratitude to forum moderator drs305 and long-standing member bcbc for their support, suggestions, feedback, and tireless efforts on behalf of Wubi, as well as regular Ubuntu, users on these forums. 

This guide is based entirely on their troubleshooting and problem solving endeavors.

I am by no means a Wubi expert. Everything I know comes from watching drs305 and bcbc hard at work.

Second, please read ALL the information before posting. If you have tried one of the solutions and it did not work or you are unable to proceed because you did not understand something, feel free to post. 

In the interest of keeping this thread "clean," I ask that you follow this simple procedure before cluttering up the thread. It will also make life easier for those responding who want to help solve problems.

Third, kudos to the developers of Wubi.

Finally, I ask forum members who are not Wubi users to please read this information carefully before offering advice in a post. 

Unfortunately, there have been instances where even experienced members have advised a thread starter to reinstall Wubi, GRUB, or suggested numerous other "fixes" which are either unnecessary or even counter-productive.

[SIZE=3]**Introduction:**[/SIZE]

Wubi, which stands for Windows-based Ubuntu Installer, is a convenient and easy way to try another operating system without the hassle of partitioning or other issues that sometimes occur when attempting to run more than one operating system.
[Wikipedia]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wubi_%28Ubuntu_installer%29%20wikipedia")

Wubi uses a virtual disk mounted within the host system (Windows), allowing users to access the full functionality of Ubuntu after the computer starts and the operating system has been chosen from the available menu.
[Wubi Guide]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide")

However, Wubi was never intended as anything other than a means to try a Linux operating system and, as such, it is usually recommended to install Ubuntu to a dedicated partition on the hard-drive.
[Agostino Russo (Wubi developer)]("http://howsoftwareisbuilt.com/2009/03/12/interview-with-agostino-russo-wubi-ubuntu/")

[SIZE=3]**Things You May Need for Troubleshooting:**[/SIZE]

1. an Ubuntu LiveCD or LiveUSB 
(the download link and instructions on how to create a CD or USB can be found [here]("http://www.ubuntu.com/desktop/get-ubuntu/download"))

2. a Windows installation/recovery disk

3. results of the [boot info script]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/")

[SIZE=3]**A Word of Caution:**[/SIZE]

Do _NOT_ try and reinstall GRUB or use boot managers such as BURG. Wubi installs cannot be booted directly from the MBR of the drive.

[SIZE=3]**The Core Issue:**[/SIZE]

There have been updates for the GRUB bootloader which have been causing problems with Wubi installs. These updates have been known to cause problems on Ubuntu versions from 9.10 onwards including upgrades to the latest 10.10 version.

(At the time of writing, a fresh install of 10.10 is not affected since there are currently no GRUB updates for this version)

I am not going to go into extended detail about the specifics of the problem since I assume most users just want the fix.

However, for those who wish to know more, the following bug reports make for interesting reading:
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/610898)
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/613288](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/613288)
[https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/653134](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/653134)

(I do not know if this also affects older Wubi installs, but I am assuming that most users will have one of the more recent versions)

The issues can be broken down into two main areas, each with its own fix or solutions.

[SIZE=3]**Problem #1:**[/SIZE]

You have Wubi installed and are unable to boot either Ubuntu or Windows.

[SIZE=3]**Possible Cause:**[/SIZE]

A late July 2010 GRUB2 update is causing a "no such disk" error (in some cases it may have a "no such device" error followed by a long string of numbers) for some users of Wubi, resulting in an unbootable system. 

If the system does not display the original Windows menu, the most likely cause of the failure is that GRUB2 was installed in the MBR and/or on the Windows partition.

[SIZE=3]**Solution #1:**[/SIZE]

Restore the Windows bootloader with the relevant Windows Recovery CD.
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1014708](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1014708)

[SIZE=3]**Solution #2:**[/SIZE]

If you do not have a Windows CD, or are unable to get hold of one, you can use an Ubuntu CD/USB to install a Windows-like bootloader.

From a terminal on the LiveCD/USB (Applications > Accessories > Terminal), run the following commands:

```
sudo apt-get install lilo
sudo lilo -M /dev/sd**[COLOR=Red]a[/COLOR]** mbr
```This assumes that sda is the Windows drive. If not, change this to reflect the actual drive e.g. sdb, sdc, etc. 

Ignore any warnings and continue with the installation to the MBR. The main point is to get lilo installed and allow the user to boot Windows, and hopefully Ubuntu, again.

[SIZE=3]**Problem #2:**[/SIZE]

You have Wubi installed and are able to boot Windows normally. 

However, attempting to boot Ubuntu leads to a number of possible failures including, but not limited to, the following:

computer reboots without user interaction, a black screen with or without any error messages, loadfont errors, file not found errors, and the like.

These issues affect the following versions: 10.04, 10.04.1, and 10.10

The solutions for each version are somewhat different and will be noted next to the relevant solution.

[SIZE=3]**Possible Cause:**[/SIZE]

Recent upgrades to GRUB and the lupin package (used for Wubi) have caused the wubildr file to become out of sync with the latest version of GRUB. This appears to be a problem whether Wubi is installed on the Windows partition or a separate partition.

As a result, wubildr is unable to handle the commands in the grub.cfg menu and the computer reboots.

[SIZE=3]**Solution #1 (10.04):**[/SIZE]

If it is a 10.04 install, the fix is to boot an Ubuntu LiveCD/USB, loop mount the wubi root.disk and manually edit the grub.cfg file.

**IMPORTANT:**

You need to correctly identify your Windows partition. The examples here are meant to be just that, examples.

If you do not know or are unsure which is the correct partition, run the [boot info script]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/") and either post it here or use the information for your own purposes.

As an example, a Windows partition with a Wubi install would look like this in the RESULTS.txt:

```
[COLOR=Red]sda3[/COLOR]: _________________________________________________________________________

    File system:       ntfs
    Boot sector type:  Windows Vista/7
    Boot sector info:  No errors found in the Boot Parameter Block.
    Operating System:  Windows 7
    Boot files/dirs:   /Windows/System32/winload.exe /wubildr.mbr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr.mbr /wubildr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr /ubuntu/disks/root.disk 
                       /ubuntu/disks/swap.disk
```Note that sda3 is the partition which contains your Windows installation as well as the files needed by Wubi.

Boot a LiveCD/USB, identify your Windows partition that contains the root.disk (make sure you mount the correct partition; this example assumes /dev/sda**[COLOR=Red]1[/COLOR]**), and edit grub.cfg as follows:

```
sudo mkdir /media/win 
sudo mount /dev/sda**[COLOR=Red]1[/COLOR]** /media/win
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
sudo cp /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg.copy
sudo chmod +w /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg
gksu gedit /mnt/boot/grub/grub.cfg
```Remove all lines in the file from the start of the file up to,_ but not including_, the following menuentry ```
 ### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
```Save, reboot.

[SIZE=3]**Solution #2 (10.04 upgraded to 10.10):**[/SIZE]

If it is a 10.04 to 10.10 upgrade, boot into Windows make a backup of the wubildr file and then copy the c:\ubuntu\winboot\wubildr file over the c:\wubildr file.

Alternatively, you can do this from the LiveCD/USB using the instructions given to mount the Windows partition. 

Run the following commands:
```
sudo mkdir /media/win 
sudo mount /dev/sda**[COLOR=Red]1[/COLOR]** /media/win
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
sudo cp -p /media/win/wubildr /media/win/wubildr.save
sudo cp -p /media/win/ubuntu/winboot/wubildr /media/win
```This example again assumes sda1, but change to reflect your actual Windows partition e.g. sda2, sda3 etc.
Reboot.

[SIZE=3]**Solution #3 (10.04.1 upgraded to 10.10):**[/SIZE]

This may leave the user with a GRUB prompt and/or the other symptoms previously mentioned.

There are 2 possible approaches here.

Either boot the LiveCD/USB and edit the grub.cfg file as before (Solution #1).

Alternatively, use the instructions provided by drs305 to boot into Ubuntu from the GRUB prompt:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10264157&postcount=33](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10264157&postcount=33)

The advantage of being able to boot from the GRUB prompt is that the user does not need to use a LiveCD/USB.

If booting from the GRUB prompt works and you are able to boot into Ubuntu, follow the instructions in Solution #1 to edit the grub.cfg file.

In this case, enter the following command in the terminal:
```
gksu gedit /boot/grub/grub.cfg
```Again, as above, remove the entries from the start of the file up to, _but not including_, the following menuentry```
 ### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
```[SIZE=3]**Permanent Fix (as of the time of writing):**[/SIZE]

Thanks to bcbc, there is a more or less permanent fix for these problems.

These steps are applied **after** you have restored the Wubi install and are able to boot normally into both Windows and Ubuntu.

From the terminal:

```
sudo mv /boot/grub /boot/grubold
sudo mkdir /boot/grub
sudo cp /boot/grubold/grubenv /boot/grub
sudo update-grub
```[SIZE=2][B]
FIXED![/B][/SIZE]

Not done yet - prevent GRUB updates from breaking the Wubi install again:

Go to System > Administration > Synaptic Package Manager and select the grub-pc and grub-common packages. Click on Package > Lock Version.

[COLOR=Red]I cannot stress this enough; if you want to prevent this happening either on a freshly installed system or a fixed system, please lock the grub packages.[/COLOR]

This fix has been tested and confirmed on a 10.04.1 install, an install upgraded from 10.04.1 to 10.10, as well as a new 10.10 install (with grub-pc reinstalled to intentionally break it).

[SIZE=3]**Additional Information:**[/SIZE]

If you find that one of the solutions does not work for your version, try another. For example, if copying the wubildr file does not get the job done, use the fix to edit the grub.cfg file.

If all of the above seems overwhelming, you can recover your data from the Wubi install from _within_ Windows. In this case, you can use [ext2read]("http://ext2read.blogspot.com/") to recover any important data from the Ubuntu root.disk. 

If using the Ubuntu LiveCD/USB, you can also access and copy your user  files. The Ubuntu folders are located in /mnt if you mounted Wubi as  instructed earlier in the guide. Your HOME folder contents are found at  /mnt/home/<your_user_name>/

Do not run the sudo update-grub command BEFORE rebooting after following the solutions above.

In most cases, locking the GRUB version in Synaptic and/or not upgrading will help prevent problems arising in the first place.

If you are uncertain as to the designation for your Windows partition, either post the results of the boot info script or use the information yourself.

Here is another example from the RESULTS.txt:

```
sda2: _________________________________________________________________________

     File system:       ntfs
    Boot sector type:  Windows Vista/7
    Boot sector info:  No errors found in the Boot Parameter Block.
    Operating System:  Windows 7
    Boot files/dirs:   /bootmgr /Boot/BCD /Windows/System32/winload.exe 
                       /wubildr.mbr /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr.mbr /wubildr 
                       /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr /ubuntu/disks/root.disk 
                       /ubuntu/disks/swap.disk
```In this case, you would mount sda2 in the commands given above. 

Please be careful and make sure you mount the correct partition.

If in doubt, ask here first.

Finally, if you would like to migrate your Wubi install to a dedicated partition, bcbc has created a fantastic guide on how to do this:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354)

### Post by Shikhin on 2011-07-15
> **Rubi1200 said:**

Here is what you need to do:

Move the root.disk and swap.disk files back to sda3 (G drive I assume) and place them in their folders with the following structure:

\ubuntu\disks\root.disk
\ubuntu\disks\swap.disk 

Next, make a backup copy of the root.disk to an external medium such as a USB stick in case this happens again.

Finally, reboot and see if everything is normal again.

You may need to apply solution # 1 to problem # 2 from the main post (but first see if Ubuntu boots normally).

If not, run the boot script again and post it here please.

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-07-15
Hi and welcome to the forums Hronak :)

Please do the following so we can get a better overview of what is going on:

Boot the Ubuntu Live CD/USB. Choose the option "Try Ubuntu without any changes." Once the desktop loads come back here and do the following:

1. Download the boot info script which is in a zipped file. There is a link in my signature.
2. Once downloaded, move the boot info script by either copying or dragging and dropping the zipped folder onto the desktop and unzip the contents by using right-click Extract here.
3. Open the folder and copy the script to the desktop (you can also drag and drop if you like)
4. Open a terminal and run the following command

```
sudo bash ~/Desktop/boot_info_script.sh
```

This will create a RESULTS.txt file on the desktop. Paste the entire contents of that file back here in a new post. Once pasted highlight all text and click the # sign on the toolbar to place code tags around the text.

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-07-15
Shikhin,
The boot script results only confirm what you already know; that the root.disk is gone.

Try searching in other locations as well, making sure you enabled the hidden files and folders option.

Unfortunately, however, if you cannot find it you have no choice but to reinstall again.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-07-17
> **Fukuro Orukuf said:**
> Guys,

Newbie here. I have installed Ubuntu on my DELL Optiplex  GX270, which until now only had run under Windows XP (yeah, I know...).  Anyways, like I said, I "managed" to install Ubuntu using Wubi. Both (XP  and Ubuntu) were running just fine until a couple of days ago. At that  time, my computer slowed down so badly while I was browsing the internet  using Firefox under Ubuntu that I shut it down (turned the power off).  When, I rebooted after I selected Ubuntu, I got this error message:

**Try(hd0,0) NTFS5**

and then:

**GRUB>**

I  have been trying to "fix" it since to no avail. I managed to reboot in  Ubuntu using an USB, but I can't have access to my files in Ubuntu in  the PCs hard drive. I should also point that the PC still runs fine  under XP.

So here is what I need guidance with (in that order):

1. Is there anyway I can access my data (Ubuntu) on the hard drive? either from XP or from Ubuntu on the USB.

2. If this is not possible how can I go about fixing this situation?

Thanks in advance.

This is an output from Ubuntu in the USB.


Hi and welcome to the forums. 

It's very important to avoid forced shutdowns when using Wubi. Wubi uses a virtual disk that is itself a file, so... if Ubuntu is in the middle of accessing files, it means the virtual disk is also accessing the hard drive, and by powering off you can corrupt it. 

In some cases Windows will repair the damage and move it to a hidden folder C:\FOUND.000 on windows.

So first thing - go to C:\ubuntu and check that the root.disk is there: C:\ubuntu\disks\root.disk
If not, turn on hidden folders (in windows explorer, go to Tools, Folder Options, View tab. Check Show hidden files and folders. Then look for C:\FOUND.000 
If the root.disk is in there, copy it back. You can only recover your data if you have this file.

If you have the file, then it might be corrupted within the virtual file system, in which case you can run fsck on it from the live USB. See [here]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How_can_I_access_my_Wubi_install_and_repair_my_install_if_it_won.27t_boot.3F") for info

### Post by bcbc on 2011-07-17
Fukuro Orukuf...

Try this:
1. boot that live USB you have, see if you can see the root.disk (copy it if you can).
i.e. go to a terminal (CTRL+ALT+t)
mount the windows partition
look for the disk
copy (assuming you have enough space to copy it)
```
sudo mkdir /media/win
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /media/win
ls /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk  #(lowercase LS)
cp /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /media/win/backuproot.disk

```

You could also try to mount the root.disk and copy personal data:
```
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
cp /mnt/home/yourUserName/xxx /media/win/somefolder

```

Once you've tried the data recovery, I would recommend booting windows and running chkdsk:
Open Computer (or "My computer" on XP)
Right click on C:, select properties, Tools
Check disk for errors:
Fix automatically

Then you'll have to reboot to complete.

I'd say the chances of it coming back with the \ubuntu\disks folder in tact will be slim. So you'll have to check C:\FOUND.000

Hopefully if you were able to copy your root.disk it won't matter. Report back with what you find and we can see what's going on.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-07-17
> **mclizardman said:**
> Well, I defragmented the hard drive, and I still got that weird message and extremely long boot time. Any other suggestions?

You can copy wubildr to the root of /dev/sda1. Then wubildr.mbr will find it quicker. But it's a bit of a manual workaround. It shouldn't hurt anything with wubi, even if you forget to remove it later, but you might want to make a note to remove it in case you install another version of Ubuntu and there is a conflict.

```
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt
cp /host/wubildr /mnt/wubildr
sudo umount /mnt
```

It shouldn't hurt anything with wubi, even if you forget to remove it later, but you might want to make a note to remove it in case you install another version of Ubuntu and there is a conflict.

```
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt
cp /host/wubildr /mnt/wubildr

It won't hurt anything in Windows 7 either? I need to make sure that Windows still works. How would I remove it when it is time to upgrade? Thanks.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-07-18
> **mclizardman said:**
> It won't hurt anything in Windows 7 either? I need to make sure that Windows still works. How would I remove it when it is time to upgrade? Thanks.
No Windows won't care.

To remove it:
```
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt
rm /mnt/wubildr
sudo umount /mnt

```

At some point - when you are comfortable that you want to be using Ubuntu on an ongoing basis - you should probably consider switching to a full install. Wubi is great to try out Ubuntu without having to partition, but the intention of Wubi is that it will lead users to do a full install eventually. So once you start having to do these manual workarounds you can weigh the effort of partitioning etc...

See [HOWTO: migrate wubi install to partition]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354")

### Post by bcbc on 2011-07-18
> **mclizardman said:**
> Thanks, I am considering doing a partition. That guide that you posted about creating a partition  inside of the how to migrate Wubi post is made if you are using a live CD. How to i create a partition from a Wubi Install? Heck I'll even do it from Windows 7. Or should I save this for a different forum topic.

That partitioning guide doesn't refer to wubi installs (or migrating wubi), so that's why there is an emphasis on the live CD, but if you first shrink the partition from Win7 then you can create new partitions in the space from the Wubi install using GParted. However, if you're trying to use GParted to split a partition that's currently in use it won't allow it.

I still recommend having an Ubuntu live CD or USB. It's a repair disk that you might need in the future. I also recommend making sure you have a windows repair disk and that your backups are in order before partitioning.

PS GParted isn't installed by default on a Wubi install - so install it from software centre or command line (sudo apt-get install gparted). It is always available on the live CD.

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-07-20
> **ardit ,A, said:**

We probably need more information to be able to help you with this.

I suggest you do the following:

Boot the Ubuntu Live CD/USB. Choose the option "Try Ubuntu without any changes." Once the desktop loads come back here and do the following:

1. Download the boot info script which is in a zipped file. There is a link in my signature.
2. Once downloaded, move the boot info script by either copying or dragging and dropping the zipped folder onto the desktop and unzip the contents by using right-click Extract here.
3. Open the folder and copy the script to the desktop (you can also drag and drop if you like)
4. Open a terminal and run the following command

```
sudo bash ~/Desktop/boot_info_script.sh
```

This will create a RESULTS.txt file on the desktop. Paste the entire contents of that file back here in a new post. Once pasted highlight all text and click the # sign on the toolbar to place code tags around the text.

### Post by HarriU11-04 on 2011-07-23

sudo umount /mnt

```At some point - when you are comfortable that you want to be using Ubuntu on an ongoing basis - you should probably consider switching to a full install. Wubi is great to try out Ubuntu without having to partition, but the intention of Wubi is that it will lead users to do a full install eventually. So once you start having to do these manual workarounds you can weigh the effort of partitioning etc...

See [HOWTO: migrate wubi install to partition]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354")
Hello

Sorry if I am asking a really dumb question, but...
My Windows machine was already partitioned (5) so when I installed wubi I installed it to an unused/spare partition. The end result was a dual-boot machine :). Dual booting works perfectly fine. I wanted to make sure everything was working fine in Ubuntu before trashing Windows.

QUESTION: Do I need to do anything if/when I trash Windows?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-07-23
> **HarriU11-04 said:**
> Hello

Sorry if I am asking a really dumb question, but...
My Windows machine was already partitioned (5) so when I installed wubi I installed it to an unused/spare partition. The end result was a dual-boot machine :). Dual booting works perfectly fine. I wanted to make sure everything was working fine in Ubuntu before trashing Windows.

QUESTION: Do I need to do anything if/when I trash Windows?

No dumb questions - Wubi is not straightforward.

So the answer is no - you cannot trash Windows. When you install with Wubi it uses a virtual disk as well as a boot mechanism that is dependent on the Windows OS. So even though you installed to a separate partition, it still created a virtual disk on it (the same as if you installed on windows' C: drive).

If you want to remove Windows you need to either reinstall Ubuntu or [migrate]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354") your existing Wubi install.

In some ways it's easier to migrate if your Wubi was on C: since you already have that partition sitting around. But there are other options e.g. migrating from the root.disk. Have a look at the migration guide and let me know if you have any questions.

---

### Post by larrypg on 2011-07-23
Thank you for the response.

I have run defrag and chkdsk in between each install and remove.  Each time I install it does the same thing as stated in my previous post.  

I only have 256M ram and noticed when I run top it is showing no swap.  Although it worked fine when I installed it in the past I am wondering if there is a way to setup swap before it does it itself.  I can get to a terminal so am able to run commands. Or that might have nothing to do with it:)

### Post by bcbc on 2011-07-23
> **larrypg said:**
> Thank you for the response.

I have run defrag and chkdsk in between each install and remove.  Each time I install it does the same thing as stated in my previous post.  

I only have 256M ram and noticed when I run top it is showing no swap.  Although it worked fine when I installed it in the past I am wondering if there is a way to setup swap before it does it itself.  I can get to a terminal so am able to run commands. Or that might have nothing to do with it:)

Thanks for any help.
With 256MB RAM - that's an absolute minimum to install with Wubi (it uses ubiquity and 256 is the minimum) and it's probably very slow to run. Even though you have 256 there may be some memory that's already assigned so that might explain why the installer is freezing.

You should probably be doing a partitioned install and using the alternate installer which requires less RAM and then choosing lubuntu or xubuntu to get the best experience.

PS use the "free" command to check memory use and swap.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-07-25
> **heather.rose73 said:**
> Thank you for your suggestion but I don't even have a desktop. All that comes up when I start my computer is the black screen of grub. I am currently using a different computer to look up possible ways to fix mine. I think what happened is that windows and ubuntu did not configure correctly so i'm trying to figure out how to load one of the OSs from grub, or possibly from a USB stick but so far I have had no luck....what information do you need to be able to try and help?

It's difficult to know what has happened since grub 0.97 is grub legacy (not used since release 9.04) - and also wubi doesn't replace the windows bootloader with grub. So, the fact that you get this on startup is puzzling.

If you can boot a CD or a USB stick, then I'd suggest booting Ubuntu in live cd/usb mode and running the bootinfoscript. If you cannot do that, and you have a windows repair CD you could install the windows bootloader.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-07-28
> **Mac-GH said:**
> Hello, I am getting an error when trying to install Ubuntu with Wubi.
I have a screenshot of the error below.

Please help, I want Ubuntu so bad :p

Likely you have dynamic drives. You cannot install Ubuntu on dynamic drives (it's possible in some circumstances, but since Ubuntu ignores the dynamic drive information it can lead to data loss).
In this particular case, it is Windows that is denying the request.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-08-09
davidsinterested,
it appears that Windows has removed the root.disk - probably detected ntfs corruption.

Click the Start button, type (don't enter) "cmd", look above and right click on cmd.exe and choose "run as administrator". Then, change to the \found.000 directory and enter DIR. The root.disk should be there, or otherwise a file named CHK0000.CHK that is the same size as the root.disk. Copy it back to the \ubuntu\disks directory.

Assuming you installed on C: ...
```
C:\cd \found.000

C:\found.000>dir
 Volume in drive C is OS
 Volume Serial Number is B4B7-99A8

 Directory of C:\found.000

19/07/2011  02:02 PM    15,000,000,000 file0000.chk
               1 File(s)              8 bytes
               0 Dir(s)  222,258,069,504 bytes free

C:\found.000>move file0000.chk \ubuntu\disks\root.disk
        1 file(s) moved.

```

Something like that. It should be possible through internet explorer but you have to enable hidden files. If the found.000 directory is empty, you need to run chkdsk.

Hopefully you find it - as the missing root.disk contains all the data that was on your Ubuntu install.

PS you should have been mounting /dev/sda2, not /dev/sdb1 (but it wouldn't have helped as the root.disk is not there anymore and those fixes shouldn't be required anymore).

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-08-09
davidisinterested

first change drive, then change directory:
```
D:
cd \found.000
```

If that still fails (and remember it must be an administrator command prompt) then you should run chkdsk (Computer, right click D:, Tools, Check disk for errors, fix automatically) and then try again. But I'd guess it's already there since it's not in D:\ubuntu\disks

### Post by bcbc on 2011-08-09
davidisinterested,

Sorry it didn't work out. It's a bit of a mystery to me what causes these disappearing root.disks. I don't think it happens a lot, and generally assume it's due to hard poweroffs although obviously not in your case. So... no idea really.

A direct install won't suffer from this - so that's worth a thought. I also keep data synched on Ubuntu One or on the host partition so there's less risk in using Wubi, which is something you could do to mitigate the risk if you stick with wubi.

output :

The superblock could not be read or does not describe a correct ext2
filesystem.  If the device is valid and it really contains an ext2
filesystem (and not swap or ufs or something else), then the superblock
is corrupt, and you might try running e2fsck with an alternate superblock:
    e2fsck -b 8193 <device>
[/COLOR]
Please see the attached boot info script results and suggest solution. Thanks in advance.

To fsck:
```
sudo mkdir /media/win
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /media/win
sudo fsck /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk
```

To mount and browse virtual disk with nautilus:
```
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
gksu nautilus /mnt
```
You can then copy data to /media/win

EDIT: you can mount before fsck'ing to copy data, but don't fsck while mounted. You can use those flags as well if you like (fyv : force even if clean/respond yes to prompts/verbose)
