### Post by bcbc on 2011-08-11
san198_1111

Another thing you can check... after you've run fsck and if you still have problems, mount the root.disk and check how much space is available:
```
df -h
```

It will show space available on /dev/loop# (probably /dev/loop1 as /dev/loop0 is your live USB file system). That's another possible cause of booting problems. Post the results here if you still have problems.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-08-13
> **huggs said:**
> Thanks, I'll give the methods in the guide a go, right after I boot back into Ubuntu. (Scanning Winblows with MalwareBytes atm)

Edit: Tried the first 2 of the 3 things from that link, resulting in a blank black screen where the purple one was before. (No big deal, for some reason changing the Plymouth theme to anything but the original Ubuntu Logo purple one results in a black screen. Switching bck brings back the purple one.)

 The modification of the login screen works, but I have my comp set to log in automatically.

 The 3rd of the 3 I didn't have the guts to try, because I have rendered my system unbootable several times in the past by messing with 05_Debian_Theme.

It is mentioned in the Plymouth thread that Wubi installs are missing a couple of modules, and that this is likely the reason that Plymouth themes don't work. Is it possible that all we have to do is replace these modules and we will have a good way to change the boot screen?

I changed my Wubi install to the solar theme. It didn't show up at first, but I remembered some fix from when 10.04 was released. Now it shows up with a little black gap between the theme and the (still) purple login background. The login background has to be changed separately which is a bit of a pain. 
On shutdown I'm getting some console messages so the solar theme is a little messed (might be because i'm on a dev release)

I'm not recommending these instructions, but this is what I did.
```
sudo apt-get install plymouth-theme-solar
sudo update-alternatives --config default.plymouth
#select solar
echo "FRAMEBUFFER=y" | sudo tee -a /etc/initramfs-tools/conf.d/splash
sudo update-initramfs -u
```

Explanation of code
1. install solar theme
2. select solar theme as default
3. old patch that gets theme to show (works on my computer with intel graphics, no idea whether this is still the way to do this).
4. update initial ram disk

I'll investigate the login background separately.

### Post by drs305 on 2011-08-16
> **Getwrecked9 said:**
> What is oneiric ocelot? I tried posting in the beginner's forum but it was closed down.

Getwrecked9,

Welcome to the Ubuntu forums.

It's the next release of Ubuntu, currently in testing. It should be released in October.

The question doesn't have anything to do with this thread however, so if you have unrelated questions please begin a new thread in the appropriate forum. There is a "New thread" button at the top left of each forum.

The *oneiric* forum shouldn't be closed. Older 'development' threads (for previous releases while they were undergoing testing) are closed to new posts, but *oneiric* should not be. If you have problems posting, we have a "Forum Feedback & Help" forum where you can ask questions and seek assistance for matters relating to the forums themselves.

### Post by spc_hicks09 on 2011-08-22
Every time I try to install Ubuntu with Wubi, Wubi fails at the download. It seems like the servers are down. I have to install it using Wubi because I'm on a netbook with no CD/DVD drive and doesn't support USB Boot. Attached is the error log, it seems as if the servers are down or something.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-08-22
> **spc_hicks09 said:**
> Every time I try to install Ubuntu with Wubi, Wubi fails at the download. It seems like the servers are down. I have to install it using Wubi because I'm on a netbook with no CD/DVD drive and doesn't support USB Boot. Attached is the error log, it seems as if the servers are down or something.
Natty doesn't have a Netbook-edition. The last release to have this was maverick, but the entry was left in the Wubi.exe for Natty by accident. So, just select Ubuntu and it should work fine.

The reason there is no Netbook edition is that the Unity desktop which was used only on Netbook on Maverick is now the standard desktop on Ubuntu. So there's no need for a separate netbook release.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-08-28
> **xnsine said:**
> I know they shouldnt be manually copied.  The wubi installation runs, it makes all the files in the ubuntu folder, but it doesnt add the wubildr line in boot.ini. In fact, it doesnt change boot.ini at all.   Here's my boot.ini:

```

timeout=10
default=multi(0)disk(0)rdisk(0)partition(1)\WINDOWS.0
[operating system]

multi(0)disk(0)rdisk(0)partition(1)\WINDOWS.0="microsoft windows xp"
/noexecute=optin /fastdetect

multi(0)disk(0)rdisk(1)partition(1)\WINDOWS.0="microsoft windows xp"
/noexecute=optin /fastdetect

multi(0)disk(0)rdisk(1)partition(1)\wubildr.mbr="Ubuntu"




```

The last line should be ```
C:\wubildr.mbr = "Ubuntu"
```The rest looks a bit strange - maybe the way you copied it. I don't have my own available to compare at the moment. But the last line is wrong and probably explains the hal.dll error.

### Post by mattwhelan on 2011-08-29
Hi all. I'm having the same issue as this person: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11134787&postcount=637](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11134787&postcount=637)

Last night I set my laptop on the floor before I went to bed and it was running. I unplugged the laptop (Dell) from the wall before I put it on the floor, but it was still running off battery power, idling. I assume the battery died during the night.

When I powered on this morning and selected Ubuntu, I received the Grub prompt (same as in linked post, #637). I've been using Ubuntu via Wubi as my main OS for about 6 months now as I still need Windows occasionally. Really love it, but this is the first time anything serious has happened... I have about 4 days of work and some smaller projects that I hadn't backed up, and I really need to recover them.

I hired a guy today to look at it, he knows a lot more than me, but we couldn't recover the "disks" folder (C:\ubuntu\disks), ran CHKDSK in Windows a few times, nothing. There's just nothing there whether mounting from Ubuntu on disc or in Windows.

Am I royally screwed here? Is there any way to recover these files? I really love Ubuntu for learning Linux/command-line/etc., but am thinking of switching to Macs at least for work stuff as this seems really strange, even if via Wubi.

I've read about 20 blog/forum posts w/ potential recovery instructions and none seem to work... is my Ubuntu installation gone forever?

### Post by bcbc on 2011-08-29
mattwhelan,
You didn't specify so I am just making sure... did you look in the c:\found.000 folder and look for a dir0000.chk directory?

PS when you use Wubi, you are using a virtual disk. This is the first point of vulnerability (single file contains your full install). The second point is that hibernation is not supported, however, Ubuntu is set by default to hibernate on a low/critical battery condition.

So, unless you've changed the computer to sleep on low battery, most likely it tried to hibernate, failed, tried again, failed etc. until finally it just powered off.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-08-31
> **kansasnoob said:**
> I could use some Wubi testing advice:

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/838040](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/838040)

After filing that, and following up on unrelated Oneiric Beta 1 bugs, I booted into Windows XP and inserted the Lubuntu disc. All I got was a "no CD found" message. With the Ubuntu CD I get the expected options.

So, first really dumb question; how do I know if a CD/iso has the wubi installation stuff?

Aside from that, does anyone else know what I should expect from the newest version of ubiquity with an Ubuntu CD?

And would it be possible at all to install Lubuntu using Wubi?

Color me dumb as a post :confused:
So far, lubuntu hasn't been added as an available distro in wubi.exe. So it's not possible to install it with that option.
I'm not even sure if lubuntu has the lupin-support package that is required. Doesn't really explain why windows doesn't recognize it as a CD though!

I'm not aware of a plan to include lubuntu with Wubi (which doesn't necessarily mean anything since I don't know what the devs are up to but it's not in the [blueprint]("https://blueprints.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+spec/foundations-o-wubi") for oneiric). I have been keeping half-an-eye out for it as I'm curious to try lubuntu with Wubi.

Regarding the Wubi option in Ubiquity - I can't comment. I haven't tried this yet in Oneiric. I guess I can give it a go during Beta. I do agree with you it's stupid to run wubi.exe standalone when you already have a CD, however if you happened to be installing from a USB then this won't support a Wubi install if the partition is > 900000 bytes (and if you happen to leave it plugged in, even wubi.exe run standalone will find it and fail). So there are more than a few minor issues to be resolved. 

Good to see you are doing a great job testing again.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-09-01
Beta already - I hadn't noticed - but should have figured with all the major updates recently: all my Oneiric installs have been complaining (Unity).

I checked the bug report and it looks good. Not rebooting sounds like  a beta issue - usually REISUB works though.

Honestly I can't understand why they haven't added lubuntu to wubi. It's not exactly a big change - they just add it to a single file as an option with a link to the iso/metalink, rebuild - and then it should work. (Provided lubuntu has the lupin-support package included in the ISO). I do see the point that most people running lubuntu have a low spec computer, but in that case they wouldn't even be able to run Ubiquity which requires 256MB ram (more than xubuntu or lubuntu need to run). And if they have to use the alternate installer then Wubi isn't offered.

### Post by nighthawk1 on 2011-09-11
If I force shut down in Ubuntu will it harm my Windows install and if I force shut down in Windows will it harm Ubuntu?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-09-11
> **nighthawk1 said:**
> If I force shut down in Ubuntu will it harm my Windows install and if I force shut down in Windows will it harm Ubuntu?

Forced shutdowns can cause file system corruption. I would think it unlikely that it would affect arbitrary files, and more likely that it would affect those in use (possibly being written to when the computer is powered down). So, in my opinion, it's not likely to affect Windows if you power-off while running Ubuntu - just your Ubuntu root.disk etc. may be corrupted, and vice versa.

At the end of the day, it's just best not to do it. In Ubuntu use this instead: [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How_to_reboot_cleanly_even_when_the_keyboard.2BAC8-mouse_are_frozen](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How_to_reboot_cleanly_even_when_the_keyboard.2BAC8-mouse_are_frozen)

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-09-11
In addition to the sensible advice posted by bcbc, I also recommend that you make a copy of the root.disk and keep it somewhere safe like an external medium.

That way, if something ever happens you have a backup.

> **marjan123 said:**
> Hi, brand new ubuntu user here.

Yesterday I installed ubuntu with wubi to check it out and here is the problem:
I can't connect to internet. It could find the eth0 but was still  disconnected. Then I wrote the data (ip etc.) manually and it says that I  am connected and in options says everything is ok, the driver is appropriate, but I still can't connect to internet.
So what's the problem? I use win7, motherboard is asrock 939Dual-sata2 and connection is fibre. Thank you
_****_
Try the [Networking and Wireless subforum]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=336"). They have some good sticky threads to help with troubleshooting and also show what info you should provide to get help. (These issues aren't wubi-specific and you'll find networking 'subject-matter experts' in that forum)

### Post by bcbc on 2011-09-14
> **Steam. said:**
> Having this problem with Wubi for quite a while now.Whenever I try to install it on ANY other hard drive than my boot/main drive in Windows, which is C: , it will go to the point where it tries to install, then it says something about not finding system/root..Any help?Sorry, but I can't think of the exact error.
When you say 'other' drive - is it physically another hard drive or is it another partition on the same drive. The only reason I could think is if it's a separate physical drive.

But what you can do is boot Ubuntu and then post the output of the following commands (the first two have lower case -L):
```
sudo fdisk -l
sudo parted -l
sudo blkid

```

This might indicate an issue with the other drive. If you like you can just run the [bootinfoscript]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/") as it will show all of the above and more.

---

Ok, so I am a first time Ubuntu user, just tried installing Mythbuntu using Wubi, itsa no working :(

I've read the first page and the last page (69th page) of this thread, and didn't see the same issue, so here goes...

Installed Mythbuntu 11.04-rev211 via Wubi (chose 3GB option), when prompted to reboot, I rebooted and got the Windows boot menu with my Windows XP Home Edition and Mythbuntu options. I chose the Windows option (not knowing which one I was supposed to), and Windows restarted, and nothing else seemed to be happening, so I assumed I was supposed to start the Mythbuntu OS. Rebooted, choosing Mythbuntu OS and the Mythbuntu OS continued it's installation process. Once it had finished it attempted to restart (I don't remember if I got the boot menu or not) and there was some catastrophic failure and it crashed back to the Windows boot menu.

Now, every time I try to start Mythbuntu from the Windows boot menu it shows the following 2 lines:

Try (hd0,0): FAT32: No WUBILDR
Try (hd0,1): FAT32: error "prefix" is not set.

Then it loads the linux/Ubuntu boot menu (GRUB?) but the only option is Windows NT/2000 or Windows XP Home Edition (I don't have NT or 2000 on this computer, go figure), but no Ubuntu or Mythbuntu listed. If I choose the NT/2000, I end up back at the Windows boot menu, if I choose the XP Home I end up starting XP Home but then ending up on Acer's system recovery menu. If I opt out of there I get back to the Windows boot menu where I can choose XP Home and XP starts correctly.

This is nuts! #-o

Please help.

Running:
Windows XP Home Edition SP3
Acer Aspire 3610
Celeron 1.6GHz
RAM 504 Mb
HDD 22GB (7GB available for installation)

### Post by bcbc on 2011-09-15
> **Steam. said:**
> There are no issues.They all work perfectly, i had OSX on that drive before.Gonna recreate the problem and take a pic of it.Ubuntu only installs succesfully on my C: drive.

> **Steam. said:**
> Correction.

The error is:

No root file system is defined, please correct this from the partitioning menu, which I can't access because when I try to launch gparted it gives me some X.Authorities or something error too.11.04/amd64.

When you switch a drive with a GPT partition table and setup a MBR partition table, unless you completely remove the GPT MBR data, you cannot install Ubuntu on it. Refer to this for more info: [http://www.rodsbooks.com/fixparts/](http://www.rodsbooks.com/fixparts/)

---

I filed a bug on this a while back. But I thought it was fixed for Natty 11.04. Is it possible you pressed SKIP during part of the installation? This could also result in the special lupin-support package not being installed - and without it you don't get the ability to boot a Wubi install.

### Post by srs5694 on 2011-09-15
> **Steam. said:**
> The disk itself is already converted fully to MBR.

To be sure, please boot a Linux installation disc, [Parted Magic,]("http://partedmagic.com") or similar tool, open a Terminal window, and type:

```

sudo fdisk -lu

```(In some environments, such as Parted Magic, you can omit the "sudo" part of the command.) Post the results here, between [noparse]```
[/noparse] and [noparse]
```[/noparse] tags for legibility.

Other than that, I never pressed "SKIP" anywhere.

Just found that bug report - it was fixed after Natty was released so you'd have to wait for Oneiric (11.10) to try it: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/759492](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+bug/759492)

The only way to get around this in Natty is to manually boot the wubi-install and install the lupin-support package. If you search this thread (the wubi megathread) you'll find more detailed discussion on how to do this.

### Post by Steam. on 2011-09-15

### Post by bcbc on 2011-09-15
> **Steam. said:**
> I've checked this myself with Acronis and within Hiren, it is MBR.This bug also occurs on other drives that never had been converted to GPT.
There are actually a number of known causes for Ubuntu to reject a drive - the mixed GPT/MBR data is one. Another would be partition table errors. Another is the presence of fakeraid metadata (no raid present) or an unsupported fakeraid setup. 

It seemed a safe bet to assume your problem was due to the GPT mix. Either way, providing the output to the requested information will help us to help you. If you don't have a bootable CD you can also reinstall with Wubi and when you get that error, drop to a terminal Ctrl+Alt+F2 and get the output from
```
sudo fdisk -lu

```

Then you can post the results here. Also, the following can provide useful info...
```
sudo parted -l
sudo blkid
``` 

(the above commands specify lower case -L, not -1)

PS if you have wubi installed on C: and the other drive is plugged in you can just run the commands from your wubi install.

---

The only way to get around this in Natty is to manually boot the wubi-install and install the lupin-support package. If you search this thread (the wubi megathread) you'll find more detailed discussion on how to do this.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-09-16
> **Steam. said:**
> You could've said to press CTRL ALT F2 from wubi earlier ! :DI'll go over on that PC now and try this.

From the install, just open a terminal (Ctrl+Alt+T). You can use Ctrl+Alt+F2 but then you need to login and it will be harder to cut and paste (unless you capture the output).

You can also run the [bootinfoscript]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/").It wasn't really clear whether you had a current install going ;)

ls -l disks

-rwxrwxrwx 1 root root   0 2011-07-15 11:18 install
-rwxrwxrwx 1 ...
and 
```
cd /ubuntu/disks
```
bash: cd : disks: Input/output error

Do you have any idea about how can I fix this?

Thank you in advance,

PS. I can boot Windows but I cannot access to c:\ubuntu\disks either.

---

### Post by srs5694 on 2011-09-19
juanvi,

Your symptoms sound like a damaged filesystem to me. I recommend running CHKDSK in Windows. (You can do this from a Command Prompt window or by navigating a maze of GUI options that ends in one to check the disk's integrity -- I don't recall the exact phrasing, though.) You may need to run CHKDSK two or three times.

I always get a bit nervous about serious disk damage like this, so if you have the capacity, I recommend backing up everything important that you can from the disk before you run CHKDSK, just in case the damage is so bad that CHKDSK actually makes matters worse.

---

error: file not found
error: no argument specified
error: no such a partition
error: you need to load the kernel first
Press any key to continue...

If I press a key, it goes back to that grub menu.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-09-19
juanvi, check this out: [http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html](http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html)

Hopefully there is a \found.000 folder with a dir0000.chk directory and your files will be in there.

### Post by juanvi on 2011-09-20
Thanks bcbc!

I found exactly what you told me. I copied the content of this directory as c:\ubuntu\disks, rebooted and I am writting now from ubuntu.

I really appreciate your help!

fi
set default="0"
if [ "${prev_saved_entry}" ]; then
  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then
    saved_entry="${chosen}"
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n "${have_grubenv}" ]; then if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}

function load_video {
true
}

insmod part_msdos
insmod ntfs
set root='(/dev/sda,msdos5)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root BEB23740B236FD09
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=auto
  load_video
  insmod gfxterm
fi
terminal_output gfxterm
if [ "${recordfail}" = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
if background_color 44,0,30; then
  clear
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.38-11-generic" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(/dev/sda,msdos5)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root BEB23740B236FD09
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-11-generic root=UUID=BEB23740B236FD09 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash
    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.38-11-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.38-11-generic (recovery mode)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(/dev/sda,msdos5)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root BEB23740B236FD09
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-11-generic root=UUID=BEB23740B236FD09 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro single 
    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.38-11-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.38-8-generic" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(/dev/sda,msdos5)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root BEB23740B236FD09
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-8-generic root=UUID=BEB23740B236FD09 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash
    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.38-8-generic
}
menuentry "Ubuntu, Linux 2.6.38-8-generic (recovery mode)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(/dev/sda,msdos5)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root BEB23740B236FD09
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.38-8-generic root=UUID=BEB23740B236FD09 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro single 
    initrd /boot/initrd.img-2.6.38-8-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Windows 7 (loader) (on /dev/sda1)" --class windows --class os {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(/dev/sda,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 16B89432B89411FB
    chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###

if [ -f  $prefix/custom.cfg ]; then

fi
### END /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-01
The only thing I can see is that the initrd.img for your latest kernel is broken into 3 fragments. I actually haven't noticed this bootinfoscript diagnostic before... so take it for what it's worth.

What I would do is either rebuild the initrd.img or run fsck on the root.disk (or both).
```
sudo update-initramfs -u
```

Also check how much space you have remaining on the root.disk. On the following command look for (loop0) that represents the root.disk:
```
df -h
```

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-01
Initial ram disk: [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Initrd](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Initrd)

I don't know how it would be corrupted... but it does happen. There's no problem with the space side of things. I suppose there might be fragmentation of the root.disk itself from Windows. 

In general, wubi is good to try out Ubuntu, but once you're tried it out and want to keep it, it's better to install it direct. I think I mentioned already in another thread you posted in, but you can [migrate]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354") the wubi or backup/reinstall manually.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-02
rymdgris,
That command will delete everything - including what's on your host partition (windows). Your best bet is to try a utility that undeletes data. 

You have done the right thing to stop using the hard drive. Any writes on the drive can erase the underlying data.

If you cannot undelete the files, you'll have to try to recover the data files direct from the drive using a utility such as [photorec]("http://www.cgsecurity.org/wiki/PhotoRec"). This won't be easy as it doesn't pick up the file names and will recover files that were already deleted as well as ones removed by that command. You'd have to manually check each one to see if it was a file you wanted to keep.

If you were led to run that command by someone maliciously you should report where this occurred.


PS: I've been trying to find a tool recommended for undeleting - there seem to be so many out there. You can try testdisk (same people who make photorec) - e.g. boot an Ubuntu live CD, install testdisk (sudo apt-get install testdisk), then follow this guide: [http://www.cgsecurity.org/wiki/TestDisk:_undelete_file_for_NTFS](http://www.cgsecurity.org/wiki/TestDisk:_undelete_file_for_NTFS)

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-02
> **rymdgris said:**
> Oh dear. The reason I wrote it was to write another command that was supposed to trick Facebook to accept my name, as I have a weird name it won't allow. Anyway, I cannot find where I found that command now, but it was some url that had the word "hacker" in it. Anyway...

I have a problem running Testdisk (i tried this first). First I tried the command you wrote, but didn't work, then I dl'ed it and tried to run testdisk_static from the folder I unzipped (with the Autorun Prompt), but didn't work neither. Also tried to put the unzipped folder on the desktop, access the desktop and the folder in terminal and run it from there, but it won't work.

Then I tried to run the photorec_static from the unzipped folder but that wouldn't work neither, so I'm pretty lost :(
okay, boot the ubuntu cd, select "Try it". Then go to a terminal (Ctrl+Alt+T). Then install and run testdisk:
```
sudo apt-get install testdisk
sudo testdisk
```

If you know which device/partition you are trying to recover you can tell testdisk, e.g.:
```
sudo testdisk /dev/sdb1
```

The rest should be similar to that guide I posted.

If testdisk doesn't work, use photorec (it's already installed with testdisk):
```
sudo photorec
```

Just make sure you save the output somewhere other than on the drive that is wiped. 

But it's best to use testdisk first as an undeleted file is a lot better than a 'recovered' noname file.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-02
You need the universe repository to be enabled. It should be by default on a live CD. 

Open Update Manager, click on Settings, then under the "Ubuntu Software" tab make sure that the Community Maintained (Universe) checkbox is checked. Save. Reload when prompted.

You can reload from command line as well to be sure:
```
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install testdisk
```

PS maybe you just need to run that "sudo apt-get update" first (if you find Universe is enabled already)

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-02
Do you need to select a location to save on an undeletion? I would have thought that it would simply undelete the file. 

okay - just reviewed that walkthrough and it does look like it's trying to copy the deleted files to a new location. So you would need to mount some other media and select that mountpoint to copy to.

e.g. plug in an external drive and each partition will either be mounted as /media/*label* (if the partition has a label) or /media/*xxxx-xxxxx-xxxx* (the partition uuid). 

Then select that location to recover files to.

After you've saved important data, you might try an undelete utility that can undelete inplace - no guarantee that will put it back to the original bootable state, but it's worth a shot.

PS I think you can also specify the recovery directory when you start testdisk. Run 'testdisk --help' to see all the options. Edit: no - photorec has this option, not testdisk it seems.

EDIT2: you can manually use the arrow keys and Enter to navigate to an alternate location, so you can hit '..' until you get to / and then select /media etc. until you get to the right location.
Also, if you navigate to the location you want to save to before running testdisk it will use that as default:
```
cd /media/label/RecoveredFiles
sudo testdisk /dev/sdb1
```

PPS: you can hold down the : to select all the files, rather than copy 1 at a time which would be a bit of a pain.

That's unusual. Are you sure you got the right partition? When I sent my computer back for repairs I deleted a whole bunch of stuff and then tried photorec to see what was left over - and pretty much everything reappeared magically. It's actually hard to delete files unless you also overwrite what you just deleted - or use special 'shred' utilities.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-03
PayPaul, it's probably best to post on the Howto itself (Rubi1200, if you like you can move it and my response). 

In answer to your questions:
1. **other** - that just happens to be a directory on my machine that I used to clean up my Downloads directory so it looked better on the screenshot - that simply shows the archive and the extracted script.
2. **-l** - it's a lowercase -L
3. **/dev/sda5** - this is an example partition. You need to change it to one that you have created for the express purpose of migrating to - it's not the one holding the wubi install - it's an empty partition of type 83 (create as an ext4 partition)
4. there is a space between wubi-move-2.0.sh and /dev/sda5

Take your time - there's no rush to migrate. Please review the Howto thread and the links e.g. the one that shows how to manually partition. Please don't hesitate to ask if you have any questions: it's best to make sure you understand before migrating. 
Thanks

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-04
sublinhado,
Look for the file: \ubuntu\install\wubildr-disk.cfg

Open it using Wordpad (or write.exe) and modify the line starting "linux /vmlinuz" and change the end from "ro" to "ro acpi=off noapic nolapic quiet splash" and save:
```
linux /vmlinuz root=UUID=$diskuuid loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk preseed/file=/ubuntu/install/preseed.cfg wubi-diskimage ro acpi=off noapic nolapic quiet splash
```

Note: I haven't done this yet, but I believe it will work. Also, (for other people reading this) this is only required when you run wubi.exe standalone (with 11.10 there are two distinct install methods, one the same as before, and the new one with the 'preinstalled image' which is the one you have).

PS [bug report]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/bugs/862007")

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-05

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-05
Brooksy_FC,

Right, it was removed because it was corrupted. So you need to refer to that link Rubi1200 posted on how to copy it back. Depending on the level of corruption, it may not be available but if the corruption is minor then it's likely in tact and ready to be used as soon as you copy it back.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-05
bigdan35, some external drives aren't available to bios at boot time - booting Wubi requires bios functions and this will fail in a way you've described if bios cannot see the drive that contains the root.disk.

A quick way to check is to run the following (lower case LS) at the grub prompt:
```
ls
```
You're looking for two drives: (hd0) and (hd1) as well as partitions on those drives e.g. (hd0,msdos1)

If you only see one drive, there's your problem.

PS make sure you have the external drive plugged in when running the bootinfoscript Rubi1200 requested.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-06
> **Brooksy_FC said:**
> I can't seem to find the c:\found.000 folder. I've showing all hidden folders from control panel. My root.disk is gone, any idea on where to find it?

Windows 7 makes it difficult. In addition to it being hidden, it's considered a 'protected os' folder so you need to uncheck that. Even after doing that I could see it but not work with it. So instead, go to an administrator command prompt and find it manually. This [link]("http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html") shows how to do this.

### Post by sldiamond on 2011-10-07
I am running Wubi in Ubuntu 9.04 under Windows XP.

After a crash, Ubuntu booted with the following error message:

"The file or directory root.disk is corrupt and unreadable. Please run the chkdsk utility. ALERT! /host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk does not exist. Dropping to a shell."

I tried the mkdir /win; mount /dev/sda1 /win; mkdir /vdisk; mount -o loop /win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /vdisk approach, but the mount failed.

From the shell, "ls /host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk" gives an input/output error.

fsck says "The superblock could not be read or does not describe a correct ext2 filesystem." It suggests e2fsck -b 8193 <device> which doesn't work.

Fortunately, I can still boot to Windows XP. From XP, C:\ubuntu\disks\root.disk DOES exist and the size looks right, but chkdsk says the file is corrupt. I tried to copy the root.disk file to a different drive, but it wouldn't copy.

I noticed warnings in earlier pages here about potential damage caused by running chkdsk. Is there something else I should do INSTEAD? Is there something else I should do BEFORE running chkdsk?

Would appreciate any suggestions. Thanks.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-07
The only way to deal with ntfs corruption is by using chkdsk. If there is a way to backup data from a corrupted root.disk prior to running chkdsk, I am not aware of it. So I'd guess you just have to run chkdsk and hope that the corruption is minor.

It's not that chkdsk will damage your file - it's that it tries to repair corruption - and depending on the level of corruption it may not be able to recover it. That's my understanding without knowing the inner workings of chkdsk.

---

### Post by huggs on 2011-10-10
It seems that we cannot load an image for a GRUB background as is possible with a non-WUBI install.
I asked drs305 in his GRUB Customizer thread about this, and here is how that went.

```
Re: HOWTO: Grub Customizer             
  I did a thread search for WUBI,
 and found the cause of why I cannot display a background image in
 GRUB on a WUBI install.

According to drs305, there are modules necessary 
for rendering an image,  which are not 
included with WUBI installed GRUB, and some other
 file  editing would also be necessary.

I have asked about this subject in the WUBI megathread
 before, but it  seems that the 
knowledgeable folks over there don't know which modules
  it is that are missing.

Could I please get the names of these missing modules, 
and I suppose a  point in the right 
direction as to what other files would need to be  edited? 
I will take this info over to the WUBI 
thread and see if it can  be sorted out, and maybe a little
 tut made, maybe the modules could be 
 included in future releases of WUBI?

The black background is so out of place on my mostly
 blue set-up, I need to rid myself of that and the ugly purple screen too
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
                                                                                                                                                                                                                        #163                                                                                  [drs305]("http://ubuntuforums.org/member.php?u=223945")                                               
             
                                                                  
  
                                             
                Join Date: Jan 2007
                                        My beans are hidden!
                    Ubuntu Development Release                                                                                          
             
                                                                                                   Re: HOWTO: Grub Customizer             
                                                                huggs,

I don't currently have access to a wubi grub.cfg on my system. Here are
  at least some of the modules loaded by my normal grub.cfg file:
  insmod vbe
  insmod vga
  insmod video_bochs
  insmod video_cirrus
  insmod gfxterm
plus any graphic used (png, tga and/or jpg)

I don't think this list is all inclusive and necessarily of any use.
 I would expect that it is much more complicated. 

Normally I would recommend going to the #grub IRC channel and ask the  devs directly, 
but I don't know how many of them are conversant on Wubi  issues. You could also ask in 
the grub mailing list, which might get a  wider audience: 
[http://www.gnu.org/software/grub/gru...nglist.en.html]("http://www.gnu.org/software/grub/grub-mailinglist.en.html")

```I thought I should put it also in this thread since it is related, and I really hope that 
someone has the ability to come up with a way that we might be able to load images in our GRUB background, and change the ugly purple screen. 

It is beyond my level of experience to do this work, or I would gladly do it myself and share how I did it. I have little to no knowledge of the Ubuntu IRC channel, or anyone on it, and I was unsuccessful at signing up for or asking on the GRUB mailing list as drs305 suggested.

I am sorry if this information is not useful, or if I am bothering anyone by posting it. It is not my intention to spam the boards. If there is anything I can do to help the process of enabling these functions in our WUBI installs, please feel free to ask.:)

I followed your 2nd link for the GRUB background, but haven't gotten that to work yet, even though when I ran update-grub, I got the message that the image was found, it still doesn't show behind the GRUB menu, but that's not nearly as big a deal to me as being rid of the purple splash. Probably the bg image isn't showing because I edited it in gimp, likely user error on my part.

Thanks a bunch for your help :)

Edit:
Weird that the bg image isn't showing up, all I did in gimp was to paste the bg image that I wanted over top of one of the ones included in the pack of images installed in the grub2-splash-images package, merge the 2 layers, and save, keeping the file type as TGA.
I had left the image in [I]/usr/share/images/grub and edited /etc/default/grub to reflect the location and file name. When I ran sudo update-grub, I got the message that the image was found, and that the custom text colors would be used.
I rebooted a couple times, and the image never showed, and the text was still default colors. So I copied the image to /boot/grub, and again edited /etc/default/grub to reflect the new location, because back in 9.10 a couple years ago, I had read that at boot time GRUB couldn't look in certain directories for a bg image. I then ran sudo update-grub again, got the same messages saying the image was found and custom text colors would be used, but again at boot, no image and default text colors.
Kinda at a loss why the image isn't showing now, unless maybe the modules required to show images in GRUB weren't included until Natty and I don't have 'em. Oh well, still I like the solar theme 200% better than the purple, and a black bootloader screen isn't really such a bad thing.
Thanks again for your help.
[/I]

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-10-13
Unfortunately, if you have followed all the steps to find the root.disk and it is no longer there then you have little choice but to reinstall.

I recommend, in future, that you make a copy of the root.disk and keep it somewhere safe like an external medium. This way, you have something to restore from if this ever happens again.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-14
> **vyomashar said:**
> Hey Guys,

I am using Ubuntu 11.4 on my PC with Win7 via Wubi on a separate partition for Ubuntu only. 

As we all know 11.10 just released yesterday. So i hit the Update Manager and hit the upgrade button, while it was Fetching Packages i went to sleep and kept the PC ON overnight. 

Woke up in the morning and saw it is asking me to install the update so i did. When the orange bar reached the end my screen went blank and i could only see and move the cursor. I thought it must be updating so kept it like that for sometime but still no change.

So i turned OFF my PC and rebooted it and selected Ubuntu from the boot menu, then nothing happened screen was blank :(. Earlier i used to see the Grub menu showing Ubuntu kernels and recovery option.

Now what do i do.. format the partition and do a fresh install of 11.10 or is there any way to fix it? 

Please help
1. Backup your data from the \ubuntu\disks\root.disk using this tool [http://ext2read.blogspot.com/](http://ext2read.blogspot.com/)  (if you have problems finding the root.disk or reading it, refer to [this]("http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html"))
2. Consider taking this opportunity to install fresh using a real dual boot - Wubi is a good trial for Ubuntu, so you can work hard to recover it, or you can just install a new proper install.
3. If you really want to try to recover,  boot a live CD, run the [bootinfoscript]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/") 
4. [Mount the root.disk]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How_can_I_access_my_Wubi_install_and_repair_my_install_if_it_won.27t_boot.3F") and check the space available: 
```
df -h
```

Post back the results of the above or if you have trouble completing any step.

```

Post back the results of the above or if you have trouble completing any step.

Thanks for your reply.

1) I will use the ext2read to backup the root.disk 
2) Will do a fresh install only
3) As i will be doing a fresh install the 3rd point doesnt count here.
4) A a bit confused about the 4th point. Could you please elaborate.

Thanks

---

### Post by PayPaul on 2011-10-14
> **bcbc said:**
> 
2. Consider taking this opportunity to install fresh using a real dual boot - Wubi is a good trial for Ubuntu, so you can work hard to recover it, or you can just install a new proper install.



I'm about to do just that. However I need to clean out ie: reformat the 3 partitions on my main drive. Can I use Gparted from a live CD to do that? Alternately I believe I can delete all those partitions and start fresh. I'd really prefer to just reformat but if that's the only option I may have to go with it.

---

### Post by djb6 on 2011-10-14
Hey all, I didn't see this mega thread earlier so started a thread about this problem I'm having with WUBI but will post it here as well.  Here is what happened.  I had 11.04 on my netbook, went to do the update, and was told there wasn't enough space.  Since I had done an install using WUBI, I went to Windows, uninstalled it, then downloaded the new WUBI with 11.10 on it.  When I went to install it, it downloaded the ISO okay, but then would start coming up with an error and would refer me to the following error file.

### Post by djb6 on 2011-10-14
I just noticed something on my umpteenth time trying to install it.  It seems to be downloading an ISO file that says in it amd64.  Usually that refers to a 64 bit OS being downloaded, correct?  In which case, my computer is actually a 32 bit.  Is there a way to choose a different ISO to download using WUBI?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-14
> **vyomashar said:**
> Thanks for your reply.

1) I will use the ext2read to backup the root.disk 
2) Will do a fresh install only
3) As i will be doing a fresh install the 3rd point doesnt count here.
4) A a bit confused about the 4th point. Could you please elaborate.

Thanks
If you're doing a fresh install the 4th point doesn't matter. (It just checks the space used/remaining to make sure the upgrade didn't fail due to running out of space). You have to mount the root.disk to check the space on it. Anyway - as I said, don't worry about it if you're reinstalling.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-14
> **PayPaul said:**
> I'm about to do just that. However I need to clean out ie: reformat the 3 partitions on my main drive. Can I use Gparted from a live CD to do that? Alternately I believe I can delete all those partitions and start fresh. I'd really prefer to just reformat but if that's the only option I may have to go with it.

While installing Ubuntu (non-wubi) you can replace all partitions at install time, or boot to the desktop (i.e. live CD) and modify the partitions in whatever way you like, before clicking on "install" from the desktop. You can also format any unmounted partitions while running Wubi (gparted isn't installed by default but you can get it from the software centre).

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-14
> **djb6 said:**
> I just noticed something on my umpteenth time trying to install it.  It seems to be downloading an ISO file that says in it amd64.  Usually that refers to a 64 bit OS being downloaded, correct?  In which case, my computer is actually a 32 bit.  Is there a way to choose a different ISO to download using WUBI?

Even if you're running 32bit windows, you have a 64bit computer. Wubi detects this - and it hasn't been wrong yet (challenged many times though). If you want 32bit you can [force]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#Can_I_force_Wubi_to_download_and_install_a_32_bit_version_of_Ubuntu.3F") it, or if you want to prove it wrong, take your chip model and look it up on intel's site to see whether it's 64bit or not.

---

I am running Wubi in Ubuntu 9.04 under Windows XP. After a crash, Ubuntu booted with the following error message: "The file or directory root.disk is corrupt and unreadable. Please run the chkdsk utility. ALERT! /host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk does not exist. Dropping to a shell." CHKDSK would not run to completion and mounting and trying to copy root.disk resulted in an input/output error. bcbc has been trying to help, but I ran into a dead end.

### Post by Raonix on 2011-10-15
Hello everyone:) I have the same problem like **blacklinerpk**

Error: "WindowsBackend" object has no attribute 'iso_path'

I also tried the both installations from usb and from windows...

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-16
> **Raonix said:**
> Hello everyone:) I have the same problem like **blacklinerpk**

Error: "WindowsBackend" object has no attribute 'iso_path'

I also tried the both installations from usb and from windows...

Please help :)
Here is my log

The problem is that you're installing from a USB stick. Wubi does not actually support this. The problem is that Wubi has a really dumb check to differentiate between a CD and a DVD ISO - it checks the size. But with a USB e.g. yours that's 6GB it sees that as a single ISO and so it figures that its a DVD. It also copies the entire 6GB to the hard drive before it rejects it.

So in general I say a USB install is not supported. The only time it works is when the USB is small enough or has a < 900K partition. 

Just copy wubi.exe to a folder, stick the CD ISO directly in that folder, REMOVE the USB (or wubi will find it and use it) and then it will install from the ISO in the folder.

---

### Post by Raonix on 2011-10-16
> **bcbc said:**
> The problem is that you're installing from a USB stick. Wubi does not actually support this. The problem is that Wubi has a really dumb check to differentiate between a CD and a DVD ISO - it checks the size. But with a USB e.g. yours that's 6GB it sees that as a single ISO and so it figures that its a DVD. It also copies the entire 6GB to the hard drive before it rejects it.

So in general I say a USB install is not supported. The only time it works is when the USB is small enough or has a < 900K partition. 

Just copy wubi.exe to a folder, stick the CD ISO directly in that folder, REMOVE the USB (or wubi will find it and use it) and then it will install from the ISO in the folder.

Thank you very much! :p;)

> I don't think that this error message is meaningful - please post the entire log file %temp%\wubi-11.10-rev24x.log  (x can be 1 or 5, probably 1 for you).

Post it in code tags (click on # above)

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-16
> **hulksmashlinux said:**
> ```
10-16 15:32 ERROR  CommonBackend: Invalid md5 for ISO D:\ubuntu\install\installation.iso (62fb5d750c30a27a26d01c5f3d8df459 != 6c222550f8330cb11cdc07c8f9a215c1)

```

The problem is the md5 check sum doesn't match. So you need to [download]("http://www.ubuntu.com/download/ubuntu/download") the desktop cd iso again, or run wubi.exe standalone (cd removed from computer) in which case it will download a preinstalled smaller image.

---

### Post by hulksmashlinux on 2011-10-16
> **bcbc said:**
> The problem is the md5 check sum doesn't match. So you need to [download]("http://www.ubuntu.com/download/ubuntu/download") the desktop cd iso again, or run wubi.exe standalone (cd removed from computer) in which case it will download a preinstalled smaller image.
Ran the wubi.exe standalone and I got a lot further but on the reboot I got "2.171639 Kernel panic - not syncing: VFS: Unable to mount root fs on disk-block(0,0)". I will delete everything, download a fresh iso and try again later. Thanks for your help.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-16
> **hulksmashlinux said:**
> Ran the wubi.exe standalone and I got a lot further but on the reboot I got "2.171639 Kernel panic - not syncing: VFS: Unable to mount root fs on disk-block(0,0)". I will delete everything, download a fresh iso and try again later. Thanks for your help.
Do you have a raid setup? Maybe some partition table error. I doubt downloading a new ISO will make a difference, but it might help diagnose.

Boot from the CD once you've burned it and select "Try it" without installing. Then run the [bootinfoscript]("http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/").

Edit: I've created [lpbug]876160[/lpbug] for the md5 checksum failure with the incomprehensible message. Please click on: this affects me.
Please also review this bug regarding the VFS mount error: [lpbug]872614[/lpbug]

---

### Post by mylesg on 2011-10-17
I have an HP Pavilion G6 that I used wubi to install Ubuntu 11.10 beta2 on and now I wish to boot a live cd but cannot access my bios anymore. I keep getting the bootloader screen no matter what I do... is there any way to get to my bios without uninstalling wubi/ubuntu?

Myles

### Post by hulksmashlinux on 2011-10-17
Ok, I have quite the odd news to report. I tor-rented a new copy of ubuntu-11.10-desktop-amd64 then ran check disk in windows 7, and sure enough there were plenty of errors fixed on my d: drive. So, instead of burning another cd I got lazy and used virtual clone drive to mount the iso and run the wubi.exe installer. It ran, completed with no errors and I am posting this right now using 11.10 64bit (woo, hoo!) so. Not sure *why* it worked, but it did.

#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  set have_grubenv=true
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ "${prev_saved_entry}" ]; then
  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then
    saved_entry="${chosen}"
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n "${have_grubenv}" ]; then if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}

function load_video {
  insmod vbe
  insmod vga
  insmod video_bochs
  insmod video_cirrus
}

insmod part_msdos
insmod ntfs
set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 34AAF130AAF0EEEC
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=auto
  load_video
  insmod gfxterm
  insmod part_msdos
  insmod ntfs
  set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
  search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 34AAF130AAF0EEEC
  loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
  set root=(loop0)
  set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
  set lang=en_US
  insmod gettext
fi
terminal_output gfxterm
if [ "${recordfail}" = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
insmod part_msdos
insmod ntfs
set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 34AAF130AAF0EEEC
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
insmod png
if background_image /usr/share/images/desktop-base/spacefun-grub.png; then
  set color_normal=light-gray/black
  set color_highlight=white/black
else
  set menu_color_normal=white/black
  set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
  if background_color 44,0,30; then
    clear
  fi
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
if [ ${recordfail} != 1 ]; then
  if [ -e ${prefix}/gfxblacklist.txt ]; then
    if hwmatch ${prefix}/gfxblacklist.txt 3; then
      if [ ${match} = 0 ]; then
        set linux_gfx_mode=keep
      else
        set linux_gfx_mode=text
      fi
    else
      set linux_gfx_mode=text
    fi
  else
    set linux_gfx_mode=keep
  fi
else
  set linux_gfx_mode=text
fi
export linux_gfx_mode
if [ "$linux_gfx_mode" != "text" ]; then load_video; fi
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 3.0.0-12-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 34AAF130AAF0EEEC
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-3.0.0-12-generic root=UUID=34AAF130AAF0EEEC loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash vt.handoff=7
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-3.0.0-12-generic
}
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 3.0.0-12-generic (recovery mode)' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 34AAF130AAF0EEEC
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    echo    'Loading Linux 3.0.0-12-generic ...'
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-3.0.0-12-generic root=UUID=34AAF130AAF0EEEC loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro single 
    echo    'Loading initial ramdisk ...'
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-3.0.0-12-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
if [ "x${timeout}" != "x-1" ]; then
  if keystatus; then
    if keystatus --shift; then
      set timeout=-1
    else
      set timeout=0
    fi
  else
    if sleep --interruptible 3 ; then
      set timeout=0
    fi
  fi
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-18
Myles,

Entering bios is completely unrelated to your installed OS. You can enter bios even without any hard drive connected. Probably best to refer to your manual on how to do this, but most computers hitting F2 or ESC at boot will do it.

PS your wubi install looks severely fragmented.

---

### Post by mylesg on 2011-10-18
> **bcbc said:**
> Myles,

Entering bios is completely unrelated to your installed OS. You can enter bios even without any hard drive connected. Probably best to refer to your manual on how to do this, but most computers hitting F2 or ESC at boot will do it.

PS your wubi install looks severely fragmented.

Hi bcbc, yes I am completely familiar with computers and hardware and know that I *should* be able to access the bios :). The problem is that hitting the esc (correct for my system) or delete or F2 keys gets me nowhere but the bootloader screen. I tried for nearly an hour, over and over, before posting. As for the fragmenting, should I defrag in Win7 or should I have done this prior to doing the wubi install?

Thank you for your input on this, much appreciated!
Myles

PS I'm not a regular windows user, Linux has been my OS of choice since 1998. This laptop is new and the factory install of win7 leaves no room for anything else w/o messing up recovery partitions etc. so wubi is a God-send for me :)

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-18
> **mylesg said:**
> Hi bcbc, yes I am completely familiar with computers and hardware and know that I *should* be able to access the bios :). The problem is that hitting the esc (correct for my system) or delete or F2 keys gets me nowhere but the bootloader screen. I tried for nearly an hour, over and over, before posting. As for the fragmenting, should I defrag in Win7 or should I have done this prior to doing the wubi install?

Thank you for your input on this, much appreciated!
Myles

PS I'm not a regular windows user, Linux has been my OS of choice since 1998. This laptop is new and the factory install of win7 leaves no room for anything else w/o messing up recovery partitions etc. so wubi is a God-send for me :)

It's a bit odd that there is fragmentation on a new computer. Usually windows won't defrag a root disk due to the size. To be honest, it's the bootinfoscript showing the fragments, and I'm not sure what that's based on - maybe it's bogus. You can check how defragmented you are in windows and if it looks good, probably just ignore it. 

I understand your frustration on the BIOS issue, but... what can I say. Maybe the problem is that it's not bios but UEFI. Either way you need to check your user manual. What's the exact model number on the computer?

The disk is fine fragmentation wise, like you said, it's a fairly new computer ~6 months old. My last computer was a dinosaur so the whole UEFI thing never even occurred to me and because I could get in and play with settings I was oblivious. Booting the live cd isn't super important, virtualbox is 'good enough' to have a look, I was just curious about real hardware detection.

Thanks bcbc! You've been very helpful :)

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-18
According to [http://h10032.www1.hp.com/ctg/Manual/c02770268.pdf](http://h10032.www1.hp.com/ctg/Manual/c02770268.pdf) :> Turn on or restart the computer. While the “Press the ESC key for Startup Menu” message is
displayed in the lower-left corner of the screen, press esc. When the Startup Menu is displayed,
press f10. I guess you know that, but... make sure you check for bios updates too.

I'll try using the final release to see if it makes a difference. Maybe it's a known issue with this hardware? I'll go and check for bug-reports / known issues etc. and see if I can't figure this out.

### Post by WaySensei on 2011-10-20
I installed Ubuntu 11.10 via wubi on windows 7. This is my first time using Ubuntu in ~3-4 years, and a lot has changed.

Is it possible to access programs (not just files) on my windows 7 system via Ubuntu? For example, since I have Microsoft Office installed on windows 7, can I open this program and use it, perhaps via Wine, in ubuntu?

---

### Post by PayPaul on 2011-10-20
> **WaySensei said:**
> I installed Ubuntu 11.10 via wubi on windows 7. This is my first time using Ubuntu in ~3-4 years, and a lot has changed.

Is it possible to access programs (not just files) on my windows 7 system via Ubuntu? For example, since I have Microsoft Office installed on windows 7, can I open this program and use it, perhaps via Wine, in ubuntu?

I think you need to go through the installation process for any Windows program in Wine to get it to work. It's also best to put that program in your Home folder to make permissions and allow the program to be seen as an executable. I know there's some set of commands in Terminal to allow permissions for program executables on other drives but I'm not sure precisely what it is. Wine is not an emulator it's a kind of virtual machine for running windows programs. See the Wine forum in this community for further details. One also needs to BROWSE for the particular drives on your computer in the Wine Config dialog. Any other drives you have on your machine are usually listed under the /media path. You do that so that when you are running say, Office 2007 in Wine you can access those files or documents that may be on those drives.

======================== sda5/Wubi/boot/grub/grub.cfg: =========================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  set have_grubenv=true
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ "${prev_saved_entry}" ]; then
  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then
    saved_entry="${chosen}"
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n "${have_grubenv}" ]; then if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}

function load_video {
  insmod vbe
  insmod vga
  insmod video_bochs
  insmod video_cirrus
}

insmod part_msdos
insmod ntfs
set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root E4ACAFF5ACAFC082
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=auto
  load_video
  insmod gfxterm
  insmod part_msdos
  insmod ntfs
  set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
  search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root E4ACAFF5ACAFC082
  loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
  set root=(loop0)
  set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
  set lang=en_US
  insmod gettext
fi
terminal_output gfxterm
if [ "${recordfail}" = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
if background_color 44,0,30; then
  clear
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
if [ ${recordfail} != 1 ]; then
  if [ -e ${prefix}/gfxblacklist.txt ]; then
    if hwmatch ${prefix}/gfxblacklist.txt 3; then
      if [ ${match} = 0 ]; then
        set linux_gfx_mode=keep
      else
        set linux_gfx_mode=text
      fi
    else
      set linux_gfx_mode=text
    fi
  else
    set linux_gfx_mode=keep
  fi
else
  set linux_gfx_mode=text
fi
export linux_gfx_mode
if [ "$linux_gfx_mode" != "text" ]; then load_video; fi
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 3.0.0-12-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root E4ACAFF5ACAFC082
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-3.0.0-12-generic root=UUID=E4ACAFF5ACAFC082 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash vt.handoff=7
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-3.0.0-12-generic
}
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 3.0.0-12-generic (recovery mode)' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root E4ACAFF5ACAFC082
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    echo    'Loading Linux 3.0.0-12-generic ...'
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-3.0.0-12-generic root=UUID=E4ACAFF5ACAFC082 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro single 
    echo    'Loading initial ramdisk ...'
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-3.0.0-12-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###
if [ -f  $prefix/custom.cfg ]; then
  source $prefix/custom.cfg;
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

============================= sda5/Wubi/etc/fstab: =============================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
# Use 'blkid' to print the universally unique identifier for a
# device; this may be used with UUID= as a more robust way to name devices
# that works even if disks are added and removed. See fstab(5).
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>
proc            /proc           proc    nodev,noexec,nosuid 0       0
/host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /               ext4    loop,errors=remount-ro 0       1
/host/ubuntu/disks/swap.disk none            swap    loop,sw         0       0
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

if loadfont /boot/grub/font.pf2 ; then

menuentry "Try Ubuntu without installing" {

    initrd    /casper/initrd.lz
}
menuentry "Install Ubuntu" {
    set gfxpayload=keep
    linux    /casper/vmlinuz  file=/cdrom/preseed/ubuntu.seed boot=casper only-ubiquity quiet splash --
    initrd    /casper/initrd.lz
}
menuentry "Check disc for defects" {
    set gfxpayload=keep
    linux    /casper/vmlinuz  boot=casper integrity-check quiet splash --
    initrd    /casper/initrd.lz
}

default menu.c32 
prompt 0 
menu title UNetbootin 
timeout 100

menu label Default 
kernel /ubnkern 
append initrd=/ubninit file=/cdrom/preseed/ubuntu.seed boot=casper quiet splash --

menu label ^Help 
kernel /ubnkern 
append initrd=/ubninit

menu label ^Try Ubuntu without installing

append initrd=/casper/initrd.lz file=/cdrom/preseed/ubuntu.seed boot=casper  quiet splash -- 
 
label ubnentry2

append initrd=/ubninit  
 
label ubnentry6 
menu label Try Ubuntu without installing

append initrd=/casper/initrd.lz file=/cdrom/preseed/ubuntu.seed boot=casper quiet splash --

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-23
jamesbon, this is the way I'd move an ubuntu install as it takes care of things like the bcdedit etc. [http://askubuntu.com/questions/70783/move-a-wubi-installation-to-another-partition](http://askubuntu.com/questions/70783/move-a-wubi-installation-to-another-partition)

For your purposes you could just create a folder C:\ubuntu\winboot and place wubildr.mbr in it. You can probably also fix the bcd entry using easybcd (which I haven't used) or bcdedit (but for that you'd probably do best to refer to Microsoft support sites). Or this link might help: [http://www.howtogeek.com/howto/20340/how-to-restore-the-wubi-ubuntu-bootloader/](http://www.howtogeek.com/howto/20340/how-to-restore-the-wubi-ubuntu-bootloader/)

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-23
> **kranezor said:**
> hi,guys
I have a big problem when upgrade from 11.04 to 11.10
1.the GNU grub menu still has older version (1.99 12ubuntu5)
2.and grub menu still has older kernel (2.6.28.. something like this)
but i check in synaptic it has version 3.0.0-12
and then i can access manually by change at grub menu
3.I update-grub but anythings no change
4.same issue when i use startup-manager instead
5.same issue when copy wubildr from /ubuntu/boot to c:\
6.try to uninstall from win7 and re-install wubi 11.10 it's cannot
change win-boot-manager when choose ubuntu it's say must load kernel first
7.fortunately I has copy of virtual image and can restore the last version 11.04
8.luckless still occur when I try to upgrade
and my boot_script
1. Version 1.99 12ubuntu5 is correct for Grub2 on release 11.10 
2. An upgrade will still retain the natty kernel 2.6.38.... in your grub menu. There should also be the 3.0.0.12 entry. If not then the upgrade didn't complete. Which would explain the rest.

Check how much space you have on the root.disk (loop0)
```
df -h
```
Make sure there are no errors on the upgrade.

> **bcbc said:**

For your purposes you could just create a folder C:\ubuntu\winboot and place wubildr.mbr in it. Or this link might help: [http://www.howtogeek.com/howto/20340/how-to-restore-the-wubi-ubuntu-bootloader/](http://www.howtogeek.com/howto/20340/how-to-restore-the-wubi-ubuntu-bootloader/)

### Post by bcbc on 2011-10-23
> **kranezor said:**
> thank you for ur reply support
this is df -h
Filesystem            Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/loop0             17G  4.9G   11G  33% /
udev                  864M  8.0K  864M   1% /dev
tmpfs                 348M  820K  347M   1% /run
none                  5.0M     0  5.0M   0% /run/lock
none                  870M  276K  870M   1% /run/shm
/dev/sda2              81G   58G   24G  72% /host

and no error during upgrade
my confuse is there are no kernel 3.0.0-12 on grub menu
and has 2.6.38-11 with 2.6.38-8 instead
So you don't have any space concerns. That's a bit strange then that the 3.0.0-12 kernel doesn't show up. And that this happens repeatedly. Maybe [file a bug]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+filebug") and attach your logfiles.
```
sudo zip -r ~/logfiles.zip /var/log
```

After the upgrade is complete, you could try:
```
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get upgrade
# check for any errors
sudo update-grub
```

Also check the /boot directory to see if both the kernel and the initrd.img are there for 3.0.0-12

---

### Post by kranezor on 2011-10-24
:) thank you very much for ur reconize reply my question
I fixed it
in my case wubi wrong detect my root.disk
I found search method from rubi2000 like this
```
search -s -f -n /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
echo $root
```
which it be in sda4
just press c at grub menu and try to change root=(hd0,4)
then can access with kernel version 3.0.0-12
finally update-grub and reboot
:guitar:

---

I found search method from rubi2000 like this
```

echo $root
```
which it be in sda4
just press c at grub menu and try to change root=(hd0,4)
then can access with kernel version 3.0.0-12
finally update-grub and reboot
:guitar:

### Post by trebz32 on 2011-10-24
Hi, I'm using wubi with Ubuntu 11.4 installed. I was wondering if it's possible to run virtualbox and guest OS under this type of installation?

---

### Post by varunendra on 2011-10-25
> **trebz32 said:**
> Hi, I'm using wubi with Ubuntu 11.4 installed. I was wondering if it's possible to run virtualbox and guest OS under this type of installation?
Definitely! Once booted, it is a normal installation with the only limitations being space (the size you chose while installing - default 16GB, max 30GB), no hibernation and performance.

### Post by giannisk88 on 2011-11-08
Hi. Is there any way to find previews versions of ubuntu with wubi install? i've installed 11.10 with wubi but i have a lot of problems with this new version.So I wonder if I could find ubuntu 11.04 wubi ?

(I searched it in the official ubuntu site but i can't find 11.04 wubi, it downloads only 11.10)

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-08
> **giannisk88 said:**
> Hi. Is there any way to find previews versions of ubuntu with wubi install? i've installed 11.10 with wubi but i have a lot of problems with this new version.So I wonder if I could find ubuntu 11.04 wubi ?

(I searched it in the official ubuntu site but i can't find 11.04 wubi, it downloads only 11.10)

[http://releases.ubuntu.com/11.04/](http://releases.ubuntu.com/11.04/)  (look at the bottom of the page for wubi.exe)
If you download the desktop cd iso and save it in the same directory as that version of wubi.exe it will find it and use it (same as running wubi.exe standalone but the plus side is then you have the ISO locally to rerun or create a CD/USB if you want to later).

---

### Post by giannisk88 on 2011-11-08
> **bcbc said:**
> [http://releases.ubuntu.com/11.04/](http://releases.ubuntu.com/11.04/)  (look at the bottom of the page for wubi.exe)
If you download the desktop cd iso and save it in the same directory as that version of wubi.exe it will find it and use it (same as running wubi.exe standalone but the plus side is then you have the ISO locally to rerun or create a CD/USB if you want to later).

Oh that's great.
Thank you very much for the quick answer.
I'll try it.
:)

Hi, one of my older PCs is showing the "You need to load the kernel first" issue, and I'd like to get rid of it.
I've tried to find more specific info and a resolve for my case, but it seems best to just post in this thread.

I've tried the "Permanent Fix" but the issue remained the same.

My previous Wubi Natty set-up didn't show this issue.

My old machine is set-up for multi-boot:
1. c: partition FAT32 with Win98se;
2. XP on NTFS in an extended DOS partition;
3. a fresh Wubi Oneiric installation on the c:-drive.

The issue is in fact that automatically trying to boot Oneiric results in returning to the Grub menu (without the boot countdown running).
Once returned to the Grub menu, I only have to press <Enter> to properly boot Oneiric.

My Grub menu has only the two default entries for a fresh Oneiric install.
I've tried to collect some info going into the Grub command line, and my disk entries are:
(memdisk) (hd0) (loop0) (hd0,msdos5) (hd0,msdos2) (hd1) (hd1,msdos1)..

The Grub root is (hd0,msdos2). This is _before_ selecting any boot option.
_After_ trying to boot automatically, the Grub root is (loop0).

> ls (hd0,msdos2)       then the list shows all files and directories in c:-root, including /ubuntu

> ls /ubuntu/disks       shows that the file 'root.disk' is present.

#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  set have_grubenv=true
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ "${prev_saved_entry}" ]; then
  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then
    saved_entry="${chosen}"
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n "${have_grubenv}" ]; then if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}

function load_video {
true
}

insmod part_msdos
insmod fat
set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 456d-100a
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/usr.disk
set root=(loop0)
if loadfont /share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=auto
  load_video
  insmod gfxterm
fi
terminal_output gfxterm
if [ "${recordfail}" = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
if background_color 44,0,30; then
  clear
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
if [ ${recordfail} != 1 ]; then
  if [ -e ${prefix}/gfxblacklist.txt ]; then
    if hwmatch ${prefix}/gfxblacklist.txt 3; then
      if [ ${match} = 0 ]; then
        set linux_gfx_mode=keep
      else
        set linux_gfx_mode=text
      fi
    else
      set linux_gfx_mode=text
    fi
  else
    set linux_gfx_mode=keep
  fi
else
  set linux_gfx_mode=text
fi
export linux_gfx_mode
if [ "$linux_gfx_mode" != "text" ]; then load_video; fi
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 3.0.0-12-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod fat
    set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 456d-100a
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-3.0.0-12-generic root=/dev/sda2 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash vt.handoff=7
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-3.0.0-12-generic
}
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 3.0.0-12-generic (recovery mode)' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod fat
    set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 456d-100a
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    echo    'Loading Linux 3.0.0-12-generic ...'
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-3.0.0-12-generic root=/dev/sda2 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro single 
    echo    'Loading initial ramdisk ...'
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-3.0.0-12-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###

### END /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-08
> **Pesmontis said:**
> Hi, one of my older PCs is showing the "You need to load the kernel first" issue, and I'd like to get rid of it.
I've tried to find more specific info and a resolve for my case, but it seems best to just post in this thread.

I've tried the "Permanent Fix" but the issue remained the same.

My previous Wubi Natty set-up didn't show this issue.

My old machine is set-up for multi-boot:
1. c: partition FAT32 with Win98se;
2. XP on NTFS in an extended DOS partition;
3. a fresh Wubi Oneiric installation on the c:-drive.

The issue is in fact that automatically trying to boot Oneiric results in returning to the Grub menu (without the boot countdown running). Once returned to the Grub menu, I only have to press <Enter> to properly boot Oneiric.
...
So it boots but only after the second time you select Ubuntu?

It doesn't sound like a wubildr issue. You might have better luck installing on an ntfs partition as it doesn't split your virtual disks up into root.disk, usr.disk and home.disk. Not that this is necessarily causing the problem, but it makes it more likely you'll run out of space as each of those is limited to 4GB on a FAT32 file system.
