### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-09
Pesmontis,
That "unknown command 'true'" does indicate some sort of wubildr issue - maybe a missing module.

But this seems pretty strange and might have more to do with the loop device being set to usr.disk initially and then replaced with root.disk for the boot.

You can update wubildr as follows:
```
sudo grub-install /dev/loop0
```

If that doesn't work, then yes, a custom entry is a good idea.
These are the lines you need to add to /etc/grub.d/40_custom and then run "sudo update-grub":
```
menuentry "Ubuntu 11.10 Latest kernel" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod fat
    set root='(hd0,msdos2)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 456d-100a
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux    /vmlinuz root=/dev/sda2 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash vt.handoff=7
    initrd    /initrd.img
}

```
You can create one for recovery as well.

Note: by using the /vmlinuz and /initrd.img you will always be booting the latest kernel as those files are linked to the /boot/vmlinuz-xxxx etc. whenever it's updated.


See this for more details: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Grub2#Creating_the_Custom_Menu](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Grub2#Creating_the_Custom_Menu)

PS can you file a bug on this?: [https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+filebug](https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+filebug)

### Post by Pesmontis on 2011-11-09
> **bcbc said:**
> .. That "unknown command 'true'" does indicate some sort of wubildr issue - maybe a missing module..
Nah, the function 'load_video' was messed-up:
```

function load_video {
true
}
```It should be:
```
function load_video {
  insmod vbe
  insmod vga
  insmod video_bochs
  insmod video_cirrus
}
```Maybe this mangling is caused by the "Permanet Fix" as described in the first post in this thread.
I'm sure that the last line should read "> sudo update-grub2" instead of "> sudo update-grub"

Anyway, I was worried that my Grub2 setup wasn't proper, so I reinstalled Grub2 and ran "> sudo update-grub2".
The resulting grub.cfg file contents are quite different from the contents I posted earlier (in the boot_info_script output).

(A side note: I think it's strange that 'grub2' is not marked as canonical in Synaptic Package Manager).

> **bcbc said:**
> .. might have more to do with the loop device being set to usr.disk initially and then replaced with root.disk for the boot..
Yes, I think this is definately the case. What I did is a meticulous check for things that need to be loaded from usr.disk, and things that need to be loaded from root.disk / saved on root.disk (grubenv).
Then I created two functions 'set_loop_usr' and 'set_loop_root' which I then applied multiple times -> I don't think it matters how often a switch is made between these virtual disks, as long as switching loop0 is done at the right lines in the grub.cfg file.
With these two functions, and with this meticulous checking & editing inside the grub.cfg file, I think I've got a working solution now, so I don't think I need to update the wubildr file.

> **bcbc said:**
> .. then yes, a custom entry is a good idea..
I shouldn't generalize, so I'll just say that in my particular case it definitely seems the way to go to just edit grub.cfg, despite being warned off..

Thanks for your explanation about editing  /etc/grub.d/40_custom and the specification of vmlinuz / initrd.img :D (now off to cooking up a bug report).

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-09
'true' is a way of saying "return true" and is inserted by grub script whenever a function is empty otherwise the grub.cfg syntax checker will reject grub.cfg.
The only reason they will be empty in 11.10 is that you ran the fixes described in post #1 which are no longer required - the problem that they were supposed to workaround was fixed for the release of 11.04 and then also retro fitted to 10.04.3 and 10.10.

sudo update-grub is the same as sudo update-grub2:
```
bcbc@ubuntu:~$ cat /usr/sbin/update-grub2
#!/bin/sh
set -e
exec grub-mkconfig -o /boot/grub/grub.cfg "$@"
bcbc@ubuntu:~$ cat /usr/sbin/update-grub
#!/bin/sh
set -e
exec grub-mkconfig -o /boot/grub/grub.cfg "$@"
bcbc@ubuntu:~$ 

```

I think that it is that switching of the loop devices. That was actually the problem that was addressed in post#1 and it was a bug recreating the loop device twice on root.disk. So maybe when that was fixed they broke something else. That's pure speculation. Better to get it in a bug report and then the info can be gathered to address it properly.

---

### Post by XCalibur54 on 2011-11-09
I have Ubuntu 11.10 installed through Wubi and I get the following error on startup:

> Try (hd0,0): NTFS5: No wubildr
Try (hd0,1): NTFS5: error: "prefix" is not set.

It flashes on the screen for about a half second (in fact I had to reboot three times to be able to read the whole thing) , then the system boots as normal with no problem.

I was just curious as to what this might be.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-09
> **XCalibur54 said:**
> I have Ubuntu 11.10 installed through Wubi and I get the following error on startup:



It flashes on the screen for about a half second (in fact I had to reboot three times to be able to read the whole thing) , then the system boots as normal with no problem.

I was just curious as to what this might be.

It's what you get when you run wubi. 

The first message is grub4dos looking for the wubildr file which is likely on C:\ but you have another partition before that without it. 
The second is something coming out of grub2 that is harmless and the grub developer decided not to bother about - been around since 11.04 was released.

---

The first message is grub4dos looking for the wubildr file which is likely on C:\ but you have another partition before that without it. 
The second is something coming out of grub2 that is harmless and the grub developer decided not to bother about - been around since 11.04 was released.

OK, I'm glad to hear it is nothing that could potentially become serious. :)

### Post by kdane4 on 2011-11-10
Can you upgrade WUBI using Alternate CD like the traditional install?

```
Upgrading Using the Alternate CD/DVD
Use this method if the system being upgraded is not connected to the Internet.

Download the alternate installation CD from http://releases.ubuntu.com/oneiric/
Burn the ISO to a CD and insert it into the CD-ROM drive of the computer to be upgraded.
If the ISO file is on the computer to be upgraded, you could avoid wasting a CD by mounting the ISO as a drive with a command like:
sudo mkdir -p /media/cdrom
sudo mount -o loop ~/Desktop/ubuntu-11.10-alternate-i386.iso /media/cdrom
A dialog will be displayed offering you the opportunity to upgrade using that CD.
Follow the on-screen instructions.
If the upgrade dialog is not displayed for any reason, you may also run the following command using Alt+F2:

gksu "sh /media/cdrom/cdromupgrade"
Or in Kubuntu run the following command using Alt+F2:

kdesudo "sh /media/cdrom/cdromupgrade"
```

the WUBI guide doesnt have any info regarding upgrades via ALT. CD.

[https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide)

```
Upgrading
Upgrading from 7.04 to 7.10 is NOT supported, due to the fundamental differences between 7.04 and 7.10. The best method is to uninstall and install Wubi-8.10 (you can save the old installation files and access them from 8.10) or move your 7.04 installation to a dedicated partition via LVPM, then upgrade using the standard upgrade-manager tool.

Upgrading from 7.10 to 8.04 might work, but has not been fully tested.

Upgrading from 8.04 to 8.10 is supported.

Upgrading from 8.10 to 9.04 is supported, but not to 9.10.

Upgrading from 9.04 to 9.10 is supported.

Upgrading from releases 10.04 and later is supported. Please review the applicable release notes before hand. Ensure you have sufficient free space, and consider backing up the \ubuntu\disks directory (from Windows) in case there are any errors, or you prefer to revert to the old release.
```

Thanks!! :)

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-10
> **kdane4 said:**
> Can you upgrade WUBI using Alternate CD like the traditional install?

Yes. It's advisable to use both the alternate CD and the internet to download the latest updates, but you could use the alternate alone as well.

You should back up your virtual disks (usually just the root.disk) and the C:\wubildr beforehand just in case, and make sure you have plenty of space available.

The main reason for backup is that upgrades do fail for whatever reason (worked for me, but that means little) - and also sometimes the new release might just not work properly on your hardware.

---

### Post by Niva on 2011-11-11
Hello, I'm here to report about my situation and ask how to go about fixing my wubi install.

I had to use wubi because a BIOS update to my Asus motherboard no longer allows linux CDs to be bootable.  I get a ISOLINUX et al Peter Anvin message and that's it.  So I managed to fix my mbr and have since been installing Ubuntu through wubi.

Today I restored my computer to another hard drive using a windows image (with Ubuntu installed.)

After booting up everything worked fine in Windows.

Checking linux I'm now confronted with the following message:
  **Try (hd 0, 0) : NTFS5: No wubildr**

After sitting there for about 30 seconds the computer proceeds to Grub and boots just fine into Ubuntu.  I had minor issues once in Ubuntu but they've been resolved now.  My only outstanding issue is getting rid of the message now.  

Any ideas as to what's causing this? 
I checked the C drive, wubildr and wubldr.mbr are both there with old dates, could something within those files be referencing the fact this is a different physical hard disk than the one it was on before?  

Thanks for any advice.

I tried the suggested front page solution of moving /boot/grub into /boot/grubold, restoring the environment and updating grub.  That led to no changes a tall.

I don't really want to have to reinstall all of Ubuntu just to get rid of that message.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-11
> **Niva said:**
> Hello, I'm here to report about my situation and ask how to go about fixing my wubi install.

I had to use wubi because a BIOS update to my Asus motherboard no longer allows linux CDs to be bootable.  I get a ISOLINUX et al Peter Anvin message and that's it.  So I managed to fix my mbr and have since been installing Ubuntu through wubi.

Today I restored my computer to another hard drive using a windows image (with Ubuntu installed.)

After booting up everything worked fine in Windows.

Checking linux I'm now confronted with the following message:
  **Try (hd 0, 0) : NTFS5: No wubildr**

After sitting there for about 30 seconds the computer proceeds to Grub and boots just fine into Ubuntu.  I had minor issues once in Ubuntu but they've been resolved now.  My only outstanding issue is getting rid of the message now.  

Any ideas as to what's causing this? 
I checked the C drive, wubildr and wubldr.mbr are both there with old dates, could something within those files be referencing the fact this is a different physical hard disk than the one it was on before?  

Thanks for any advice.

I tried the suggested front page solution of moving /boot/grub into /boot/grubold, restoring the environment and updating grub.  That led to no changes a tall.

I don't really want to have to reinstall all of Ubuntu just to get rid of that message.
You can't actually get rid of that message. It's not an error. It just means that there is no "wubildr" file in the root of the first partition. This is normal - I get it twice because my wubildr is on the 3rd partition.

However, for some people such as yourself, there is a lengthy delay between the message and Ubuntu loading. I honestly don't know what this is caused by and why it only happens to some people, but I'd guess that it's related to the physical position of the wubildr file on the partition. Or maybe fragmentation of the file. Just a guess.

So... tl;dr... you can use the following workaround to copy the wubildr to the first partition:
```
sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt
sudo cp /host/wubildr /mnt
sudo umount /mnt
```

That should speed up the process.

PS you can also try refreshing the wubildr first by running (that's loop plus a zero):
```
sudo grub-install /dev/loop0
```

The main reason for backup is that upgrades do fail for whatever reason (worked for me, but that means little) - and also sometimes the new release might just not work properly on your hardware.

Hello everyone.
I have 4mb adsl connection. Everything else is fine.

Now i started wubi installer and its estimated time is like 24 -22h. i gave it some time but its not improving.
Tried to install wubi by offline method (.iso file with wubi loader in same folder) but it didnt worked.
Any idea what to do??

---

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-11-12
Hi,
you need to be more specific please.

When you tried the offline method were there any error messages?

You can check for the Wubi error log in the %temp% folder.

Any other relevant information could also help us such as the specifications for the computer and which version of Windows you are running.

---

### Post by UsmanKhan on 2011-11-12
> **Rubi1200 said:**
> Hi,
you need to be more specific please.

When you tried the offline method were there any error messages?

You can check for the Wubi error log in the %temp% folder.

Any other relevant information could also help us such as the specifications for the computer and which version of Windows you are running.

WEll in short i had installed kubuntu 1 week before through wubi.(that install was perfect and quick and kubuntu ran smoothly but i didnt like the interface so wanted to install ubuntu and remove that wubi) using win 7 intel cor2d 2.2gh
2gb ram

In offline method wubi say "an error occured" and gives the path to log file in temp folder

---

### Post by kdane4 on 2011-11-12
> **bcbc said:**
> If you ever do wubi upgrade testing it's really painful running it over the net - it takes forever. The alternate CD ISO takes the sting out of that. And PS you don't need to burn it to a CD either.

Yup, and I don't really trust my wireless connection that's why I prefer the offline method. The  first time I installed WUBI (i just ran wubi.exe and let it download/install), I waited for hours only to receive SQUASHFS errors mid-installation. I prefer using torrents because it really saves a lot of time and I get to check the hash for consistency. :)

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-13
> **ObsceneJester said:**
> Hi, sorry to bother everyone however I'm having a little trouble trying to get Ubuntu onto my computer.

I keep getting **'Windows backend' object has no attribute 'cd_path'**
...

What is your F: drive? It isn't a CD but Wubi thinks it is:
```
11-12 23:43 DEBUG  CommonBackend: original_exe=F:\wubi.exe

11-12 23:43 DEBUG  WindowsBackend: drive=Drive(F: hd **876139**.960938 mb free ntfs)

11-12 23:43 DEBUG  Distro:   checking whether F:\ is a valid Ubuntu CD
11-12 23:43 DEBUG  Distro:   parsing info from str=Ubuntu 11.10 "Oneiric Ocelot" - Release amd64 (20111012)
11-12 23:43 DEBUG  Distro:   parsed info={'name': 'Ubuntu', 'subversion': 'Release', 'version': '11.10', 'build': '20111012', 'codename': 'Oneiric Ocelot', 'arch': 'amd64'}
11-12 23:43 INFO   Distro: Found a valid CD for Ubuntu: F:\
11-12 23:43 INFO   root: Running the CD menu...
```

Did you copy the desktop cd ISO into the root of F: because I think Wubi is confused. Try removing it from F:\ and stick wubi.exe and the ISO in the same non-root folder and run wubi from there. 

PS just noticed it also failed on D: which indicates that your CD is probably bad. This is common and can be from an interrupted download (the size of the iso doesn't match the download site listing) or a bad burn. So check this as well.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-13
> **ObsceneJester said:**
> Alright I tried to burn again but I kept getting the same thing.  This time I am trying to put it on my external HD and I got this

If F: is the same drive as shown in your Wubi log, then you shouldn't be trying to make it a bootable USB. That's intended for a usb stick  not a drive.

You can do a normal Ubuntu install from a usb stick but not Wubi. You can burn an Ubuntu desktop CD ISO to a DVD and install using Wubi, but you can't install Wubi with an Ubuntu DVD ISO.

The easiest way to install Wubi is without burning anything. Just place the downloaded desktop CD ISO in your downloads folder along with Wubi.exe and run wubi.exe from there. Make sure you also remove any other CD's or USB's that contain Ubuntu from your computer as wubi will find them and use them instead. That includes ISO files saved in the root of any drive.

If you want a CD/DVD so you can boot Ubuntu in live mode, or install direct, follow the instructions here: [http://www.ubuntu.com/download/ubuntu/download](http://www.ubuntu.com/download/ubuntu/download) (step 2, click on SHOW ME HOW).

---

### Post by dylwig on 2011-11-16
I dont know if this will help anybody but while I was installing 11.10 using Wubi, it would get through the checking file versions part of the install where my firewall would flag a file, I believe it was btloader.py or something with the .py extension. So I tried again and got the same flag and same error "Windows Backend object has no attribute 'iso-path' - see log for details." So finally I installed a fourth third time but when it asked me to download that .py extension which I believe does a hash check of the install files, I denied it and it installed no problem. After getting through the Hp Mini 210 touchpad issues I updated and am running no problem.:D

---

So I tried again and got the same flag and same error "Windows Backend object has no attribute 'iso-path' - see log for details." So finally I installed a fourth third time but when it asked me to download that .py extension which I believe does a hash check of the install files, I denied it and it installed no problem. After getting through the Hp Mini 210 touchpad issues I updated and am running no problem.:D
Hi and welcome to the forums :-)

Thanks for sharing this with us and hope you enjoy using Ubuntu.

### Post by God_of_War on 2011-11-18
Hi everybody, im new in this forum and im having a problem booting with unbutu.
Yeah ive read all the info and solutions u guys gave but what i want, hope don´t bother u guys, is just a _***c*****lear and straight **_answer(solution) to my problem, im not an expert at all, by the opposite,, would say a noob, but can undesratnd the most ure saying, just need a straight answer, that´s all, sorry for the text i know u guys put alot of effort to try to help people out and i appreciate and thank that effort.

Anyway, so, i installed unbutu with wubi installer to the C:\ drive, everything went fine during the instal, no error or mesages of error at all, when the installation is complete they asked if i wanna reboot now or later, i press now, then it appears the dual boot OS, VISTA or unbutu, i choose unbutu as i wanted, that´s why i installed it.
After i press unbutu it appears that: "Try(hd0,0):NTFS5:No Wubildr
                                                     "Try(hd0,1):NTFS5:([No wubildr]), -(im not sure if it says that where i put the[] beacause i didn´t had time to copy all the message even pressing the Pause/Break key, it just passes to fast, anyway, below that it doesn´t appear anything more but i guess after the second "Try" it says "error....." anything like that, dont know for sure, ill try get all the error later but i think u guys can give me a answer to fix this wiht this information i gave, then after that very brief error message( less then 1 sec) it goes to a black screen with a white blinking trace in the upper left corner of the screen, just like this:
[http://www.marlerblog.com/uploads/image/Win3x_Black_Screen_of_Death(3).gif]("http://www.marlerblog.com/uploads/image/Win3x_Black_Screen_of_Death%283%29.gif")

God_of_War,

You have the 'preinstalled' version of Ubuntu with Wubi. It's trying to do it's first boot but is failing - normally this is due to a graphics card incompatibility. What computer brand/mode/graphics card do you have?

Refer to this post (method 2) to modify the boot options: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10089820&postcount=8](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10089820&postcount=8)

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-23
> **God_of_War said:**
> Don´t think so, already made couple of disk check and it never found any error, made it in the morning.

Drop to an administrator command prompt: click Start, type "cmd", look above and right click "cmd.exe" and select Run as Administrator.

Enter the following:
```
dir /s \ubuntu
write \ubuntu\install\wubildr-disk.cfg
```

Copy and paste the results back here. (note: "write" is WordPad, it does not 'write' anything; if the file is not found you'll get an error message popup)

---

write \ubuntu\install\wubildr-disk.cfg
```Copy and paste the results back here. (note: "write" is WordPad, it does not 'write' anything; if the file is not found you'll get an error message popup)

### Post by lolpenguin on 2011-11-24
Hold on a moment, I don't think you guys understand Windows permissions that well. Even as an administrator, you cannot edit system files, even when you use the Run as Admin option.
IF C:\ubuntu\ does not exist, the obviously there is a problem with the file system.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-24
> **lolpenguin said:**
> Hold on a moment, I don't think you guys understand Windows permissions that well. Even as an administrator, you cannot edit system files, even when you use the Run as Admin option.
IF C:\ubuntu\ does not exist, the obviously there is a problem with the file system.

What do you think is a system file? The wubildr-disk.cfg is not.

I don't think there's anything obvious about this problem: apparently the user can see the C:\ubuntu\install directory from windows explorer, but the C:\ubuntu directory doesn't exist from the command prompt. So it's easy to say - yes it's file corruption - but not in any way that I've observed before. And apparently chkdsk doesn't find any corruption either.

Sometimes, the best of us make little typos or other mistakes - and it's important to rule these out before jumping to conclusions.

Now when I reboot as asked after finish the install Wizard and trying to boot from Ubuntu I got the following error message:
 
     Quote:

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-25
> **thinker.agustin said:**
> Hi Guys,

I just performed my very first installation of Wubi. exe where I installed Wubi 11.10 "ubuntu-11.10-desktop-i386.iso" on Windows XP Professional SP3, installing "Ubuntu".
Now when I reboot as asked after finish the install Wizard and trying to boot from Ubuntu I got the following error message:
 
     Quote:
                                                 Try (hd0,0): NTFS5: No wubildr
Try (hd0,1): NTFS5: error: "prefix" is not set.                                 

I saw that this was posted previously but in my case, it does not show just for a few seconds or couple of minutes, it finally never  boot and the screen stays showing the message "for ever".

I have just found information in related issues where an "Error Checking" was enough to overcome a similar issue, or I've read that the messages do not represent any harm...but does not fit to the unexpected behavior that I am facing.
Thank you very much in advance.
This suggests that the Wubildr.mbr is hanging. I recommend [filing a bug]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/wubi/+filebug").

You could try defragmenting and running chkdsk. This might help. Or another workaround is to copy the wubildr file (in C:\) to the root of first partition on the computer. You can either do this from a live CD, or if you can mount the first physical partition from Windows you can do it there (note some partitions are hidden in windows).

I have one of the "black screen" problems when booting 11.10 64bit wubi installation...  I have read through these threads and don't see a solution...  I did edit my wubildr-disk.cfg file and it now reads:
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
search --set=diskroot -f -n /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
probe --set=diskuuid -u $diskroot
linux /vmlinuz root=UUID=$diskuuid loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk preseed/file=/ubuntu/install/preseed.cfg wubi-diskimage ro quiet splash nomodeset
initrd /initrd.img
boot
I have a 6320 Radon card in a HP notebook and have installed with WUBI twice with the same results.  As soon as I do a reboot after the install is complete I get quite a bit of text that looks normal, no serious appearing error messages, but the screen goes to black and the keyboard is locked. Anyone have any idea's?

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-27
> **brienza112 said:**
> I recently downloaded and installed Wubi without any problems. When I hit reboot however, no option was presented on startup that allowed me to boot from Wubi, Windows XP booted as is normal on my PC. The instructions for installing Wubi show a boot loader, but I can't seem to access this specific screen. I have tried F12, and the most likely option I've seen is booting from the Hard Drive, but when this happens Windows XP is automatically booted. What should I do upon startup to bring up a boot loader menu that will let me use Wubi?
Right click on "My Computer", Properties, Advanced tab, Startup & Recovery settings. Change the time to display operating systems from zero to 10. (For some reason this seems to happen to a small percentage of users. An even smaller number are totally missing the Ubuntu boot entry, but check the timeout first).

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-27
> **JoeFriday49 said:**
> Correct, I never even get the system to boot...  Removing quiet splash did seem to make the purple screen stay up longer, but still nothing was displayed...
 
Before trying wubi I did boot off I did boot off a cd with version 10.04lts and it worked fine...  Maybe i should just install teh older version...

That seems like a reasonable idea - to use 10.04 if it's working. 

Bit strange though that 11.10 shouldn't even boot. Also I'd expect there wouldn't be a purple screen when you remove 'quiet splash'. Hold down the shift key when you boot Ubuntu and make sure it hasn't already created the grub menu - if it appears then override that instead of the wubildr-disk.cfg (which is only for the first boot).
You could also try an 11.04 CD to rule out some other issue.

### Post by StumpDK on 2011-11-28
I've searched this mega thread to see if this problem is mentioned before, but have not been able to find it. I've encountered this problem while using Wubi and Mint4Win, and while installing both Ubuntu, Kubuntu and Linux Mint. I think this information could help people trying to create a dual boot solution with Windows and Linux using the Wubi/Mint4Win installers.

**Problem**
As I installed Windows, I used the Windows installer to partition my hard drive, creating a partition for Windows and leaving some space for another partition to be used later.
As [this page]("http://www.sevenforums.com/installation-setup/10408-whats-100mb-partition-can-i-delete.html") indicates, creating partitions with the Windows installer results in an additional, hidden partion of 100 megabytes, marked in Windows Disk Manager as "System reserved" or something like that.
This partition is used to boot Windows. It can be deleted, but requires Windows installer to repair the system afterwards (this is also stated in the thread referred to above).
Why is this a problem?
When using Wubi/Mint4Win, the software assumes, that the C-drive is the first drive. This results in the following errors while booting and finishing the Linux installation:

[LIST]
[*]On boot: "Try hd(0,0): NTFS5: No wubidlr.
[*]After boot, when the installation finishes, this error pops up (closing it just launches another window with the same error): "No root file system is defined. Please correct this from the partitioning menu."
[/LIST]

As far as I'm concerned, most threads regarding thoose errors states that a workaround choosing manual configuration of the partions should help the problem. This is also true.
But it pretty much ruins the idea of an easy dual boot installation with Wubi. 

**Solution**
The problem lies in the fact that Linux searches for the system root on the unamed, unlettered and hidden Windows system partion, and not on the C-drive, where the necessary files are actually placed.
Some states that copying the needed files to the hidden Windows partition solves this problem, but for me this wasn't not working, as it solved the first problem, but I was stil getting the second error while the Linux-installer was trying to complete the installation.

My solution was to get rid of the system partition. This is done by installing Windows without creating the partions with the installer (of course, this requires that the partions are made before installing Windows, for example by using GPARTED in a Linux-system booted from USB). This however requires that Windows is installed again.
If you want to keep your Windows installation, you could try deleting the hidden system partition, as descriped in the thread referred to above. But I haven’t tried the solution, and can’t guarantee neither the solution or your data!

**Wish to the Wubi developers**
Is it possible to change the Wubi/Mint4Win installer so that Linux are looking for the files on the right partition? I think that would solve alot of problems for people setting up dual boots.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-28
StumpDK,

Theses are two separate issues. 1) 'no root filesystem' is usually due to partition table errors. 2) grub4dos finding the wubildr file on the root of a partition.

Even if the partition corruption affects 2), then copying wubildr to the system partition won't make a difference. Most likely you corrected the partition table errors when you removed the system partition.

I have a hidden system partition, no drive letter, etc. etc. and wubi loads fine - the wubildr file is actually on the 3rd partition.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-11-30
> **brienza112 said:**
> Yeah, it wasn't there. Should I just try reinstalling Wubi?


[boot loader]
timeout=10
default=multi(0)disk(0)rdisk(0)partition(2)\WINDOWS
[operating systems]
multi(0)disk(0)rdisk(0)partition(2)\WINDOWS="Microsoft Windows XP Home Edition" /fastdetect /NoExecute=OptIn
[spybotsd]
timeout.old=30
No don't reinstall. I've seen issues when there is something non-standard in the boot.ini. In your case it's the [spybotsd] entry (or maybe whatever is recording that wouldn't let Wubi.exe change the boot.ini - more likely).
Backup the boot.ini before editing, then edit as follows:
```
[boot loader]
timeout=10
default=multi(0)disk(0)rdisk(0)partition(2)\WINDOWS
[operating systems]
multi(0)disk(0)rdisk(0)partition(2)\WINDOWS="Microsoft Windows XP Home Edition" /fastdetect /NoExecute=OptIn
**C:\wubildr.mbr = "Ubuntu"**
[spybotsd]
timeout.old=30
```

---

### Post by brienza112 on 2011-11-30
> **bcbc said:**
> No don't reinstall. I've seen issues when there is something non-standard in the boot.ini. In your case it's the [spybotsd] entry (or maybe whatever is recording that wouldn't let Wubi.exe change the boot.ini - more likely).
Backup the boot.ini before editing, then edit as follows:
```
[boot loader]
timeout=10
default=multi(0)disk(0)rdisk(0)partition(2)\WINDOWS
[operating systems]
multi(0)disk(0)rdisk(0)partition(2)\WINDOWS="Microsoft Windows XP Home Edition" /fastdetect /NoExecute=OptIn
**C:\wubildr.mbr = "Ubuntu"**
[spybotsd]
timeout.old=30
```

### Post by bcbc on 2011-12-01
> **brienza112 said:**
> Thank you so much, it's working now. Is Wubi supposed to run slowly? My computer is old but it's significantly slower using Ubuntu right now, and I suspect it has something to do with loading from Windows.

If you have severe fragmentation or little free space, that will make it slower. Wubi is slower than a direct install, but I wouldn't expect it to be that much slower. Actually, I have noticed that the most recent releases of Ubuntu are more resource intensive, so if you don't have a lot of Ram it might just be slow. You could try a direct install for comparison purposes, or xubuntu/lubuntu which are less resource-intensive. (lubuntu isn't available on wubi).

### Post by JohnDillinger43 on 2011-12-08
I'm sure I haven't tried everything I should, and I'm sure I won't provide all the information I should, but I've been trying to repair my Wubi install for a couple weeks now, and nothing I do can get it to boot.  This is the first time I've used Linux, and I'm by no means an expert computer user.  I'm running Windows 7 and the Wubi install is Ubuntu 11.10.  From the Windows partition manager I believe my Windows partition is /dev/sda3 (laptop with just one hard disk, no partitions except the default three for Win7).

The problem started after a hard reboot of Windows (surprise surprise): my boot menu still gives me Windows 7 and Ubuntu options, but when I select Ubuntu it gives me the old "no wubildr" and sends me to grub.

I've tried booting from grub using
```
linux /boot/vmlinuz-3.1.2 root=/dev/sda3 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro
```
but it always tells me file not found.  I'm not sure if I have the kernel version correct, or if that's even the correct command to use.

I've tried mounting the file system after booting from a Live CD, using
```
sudo mkdir /media/win
sudo mount /dev/sda3 /media/win
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
```
but I got the "must specify filesystem" message.  After amending the last line to
```
sudo mount -t ntfs -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
```
I got a message saying
```
Failed to read bootsector (size=0)

```
and something to the effect of that it wasn't a valid NTFS file system.  I also tried ```
-t ntfs-3g
```, but that didn't make a difference.

I've replaced the wubildr file a couple times, both from a hosted version from this forum, and from the /ubuntu/winboot folder.  I can't find any grub.cfg file in the windows folders; from looking around I gather that's not accessible from the NTFS file system?

Any help is much appreciated; as I said I'm very new to Linux and don't know a heck of a lot about bootloaders or OS's in general.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-12-08
JohnDillinger43,
The root.disk has an ext4 file system, so -t ntfs wouldn't work anyway: -t ext4 is correct however if it's not detecting it automatically there is a problem. Most likely some corruption occurred with the hard shutdown (not sure why a win7 shutdown would affect the root.disk, but...)

So the first thing you should do is run chkdsk. Have a look at this for some more info (and after running chkdsk make sure the root.disk is still there): [http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html](http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html)

---

I may play around a bit to see if I can repair it for my own edification (I had already run chkdsk, and the root.disk file is still there), but probably I'll just uninstall and then repartition with gparted to set up a dual boot.

### Post by bcbc on 2011-12-13
hmm puzzling. What's also weird is that your grub.cfg only has an entry for the 2.6.32-24 kernel, but you supposedly have:
```
             =                boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-22-generic                 2
               =                boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-23-generic                 2
               =                boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-24-generic                11
               =                boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-25-generic                 3
               =                boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-26-generic                 4
               =                boot/vmlinuz-2.6.32-32-generic                 3
```
Did you lose grub2 along the way (i.e. install something like LVPM which replaces it with grub-legacy?). 
Can you try booting a different kernel... hit 'e' on the first entry and change the -24 to -32 and then Ctrl+X to boot?
Not sure it will make a difference, but it seems a bit strange. 

Also you could try changing the recovery entry from 'single nomodeset' to 'recovery nomodeset' and see whether that gives you a different result.

I'm assuming that it's not the ntfs partition but rather a problem on the root.disk itself.

---

I edited the other kernels out of the grub.cfg because of earlier problems. 24 was the only one that would boot.
No, I have never replaced grub with anything. I've not heard of LVPM. (BTW, I could always boot Windows fine.)

I tried editing the grub command to -32, and the recovery entry, both stop at the same NTFS error.

So, I then tried booting to the oldest kernel, -22 and it worked! Thanks!

Now, I guess I'd like to know how I can lock things to prevent this happening again?
Or maybe why the newer kernels will not boot?

### Post by bcbc on 2011-12-13
> **matt! said:**
> I edited the other kernels out of the grub.cfg because of earlier problems. 24 was the only one that would boot.
No, I have never replaced grub with anything. I've not heard of LVPM. (BTW, I could always boot Windows fine.)

I tried editing the grub command to -32, and the recovery entry, both stop at the same NTFS error.

So, I then tried booting to the oldest kernel, -22 and it worked! Thanks!

Now, I guess I'd like to know how I can lock things to prevent this happening again?
Or maybe why the newer kernels will not boot?
It could be fragmentation - the bootinfoscript reports "fragments" next to the kernel and initrd (but to be honest I don't know how this is determined or whether it means anything).  You could try regenerating the initramfs... e.g. for the -32 should be something like:
```
sudo update-initramfs -u -k 2.6.32-32-generic
```
But this doesn't explain why the normal trigger that created the original initrd failed in the first place.

---

======================== sda2/Wubi/boot/grub/grub.cfg: =========================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  set have_grubenv=true
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ "${prev_saved_entry}" ]; then
  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then
    saved_entry="${chosen}"
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n "${have_grubenv}" ]; then if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}

function load_video {
  insmod vbe
  insmod vga
  insmod video_bochs
  insmod video_cirrus
}

insmod part_msdos
insmod ntfs
set root='(hd0,msdos3)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 406CBD7B6CBD6BF2
loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
set root=(loop0)
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=auto
  load_video
  insmod gfxterm
  insmod part_msdos
  insmod ntfs
  set root='(hd0,msdos3)'
  search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 406CBD7B6CBD6BF2
  loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
  set root=(loop0)
  set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
  set lang=en_US
  insmod gettext
fi
terminal_output gfxterm
if [ "${recordfail}" = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
if background_color 44,0,30; then
  clear
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###
if [ ${recordfail} != 1 ]; then
  if [ -e ${prefix}/gfxblacklist.txt ]; then
    if hwmatch ${prefix}/gfxblacklist.txt 3; then
      if [ ${match} = 0 ]; then
        set linux_gfx_mode=keep
      else
        set linux_gfx_mode=text
      fi
    else
      set linux_gfx_mode=text
    fi
  else
    set linux_gfx_mode=keep
  fi
else
  set linux_gfx_mode=text
fi
export linux_gfx_mode
if [ "$linux_gfx_mode" != "text" ]; then load_video; fi
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 3.0.0-12-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,msdos3)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 406CBD7B6CBD6BF2
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-3.0.0-12-generic root=UUID=406CBD7B6CBD6BF2 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro   quiet splash vt.handoff=7
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-3.0.0-12-generic
}
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 3.0.0-12-generic (recovery mode)' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,msdos3)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set=root 406CBD7B6CBD6BF2
    loopback loop0 /ubuntu/disks/root.disk
    set root=(loop0)
    echo    'Loading Linux 3.0.0-12-generic ...'
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-3.0.0-12-generic root=UUID=406CBD7B6CBD6BF2 loop=/ubuntu/disks/root.disk ro single 
    echo    'Loading initial ramdisk ...'
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-3.0.0-12-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_lupin ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###

if [ -f  $prefix/custom.cfg ]; then
  source $prefix/custom.cfg;
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###

============================= sda2/Wubi/etc/fstab: =============================

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
# Use 'blkid' to print the universally unique identifier for a
# device; this may be used with UUID= as a more robust way to name devices
# that works even if disks are added and removed. See fstab(5).
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>
proc            /proc           proc    nodev,noexec,nosuid 0       0
/host/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /               ext4    loop,errors=remount-ro 0       1
/host/ubuntu/disks/swap.disk none            swap    loop,sw         0       0
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

               =                boot/grub/grub.cfg                             1

=========================== sdb1/boot/grub/grub.cfg: ===========================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

if loadfont /boot/grub/font.pf2 ; then
    set gfxmode=auto
    insmod efi_gop
    insmod efi_uga
    insmod gfxterm
    terminal_output gfxterm
fi

set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray

menuentry "Try Ubuntu without installing" {
    set gfxpayload=keep
    linux    /casper/vmlinuz  file=/cdrom/preseed/ubuntu.seed boot=casper quiet splash --
    initrd    /casper/initrd.lz
}
menuentry "Install Ubuntu" {
    set gfxpayload=keep
    linux    /casper/vmlinuz  file=/cdrom/preseed/ubuntu.seed boot=casper only-ubiquity quiet splash --
    initrd    /casper/initrd.lz
}
menuentry "Check disc for defects" {
    set gfxpayload=keep
    linux    /casper/vmlinuz  boot=casper integrity-check quiet splash --
    initrd    /casper/initrd.lz
}

### Post by bcbc on 2011-12-15
> **FaceToChair said:**
> I created a new disk in wubi using this method [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How_do_I_create_a_virtual_disk_in_Ubuntu.3F](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How_do_I_create_a_virtual_disk_in_Ubuntu.3F)

But how do I use the new virtual disk? When I check how much memory I have it says I still have the same amount.

That code is equivalent to creating and formatting a partition. It doesn't mean you are using it. You could e.g. move /home to a separate virtual disk which frees up space on your root.disk, but there is a separate script to do this. Or you could resize your root.disk to make it bigger - again there's a separate script for this. If you just want to use your new virtual disk as a storage space, you could add an entry in /etc/fstab to mount it on startup - or mount it manually:
```
sudo mkdir /media/newdisk
sudo mount -o loop /host/ubuntu/disks/newdisk.disk /media/newdisk
```

However, until you move data from your current install to the new virtual disk, you won't see any space freed up.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-12-15
> **MeetM said:**
> 
M not able to boot into Ubuntu...It displays  a blank screen which lasts forever. It was working fine few days back.. Please help
It's pretty risky using Wubi on a computer with dynamic disks. I don't advise it as linux isnt' aware of them. Whether this has anything to do with it not booting - well I doubt it. But you don't have any older kernels to boot either.

You could try hitting 'e' on the first entry, remove quiet splash and add nomodeset and then hit Ctrl+X to boot. See if there is a meaningful failure message. Or run the recovery instead, but edit that entry as well replacing 'single' with 'recovery nomodeset' before hitting Ctrl+X. 

My advice - recover your data using an ubuntu cd or a tool like [http://ext2read.blogspot.com/](http://ext2read.blogspot.com/) and then uninstall Ubuntu. First get rid of the dynamic drives before dual booting, or use a virtual machine. If you were to e.g. create a new virtual disk on the /host you could easily destroy data if there were some hidden dynamic partitions that linux is unaware of.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-12-16
> **MeetM said:**
> After i removed quiet splash in Normal mode and recovery mode, it displayed ```
Loading initial ram disk....
```
After that I inserted an echo test command at the last line and it
displayed the echo message so i guess it didn't got stuck at initializing ram disk. Then i read this blog [http://ubuntuliving.blogspot.com/2010/03/way-out-of-all-those-wubi-problems.html](http://ubuntuliving.blogspot.com/2010/03/way-out-of-all-those-wubi-problems.html) .
He has wubildr for 9.10. But anyways, i had previously taken a backup of wubildr files. So i tried with them but no change. Thanx

The 9.10 thing won't work anymore - it's just for 9.10.

I have no idea what's problem you have. But, in general with Wubi, you can try:
1. 'chkdsk /r' from windows. You can run it using the 'drive tools' menu, select the drive that holds the \ubuntu directory (in your case probably C: and then reboot to complete)

2. Run fsck from an Ubuntu CD or USB that you boot from (and select "Try Ubuntu"). In your case, you'd drop to a terminal (Ctrl+Alt+T) and run:
```
sudo mount /dev/sda2 /mnt
sudo fsck -fv /mnt/ubuntu/disks/root.disk
```
(The -f option forces an fsck even if the filesystem appears clean, since in your case, the bootinfoscript didn't have a problem mounting and reading it. -v means "verbose output". You can also add -y to let fsck fix automatically)

3. You have to chroot into the wubi install and try to repair it. This is required if e.g. your initrd.img is messed - and it could be based on the symptoms you are describing. This is a pain and might be an opportune moment to recover data and reinstall with a normal dual boot.

### Post by brushman on 2011-12-31
I tried to install xubuntu on a vista machine using wubi, but after the first reboot, I get a glitched out screen (graphics are all messed up). The screen glitches out after or during the blue xubuntu loading screen where the little bar goes back and forth.

I was able to avoid the screen by instead going into safe graphics mode by pressing the escape key when xubuntu is highlighted in the boot options. However, after finishing installation when I reboot it just freezes at the blue xubuntu loading screen.

I completely tried reinstalling xubuntu with wubi and I get the same problem.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-12-31
> **brushman said:**
> I tried to install xubuntu on a vista machine using wubi, but after the first reboot, I get a glitched out screen (graphics are all messed up). The screen glitches out after or during the blue xubuntu loading screen where the little bar goes back and forth.

I was able to avoid the screen by instead going into safe graphics mode by pressing the escape key when xubuntu is highlighted in the boot options. However, after finishing installation when I reboot it just freezes at the blue xubuntu loading screen.

I completely tried reinstalling xubuntu with wubi and I get the same problem.
Check out [this thread]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1613132"). (post #8 is wubi-specific for install, after install see post #1). Probably you need nomodeset and then a custom driver installed.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-12-31
> **xircon said:**
> Trying to install wubi into a dual boot system (win7/Debian sid) and I am not getting the boot menu - it boots direct to win7.


Your bootinfoscript doesn't show any Wubi files. Maybe you ran it after uninstalling wubi?

Normally if you install Wubi and it boots straight to Windows, it's a windows boot manager issue (with the timeout). But on recent Wubi releases it boots straight into the Wubi install on the first reboot (only Vista/7) regardless of timeout. You can still have the timeout issue but only after the first Ubuntu boot.

So not really sure what the issue is. Please try again and if still having a problem post the Wubi log from the *%temp%* directory (wubi-11.10-rev24x.log)

---

### Post by bcbc on 2011-12-31
xircon,
So you have grub boot loader so boot, select windows, and it should boot direct to the Wubi and kick off the installation (runs ubiquity). 

If it doesn't go to a windows administrator command prompt and run 'bcdedit'. Make sure there is an entry for Ubuntu. You can also check in 'Startup & recovery settings'.  Just avoid setting Ubuntu as the default OS from there. If it's not working please post the bcdedit output. Thanks

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-01-01
xircon,

It looks like you have 2 bcd stores, one on /dev/sda2 (the partition with the boot flag set) and one on /dev/sda3 (I believe that's the one you're displaying). If the /dev/sda3 was being used it should always show and wait for prompt (even before you installed Wubi as it has the "displaybootmenu" set to "Yes").

On my machine (also dell), the /boot/bcd is not on my C: drive (/dev/sda3) - it's only on /dev/sda2.

I don't know a whole lot about BCD - but if this is your problem then you will probably also have issues resuming from hibernation in Windows. I would imagine the fix is pointing windows at the /boot/bcd on /dev/sda2 but that's probably a question for a windows forum. (I'm not sure how to do this)

---

Will investigate.  Never use W7, was going to install wubi to back up my debian install.

### Post by markuska on 2012-01-07
Hi,
I had wubi installed and after upgrading I could not boot ubuntu. I would get the no wubildr error but even after minutes it wouldn't boot. I moved /ubuntu/winboot/wubildr to c and and now it boots to grub.  I tried to boot from grub and that did not work.  I also booted from a live usb and tried to edit the grub.cfg file and could not. The second mount instruction would not work.  I'm guessing that is because root.disk is not present in /ubuntu/disks/ shown in the boot summary below.  Here is my output from boot info script.  Any help would be appreciated.

```

============================= Boot Info Summary: ===============================

 => Windows is installed in the MBR of /dev/sda.
 => Syslinux MBR (4.04 and higher) is installed in the MBR of /dev/sdb.

sda1: __________________________________________________________________________

    Boot sector type:  Dell Utility: FAT16

                       /ubuntu/disks/swap.disk

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2012-01-07
Hi and welcome to the forums markuska :)

Yes, the root.disk does indeed appear to be missing.

You can read more about this and possible ways to recover the root.disk in this great blog posting by experienced forum member and Wubi expert bcbc:
[http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html](http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html)

Let us know if this helps and/or you require further assistance.

---

### Post by markuska on 2012-01-07
Hi,

I checked for found.x directories and there were 3.  000, 001, and 002.  The only one with directories in it was 001, however the dates don't match up with my installation.  The 002 folder has about 400 files in it, and a lot of them have size 0.  Any idea how to find out if one of them is the root.disk?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-01-08
> **markuska said:**
> Hi,

I checked for found.x directories and there were 3.  000, 001, and 002.  The only one with directories in it was 001, however the dates don't match up with my installation.  The 002 folder has about 400 files in it, and a lot of them have size 0.  Any idea how to find out if one of them is the root.disk?

It's a bit unusual to have so many recovered files (something unstable with your system?). 

The root.disk would be a large file - with 11.10 the minimum size is 5GB and with earlier releases it's 3GB although in practice most installs are larger. So that's a good guideline to try to identify it. Since it's not the \ubuntu\disks folder that's gone, you're looking for a single file named file0000.chk where 0000 could be any number.

---

### Post by markuska on 2012-01-08
Well the largest file in found.002 is 1024280 bytes, no where close to 5 Gigs.  There are also several other files close in size. Two more are about 800000 bytes and there are about 20 others over 100000 bytes.  Should I attempt to move the largest file and overwrite the root.disk currently in \ubuntu\disks which is corrupted?  Or is it save to say that recovering my root.disk is a lost cause?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-01-08
If there is still a root.disk in \ubuntu\disks then absolutely do not overwrite it.

So... you have to run chkdsk /r  (or /f) and make sure it runs completely. If \ubuntu is on C:\ then you'll need to reboot to complete chkdsk. If after that the root.disk is still in \ubuntu\disks then try booting it again. If that fails, you can run a new bootinfoscript and see if it reports any filesystem errors. In that case you should run fsck on the root.disk from a live CD.

---

fsck: Error 2 while executing fsck.ntfs for /dev/sda2

```

And again is the boot info script result for sda2:

### Post by bcbc on 2012-01-08
you have to fsck the root.disk, not the partition. But the bootinfoscript still isn't reporting the root.disk which makes me believe there is still corruption (ntfs).

---

### Post by markuska on 2012-01-08
Well it doesn't seem that I am going to be able to recover the root.disk.  And I don't have the time to explore everything so I am just going to proceed by reinstalling wubi.  However, when I do reinstall wubi how do I make it so this problem does not come up again?  That is, how do I update wubi properly?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-01-08
> **markuska said:**
> Well it doesn't seem that I am going to be able to recover the root.disk.  And I don't have the time to explore everything so I am just going to proceed by reinstalling wubi.  However, when I do reinstall wubi how do I make it so this problem does not come up again?  That is, how do I update wubi properly?

One way you can corrupt files (and file systems) is to perform hard shutdowns. If that's the reason for the corruption then just avoid that. If it's some other cause, then it's probably going to occur again. Wubi won't work very well for you if that's the case.

---

### Post by markuska on 2012-01-08
I agree so I went ahead and uninstalled wubi and partitioned and installed ubuntu separately.  Thanks for the help anyways.  Is there anything similar I need to worry about with a dual boot instead of wubi?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-01-08
> **markuska said:**
> I agree so I went ahead and uninstalled wubi and partitioned and installed ubuntu separately.  Thanks for the help anyways.  Is there anything similar I need to worry about with a dual boot instead of wubi?

File system corruption isn't unique to Wubi. But if the corruption was caused by something on Windows then your new Ubuntu install will be safe from that. If your install freezes use [Alt+SysRq R-E-I-S-U-B]("en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magic_SysRq_key#.E2.80.9CREISUB.E2.80.9D_.E2.80.93_safe_reboot") to safely reboot.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-01-09
> **lolz1234 said:**
> Hi, I'm trying to uninstall Ubuntu (It isn't booting correctly), but it won't allow me to. The message I get is in the attachments. I'm kind of a newbie at this, but any advice?

It's trying to delete the "Ubuntu" entry in the windows boot manager, but it can't find it. This can be because it's already deleted manually (unlikely), or it can happen if you did a system restore (more likely). It may be possible that Wubi did a partial uninstall, or perhaps that the BCD is no longer visible to Windows (which [can occur sometimes unrelated to Wubi]("http://askubuntu.com/questions/73585/wubi-installer-error").) 

You might be better off following the manual uninstall instructions at [https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#Uninstallation](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#Uninstallation)

I have a problem with Ubuntu installed with Wubi. Basically, my performance is awful. Some applications keeps crashing for some reason, for example Firefox(right now I'm using Chrome, but I'd still like to use FF :( ) and Software Center(plus many other apps. 

Should I do some kind of clean install over this one? 

Btw, I have 6GB of RAM(DDR3) and 2.4GHz Intel i5 dual-core processor, so that should be more than enough. 

I know Ubuntu is better. I used it "back in the 7.x days", and it was blazing fast. Right now, even Windows 7 is faster. 

I hope someone can answer :P Thanks in advance.
This is not typical for Wubi. I would guess there is a hardware incompatibility or something has installed badly. Maybe review your logs /var/log/syslog and /var/log/dmesg to see if something is failing. As far as firefox goes I'd expect a corrupt profile (?).
Reinstalling will clear up any corruption, but not resolve any hardware problems. It will also delete the entire wubi install and replace it (data/programs/settings).

Do I need to reinstall... redo the grub, I found the root file. Too many bits and piece to connect to make it clear.

I don't see XP - it says you have vista/7 installed. There's no boot.ini (typical with XP). 
Also, do NOT install grub with Wubi. It's dangerous and depending on the version can destroy the windows bootsector in addition to preventing any OS from booting.

So, since you are running XP but there's no trace of it in the bootinfoscript I don't know what to suggest. If in fact you're running vista or 7, then go to an administrator command prompt and enter: bcdedit
and post the result.

Also, please include the wubi log file (found in the %temp% directory). It will be called something like wubi-9.10-....log

PS I don't recommend 9.10 wubi. Apart from the fact that 9.10 is no longer supported, the Wubi version is known to fail after updating your kernels (that might not be an issue if you can't update).

### Post by bcbc on 2012-01-23
> **letank said:**
> bcbc,

Here nothing happens until you press CTRL-D and it returns to the previous first listed message.

Okay, I was a little confused. I thought you were doing a new install (and you saying it's XP; still a little confused why you mentioned the XP). Anyway, since the root.disk file system has a problem it's probably a good idea to fsck it. First I recommend running "chkdsk /r" from Windows to make sure there is no ntfs corruption, then boot the live CD, mount the windows partition and fsck the root.disk:

```
sudo mount /dev/sda3 /mnt
sudo fsck -f /mnt/ubuntu/disks/root.disk
```
(-f means force check even if it appears clean)

PS before you fsck, you could try to mount it to recover important data, but it's important that you unmount if before running fsck.

See [here]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How_can_I_access_my_Wubi_install_and_repair_my_install_if_it_won.27t_boot.3F") for more info

### Post by mayankgupta2005 on 2012-01-27
Hi,

> **Rubi1200 said:**
> 

Run the following commands:
```
sudo mkdir /media/win 
sudo mount /dev/sda**[COLOR=Red]1[/COLOR]** /media/win
sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt
sudo cp -p /media/win/wubildr /media/win/wubildr.save
sudo cp -p /media/win/ubuntu/winboot/wubildr /media/win
```This example again assumes sda1, but change to reflect your actual Windows partition e.g. sda2, sda3 etc.
Reboot.




When I run the command ```
 sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt 
``` I get an error - "Segmentation Fault". Any leads on this?

Thanks,
Mayank.

---

### Post by mayankgupta2005 on 2012-01-27
> **mayankgupta2005 said:**
> Hi,




When I run the command ```
 sudo mount -o loop /media/win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /mnt 
``` I get an error - "Segmentation Fault". Any leads on this?

Thanks,
Mayank.

Figured it out. Just ran the fsck command on root.disk.

Thanks,
Mayank

### Post by bigmat65 on 2012-02-01
I'm not entirely sure if this is the forum to ask this on, but I installed Wubi for my dad on his laptop since he wanted to try it out after I put a dual boot on my computer with Ubuntu and Windows 7 and I'm not very practiced with Ubuntu just yet (old windows habits die hard) and one day he tried to uninstall it, but instead of using the uninstaller he just highlighted the file in windows and hit the delete key, but was confused when the root.disk file would not delete (since it is a DISK file and is just space on the hard drive) he then preceded to delete the items in the recycle bin and came and asked me to get it off his computer. I tried reinstalling Wubi hoping to get back the uninstaller, but it won't so I tried partition editors, both gparted and the editor in windows, I know it is a file in windows but I had to look, with no success. I ran chkdsk to no avail and am at my last guess of defragging the drive, but I wanted to know if there was any other way to remove the root.disk file and give the space back, he used the max which I think was 30gigs.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-02-02
> **bigmat65 said:**
> I'm not entirely sure if this is the forum to ask this on, but I installed Wubi for my dad on his laptop since he wanted to try it out after I put a dual boot on my computer with Ubuntu and Windows 7 and I'm not very practiced with Ubuntu just yet (old windows habits die hard) and one day he tried to uninstall it, but instead of using the uninstaller he just highlighted the file in windows and hit the delete key, but was confused when the root.disk file would not delete (since it is a DISK file and is just space on the hard drive) he then preceded to delete the items in the recycle bin and came and asked me to get it off his computer. I tried reinstalling Wubi hoping to get back the uninstaller, but it won't so I tried partition editors, both gparted and the editor in windows, I know it is a file in windows but I had to look, with no success. I ran chkdsk to no avail and am at my last guess of defragging the drive, but I wanted to know if there was any other way to remove the root.disk file and give the space back, he used the max which I think was 30gigs.
If you can see the root.disk file within the \ubuntu\disks directory but cannot delete it, then likely there is some filesystem corruption. What message do you get when you attempt to delete?

If there is corruption you'll need to run chkdsk to correct it before you can delete it. Follow the instructions on this page: [http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html](http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html)
(That's intended to recover the root.disk, but the same principle applies. If it gets repaired and moved to C:\found.000 you'll need to delete it from there to free up the 30GB space). If it gets repaired in place, you can just delete it from the \ubuntu directory.

Also review the instructions to [manually remove Wubi]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How_do_I_manually_uninstall_Wubi.3F").

---

> If you can see the root.disk file within the \ubuntu\disks directory but cannot delete it, then likely there is some filesystem corruption. What message do you get when you attempt to delete?

If there is corruption you'll need to run chkdsk to correct it before you can delete it. Follow the instructions on this page: [http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html](http://ubuntu-with-wubi.blogspot.com/2011/08/missing-rootdisk.html)
(That's intended to recover the root.disk, but the same principle applies. If it gets repaired and moved to C:\found.000 you'll need to delete it from there to free up the 30GB space). If it gets repaired in place, you can just delete it from the \ubuntu directory.

Also review the instructions to [manually remove Wubi]("https://wiki.ubuntu.com/WubiGuide#How_do_I_manually_uninstall_Wubi.3F").

Ok, I went through the guide to the manual uninstall and reran chkdsk, this time it found something and fixed it and I deleted it through the command prompt. Thanks for the help. I don't know why it missed the file corruption the first time.

I have downloaded Ubuntu 11.10 ISO 4 times and have used a friend's LiveCD, so I am 100% sure that it is not a corrupted file. I have also gone into the C:\ubuntu\ file and wubildr.mbr is not missing or corrupted. Also, this doesn't relate to Wubi but it may help. When I run Ubuntu through Virtual Box, it is able to start up and start copying files until (this happens every time at the same place) I get this error: A critical error has occured while running the virtual machine and the machine execution has been stopped. If i choose to ignore this and continue, this error pops up:The virtual machine may run into an error condition as described below: Unable to allocate and lock memory. The virtual machine will be paused. Please close applications to free up memory or close the VM. It then pauses. I have literally allocated half of my system's RAM to it (4 GB) and it still refuses to work. I hope there is an easy fix to this that will not require a full system wipe and reinstall, since this computer is less than a year old now. Please notify me (since I am such a n00b at this) if I am posting in the wrong forum. Thank you, PortableSounds
The "missing or corrupted" error means that wubildr.mbr is not found. You can see it in Windows but when you are booting, it needs to be called using bios functions and it's not accessible. Why that is? I don't know. Could be a number of reasons e.g. some older bioses can't read past 137GB from the start of the disk. Doesn't seem likely if it's a new computer though.

As for the virtual box issue it's likely a totally different issue. Not really sure there either. I've been running a number of virtual machines using vmware player. Not really used virtual box much (actually just trying win 8 beta on virtual box).

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-03-01
> **tbhatia4 said:**
> Hello,
Everyone was using wubi in my windows installation earlier but my windows got corrupted and i had to resindatll but the major issue was to save my ubuntu so i copied trhe root.disk as suggested by many threads and replace it with the new installlation in the d: drive where my new ubuntu installation exists but the issue i am facing after replacing the root file is that it gets stuck at a root screen and asks for the ubuntu login which i am quite sure i have entered correctly but of no use.
although after replacing the root file with the actual installation it works fine......
kindly help me out!!!!
Boot the new install that's working - when you see the grub menu, hit 'E' on the first entry and write down what you see (carefully). In particular you care about the lines:
```
set root='(xxx)'
and
linux /vmlinuz- root=UUID=yyyyy

```

Then switch to your backup root.disk, and boot, again hitting 'E' when you see the grub menu. Make sure that xxx and yyyy are the same as the working install. Then hit Ctrl+X to boot.
When booted, drop to a terminal (Ctrl+Alt+T) and run:
```
sudo update-grub
```

That bootinfoscript is a lot better. You have C:\wubildr and the (uninitialized) virtual disks.

When you restart your computer it's supposed to try to boot Ubuntu automatically (the first time only) and it should say:
```
Completing the Ubuntu installation
For more installation options, press ESC now 5...4...3...2...1
```

If you don't press anything it should kick off the installer automatically, run completely unattended (no user input) while showing a slideshow, and then reboot. After that, you should see the option of Windows or Ubuntu. When you select Ubuntu you should see the grub menu, and selecting the first entry should boot.

So... can you just verify what is happening that is different?

Hi there,

I am wondering if there is anyway possible to boot into a Wubi installation without the Windows bootloader?

I have had a dual boot for some time which has been working great, until my primary SSD drive crashed. The Wubi installation I have on my second drive which I now can't boot into.

I tried installing lilo (among other things) but it was a no go. I really don't see a reason it wouldn't be possible. I just can't seem to figure it out. The disk (/dev/sda) is set as bootable.

I am currently using a bootable USB and I would be overjoyed if someone could point me in the right direction to solve this.

Thanks!

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-03-27
> **enzipher said:**
> Hi there,

I am wondering if there is anyway possible to boot into a Wubi installation without the Windows bootloader?

I have had a dual boot for some time which has been working great, until my primary SSD drive crashed. The Wubi installation I have on my second drive which I now can't boot into.

I tried installing lilo (among other things) but it was a no go. I really don't see a reason it wouldn't be possible. I just can't seem to figure it out. The disk (/dev/sda) is set as bootable.

I am currently using a bootable USB and I would be overjoyed if someone could point me in the right direction to solve this.

Thanks!
Yes you can boot it using grub2 from a normal Ubuntu install. I doubt you can do this with lilo - the bootloader needs to be able to loop mount the root.disk to extract the kernel and initrd.img. Grub2 can do this, whereas grub legacy couldn't. (You'd need to manually setup the custom grub2 menu entry to boot from the root.disk).

I've never tried to directly boot a root.disk from a live CD/USB - not sure if that would be possible.

I would suggest migrating from the root.disk to a normal dual boot on the second drive. First create the ext4 partition to hold the install, and then a swap partition, and then migrate using the *--root-disk=* options of this script: [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1519354)
Afterwards, just boot from that second drive.

### Post by bcbc on 2012-03-30
I was hoping to see the physical location of the initrd.img and kernel files. But bootinfoscript didn't determine these for some reason.

In some older bioses files that are beyond 137GB from the start of the disk are not visible to bios functions and cannot be booted. Can you just confirm that the files are visible. Hit 'C' from the grub menu, then:
```
ls /boot
```
Make sure you can see initrd.img-3.0.0-17-generic
Then hit ESC to return to the menu.

If you could see them, then hit 'E' to edit the first entry. Remove 'splash vga=786  quiet splash vt.handoff=7' and hit CTRL+X to boot. See where it fails.

### Post by bcbc on 2012-04-02
zlatko23,

You need to locate the root.disk... if it was recovered separately it would be a large file (> 5GB) named chk0000.chk (or chk????.chk). There may also be more than one \found.??? folder. So check for it first.

Usually in cases where the whole \ubuntu\disks directory is recovered to dir0000.chk, both the root.disk and swap.disk are present. But there have been cases where the root.disk alone is missing and has not been recovered - perhaps the corruption was too severe. 
So, I suppose it's possible that this is the case here.

I can't really offer much more advice than that - most of what I know is based on other users having corruption and their report of what happened (since it hasn't happened to me personally). But the plain fact is, that if you cannot locate the root.disk there is no way (I am aware of) to recover data off it.

Don't uninstall the -15 kernel - it's your only working kernel now.

Did you run that command I showed from the grub prompt?
1. Boot computer
2. Select Ubuntu
3. Hit 'C' to get to a grub prompt as soon as you see the grub menu
4. enter 
```
ls /boot
```
or
```
ls (loop0)/boot
```
(Press Esc to return to grub menu.)

Report back and let me know if you can see the -17 kernel and initr.img.

Thanks

### Post by bcbc on 2012-04-06
There is a bug in 11.10 wubi where the recovery mode doesn't work properly... you have to edit it 'E' before booting and delete the first line:
```
	set gfxpayload=$linux_gfx_mode

```
Then Ctrl+X to boot.

I'm wondering whether there is some graphics card issue. Some people have had backlight/blank screens.

Anyway, maybe you can explore that if the following doesn't work... to purge and reinstall the kernel:
```
sudo apt-get remove --purge linux-image-3.0.0-17-generic
sudo apt-get install linux-image-3.0.0-17-generic

```

after executing the command:

sudo mount -o loop /win/ubuntu/disks/root.disk /vdisk
I  get a message as, "mount: you must specify the filesystem type"... 

I tried -t ntfs-3g,ext2,3,4 .. no good

I just want to recover files..what should I do?
This indicates filesystem corruption so you should fsck the root.disk. 

(Also, if you recovered the root.disk from a file in the \found.000 directory named chk0000.chk, make sure you got the real root.disk - the size should be 5 to 30 GB depending on what you selected at install time.)

---

### Post by sffvba[e0rt on 2012-04-19
Quick question to the Wubi experts.

I had an issue installing Ubuntu 11.10 64-bit by downloading the wubi.exe file and having it download the rest needed to install the distro.  When I downloaded an ISO of 11.04 64-bit and placed it in the same folder as wubi.exe the install went without hitches.

Anybody had similar issues like this and do you know if it is a known bug or not?


Cheers
404

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-04-19
> **not found said:**
> Quick question to the Wubi experts.

I had an issue installing Ubuntu 11.10 64-bit by downloading the wubi.exe file and having it download the rest needed to install the distro.  When I downloaded an ISO of 11.04 64-bit and placed it in the same folder as wubi.exe the install went without hitches.

Anybody had similar issues like this and do you know if it is a known bug or not?


Cheers
404
There's bug [lpbug]862003[/lpbug]. This bug shows up as a Permission denied on *x*:\wubildr (x: is some drive letter) right at the end of the install, but the install itself is actually successful. You can check the wubi log file in %TEMP% (wubi-11.10-rev245.log) to confirm whether this was the problem.

It has been fixed for 12.04.

Also, the standalone wubi.exe in 11.10 won't install on a FAT32 partition (only NTFS). That's also been fixed for 12.04. Bug [lpbug]882393[/lpbug]

---

### Post by sffvba[e0rt on 2012-04-19
> **bcbc said:**
> There's bug [lpbug]862003[/lpbug]. This bug shows up as a Permission denied on *x*:\wubildr (x: is some drive letter) right at the end of the install, but the install itself is actually successful. You can check the wubi log file in %TEMP% (wubi-11.10-rev245.log) to confirm whether this was the problem.

It has been fixed for 12.04.

Also, the standalone wubi.exe in 11.10 won't install on a FAT32 partition (only NTFS). That's also been fixed for 12.04. Bug [lpbug]882393[/lpbug]

Ah nice... I didn't restart after the error to see if it had actually worked but the error with the random drive letter seems like the problem in my error log.


Cheers
404

### Post by Ani on 2012-04-20
I am trying to install Ubuntu 12.04 using wubi.

I am looking at [http://people.canonical.com/~evand/wubi/precise/](http://people.canonical.com/~evand/wubi/precise/)

and I can see wubi-r265.exe and wubi-r265-signed.exe

what is the difference between the two and which one should I use?

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-04-20
> **Ani said:**
> I am trying to install Ubuntu 12.04 using wubi.

I am looking at [http://people.canonical.com/~evand/wubi/precise/]("http://people.canonical.com/%7Eevand/wubi/precise/")

and I can see wubi-r265.exe and wubi-r265-signed.exe

what is the difference between the two and which one should I use?
Use signed (or stable which is always the latest). The difference between the two is that it's signed by a certificate registered to Canonical (verifies the source of the exe).

But both are the same otherwise.

---

### Post by bcbc on 2012-04-21
> **rima said:**
> Could you get viruses in Wubi? I don't mean viruses in Linux, but in Windows, when using Linux. And I'm talking specifically drive-by viruses

In theory, yes. In practice, it seems a low probability for virus makers to target Windows via a linux install, even lower with Wubi.

### Post by bcbc on 2012-04-22
> **henzerani said:**
> Hi.  I've very new to this and I'm having a little trouble getting started.  I have downloaded Wubi via the Snapfiles website.  I began the install which ran to the point where it asked for the computer to be rebooted.  I rebooted and it opened in windows as normal.  I've rebooted a couple more times and the reinstalled Wubi.  But the computer always starts in Windows. I was expecting to have a choice when I restarted. I'd be really grateful if someone could point me in the right direction?

I'm using a ten year old HP pc, with an AMD 1.53Ghz processor and IGB of RAM.  And I'm running XP.

You should get wubi from here: [http://www.ubuntu.com/download/ubuntu/windows-installer](http://www.ubuntu.com/download/ubuntu/windows-installer)

Assuming that the version on snapfiles is equivalent (I have no idea), then you should check whether the OS timeout is set correctly:
Right click on My computer
Click on Properties
Click on the Advanced tab
Click on Startup & Recovery settings
Make sure the "Time to display operating systems" is set to 10.
